The "AI Assistant" Dichotomy: While a vast majority of tech vendors promote the concept of an "AI personal assistant," a small but firm minority of individuals reject this notion outright, exemplified by the comment, "I do not need a personal assistant, as I am capable of synthesizing and acting on information on my own"

Coordinated Product Endorsements: Multiple individuals submitted comments that specifically and enthusiastically endorsed a single AI tool, suggesting a potential organized advocacy or public relations effort.

keyQuotations.quote: [AI] can create an era of healthcare abundance: every senior who needs daily check-ins or coaching can get it, every care gap can be addressed promptly, and no one is left without guidance due to staff shortages or cost.
This is the true promise of AI in healthcare: it can be the great equalizer, ensuring that everyone has access to the same high-quality care.
Accelerating responsible AI adoption is no longer optional; it is essential to sustain access, quality, and equity for Medicare beneficiaries while increasing the return on tax dollars.
Thoughtfully deployed AI can humanize healthcare by integrating patients’ desired outcomes and improving transitions of care.
Unlocking More Value Through Ambient Speech Generative AI (AS-GAI): Strategic Rationale: Reducing cognitive load, improving accuracy, freeing time for patient engagement.
AI tools are ready - the data is not.
AI in healthcare currently exists in a largely unregulated environment, introducing concerns regarding the design, use, and impact of AI.
Often the data in the EHR is incorrect and having an AI enabled tool that is only fed off that incorrect data would make patient care worse.
[Patients] are not anti-technology but have deep and justiﬁed mistrust from years of mishandling health data.
Yet the promise of AI is throttled by a chokepoint: large EHR vendors who were entrenched under the HITECH Act and now act as data and innovation gatekeepers.
Reliance on AI without human clinical oversight erodes professional judgment and may introduce unintended bias.
Without support and meaningful incentive structures from the government, we run the risk of only the largest or wealthiest health care organizations adopting AI tools.
Just because AI, or any other technology, is possible in our age of technology worship, doesn’t mean that it’s automatically a good thing.
Large Language Models (LLMs) offer an opportunity to reallocate epistemic authority in healthcare.
Every AI powered digital health solution must uphold patients’ rights to transparency, fairness, safety, and human oversight.
AI is a tool, not a panacea, and must be used in a governed manner.
With the ongoing emergence of AI tools, the entirety of a patient's EHI will be extracted soon--with, or without, the cooperation of the health IT vendors.

In short, we believe CMS and ONC should embrace a dual-path approach: (1) maintain and evolve USCDI as the backbone for structured data exchange, while (2) simultaneously encouraging safe, transparent use of AI models to enhance data completeness and utility from less structured inputs.
With increasing use of AI or chatbots, it is nearly impossible to speak to an individual and have an issue resolved.

5.themeDescription: Patient and Caregiver Empowerment and Rights

sections.executiveSummary:
Public sentiment overwhelmingly affirms that the current digital health ecosystem is profoundly and unacceptably fragmented, placing an immense burden on the patients and caregivers it is meant to serve. A near-unanimous and urgent call from all corners of the healthcare landscape demands a fundamental shift toward a patient-centric model where individuals have seamless access to and ultimate control over their complete health story.

Key tensions emerge not on the goal of patient empowerment, but on the path to achieving it. Debates rage between a government-led, unified platform versus a competitive, open API-driven app ecosystem; between strict security measures for digital identity versus the need for equitable access; and between a vision of empowerment through unfettered data sharing versus a definition rooted in the absolute right to privacy. Caregivers are universally recognized as the "invisible frontline," yet they remain critically undersupported by current technology and policy.

The dominant recommendations urge CMS to use its regulatory and financial power to mandate functional, complete, and usable data access that extends far beyond current standards. This includes enforcing robust and standardized caregiver proxy access, ensuring all digital tools are co-designed with and accessible to the diverse populations they serve, and championing policies that address the digital divide and build patient trust.

consensusPoints.text: The current health data system is critically fragmented, burdensome, and fails patients.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Virtually every commenter across all stakeholder groups (over 90%)

exceptions.text: While there is universal agreement on the problem, commenters diverge significantly on the solutions, from market-driven private apps to government-led mandates.

consensusPoints.text: Patients must have control and ownership over their own health data.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of stakeholders—from patient advocates and individuals to technology companies and provider groups

exceptions.text: The practical definition of 'control' is a major area of debate. For some, it means the ability to easily share data via APIs; for others, it means an absolute right to privacy and the ability to block data sharing.

consensusPoints.text: Caregivers are essential and must be formally supported by digital health systems.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A broad coalition of commenters, including caregiver advocacy groups, individual caregivers, providers, and researchers

exceptions.text: While support for caregivers is universal, specific recommendations vary from simple proxy access in portals to comprehensive, role-based permissions and reimbursement for caregiver-enabling technologies.

consensusPoints.text: Digital tools must be usable, accessible, and designed for diverse populations.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Path to a Unified Patient Experience
areasOfDebate.description: This debate centers on the fundamental architecture for patient data access. One side advocates for a centralized, government-led portal for simplicity and equity, while the other champions a decentralized, market-driven ecosystem of apps powered by open APIs to foster innovation. A related debate concerns the method of access, weighing the security of a federated digital identity against the potential for it to create friction and exclude vulnerable populations.

positions.label: Unified Portal
positions.stance: CMS should lead the development of a single, unified "Health Hub" or portal where beneficiaries can access all their aggregated clinical and claims data in one place. This provides a simple, one-stop solution that reduces the cognitive burden of "portalitis" for all beneficiaries, especially those with lower digital literacy.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by many individuals, caregivers, and some consultants
keyArguments: Provides a simple, one-stop solution that reduces cognitive burden.
Benefits all beneficiaries, especially those with lower digital literacy.

positions.label: Open API Ecosystem
positions.stance: CMS should focus on mandating robust, standardized APIs (e.g., FHIR) to allow a competitive marketplace of third-party applications to aggregate and present data. This fosters innovation, competition, and choice, allowing for specialized apps tailored to specific needs.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by most tech vendors, health IT experts, and standards bodies
keyArguments: Fosters innovation, competition, and choice in a marketplace of third-party apps.
Allows for specialized applications tailored to specific patient needs.

positions.label: Federated Credential is Essential
positions.stance: A secure, reusable digital identity (e.g., integrating with Login.gov) is the most effective solution to the "multi-portal credential problem," simplifying access, enhancing security, and enabling a seamless patient experience.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by a strong majority of tech vendors and interoperability orgs
keyArguments: Solves the "multi-portal credential problem" by providing a single, reusable identity.
Enhances security and simplifies access for a seamless experience.

positions.label: Digital ID Adds Friction
positions.stance: Mandating a digital identity adds another burdensome step, raises serious privacy and equity issues, and could disenfranchise vulnerable populations without government IDs or digital literacy. Patients prefer simpler, built-in methods like FaceID/TouchID and participation must be strictly opt-in.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by some tech vendors, patient advocates, and privacy-focused groups
keyArguments: Creates a burdensome extra step that raises privacy and equity concerns.
Risks disenfranchising vulnerable populations who lack government IDs or digital literacy.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Nature of Patient Data Control
areasOfDebate.description: This debate explores the core definition of patient empowerment. Is it achieved primarily through the ability to easily share data with third-party apps to foster innovation and utility, or is it fundamentally rooted in the right to privacy and the ability to prevent data from being shared without explicit consent?

positions.label: Empowerment via Sharing
positions.stance: Patients are empowered by having easy, API-driven access to their data so they can use innovative third-party apps to manage health, share records with new providers, and engage in the digital health economy.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by most tech vendors and interoperability advocates
keyArguments: Enables use of innovative third-party apps for health management.
Facilitates easy sharing of records with new providers.
Allows patients to engage in the digital health economy.

positions.label: Empowerment via Privacy
positions.stance: Patients are empowered by having an absolute right to control their data, including the right to prevent it from being shared without explicit consent, the right to keep it private between themselves and their chosen provider, and the "right to be forgotten."
positions.supportLevel: Supported by a vocal contingent of individuals and health freedom activists
keyArguments: Guarantees the right to prevent data sharing without explicit consent.
Upholds the privacy of the patient-provider relationship.
Includes the "right to be forgotten."

areasOfDebate.topic: Timing of Patient Access to Sensitive Results
areasOfDebate.description: This debate weighs the principle of immediate data access against the clinical and ethical concerns of releasing potentially distressing information without professional context.

positions.label: Immediate Access
positions.stance: Patients have a right to real-time, unfiltered access to all their health information, including test results, as soon as it is available. This empowers them to be proactive partners in their care and aligns with Cures Act principles.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by patient advocates and some tech vendors
keyArguments: Upholds the patient's right to real-time, unfiltered access to their own data.
Empowers patients to be proactive partners in their care.
Aligns with the principles of the Cures Act.

positions.label: Clinician-Mediated Release
positions.stance: The release of sensitive results (e.g., cancer diagnosis) should be thoughtfully timed or mediated by a clinician to prevent undue patient anxiety, confusion, and psychological harm from receiving complex or distressing news without clinical context.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by most provider organizations
keyArguments: Prevents patient anxiety and psychological harm from unmediated bad news.
Ensures patients receive complex results with necessary clinical context.

areasOfDebate.topic: Patient Motivation and Desired Level of Data Access
areasOfDebate.description: This is a debate about the typical patient's desire for data engagement. Are most patients, especially those with complex needs, eager to dive deep into their full medical records, or is the primary desire for their providers to have a consolidated view, with patients using portals for simpler, transactional tasks?

positions.label: Deep Engagement
positions.stance: Patients, especially those with complex conditions, want and need direct access to their complete, unabridged health records to actively manage their care, advocate for themselves, and drive better outcomes.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by most patient advocates and patients with complex conditions
keyArguments: Patients with complex conditions need full record access to manage care.
Direct access enables self-advocacy and drives better outcomes.

positions.label: Pragmatic Utility
positions.stance: Most patients have limited interest in deep chart review; they primarily want their doctors to have consolidated records and use portals for simple, transactional tasks. The more critical goal is a single consolidated record for providers.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by a small but vocal minority, notably a physician with EHR experience
keyArguments: Most patients are not interested in detailed chart review.
The primary desire is for their providers to have consolidated data.
Patient portal use is mainly for simple, transactional tasks.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The overwhelming emotional, cognitive, and administrative burden of navigating a fragmented system ("data delayed is data denied"); the trauma and medical risks of incomplete records; the difficulty of correcting inaccurate information; and the lack of formal recognition and support for caregivers.
specificPoints: They provide visceral, lived experiences of system failure, linking data fragmentation directly to patient harm, diagnostic errors, and extreme stress. A TBI survivor calculated that managing his care took 75% of his cognitive energy. A caregiver discovered her mother's terminal diagnosis by chance due to fragmented records. They see the problem not as a technical glitch, but as a moral failure of a system not designed for people.

A single, unified place to see all records; simple, intuitive tools co-designed with them; standardized, easy-to-use proxy access for caregivers; and a simple, mandated process for correcting their records.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of clear and universally adopted standards for data exchange (FHIR APIs); inconsistent API performance; information blocking by incumbents; barriers to market entry and scaling patient-facing solutions; and the lack of a universal patient identity solution.
specificPoints: They view the fragmented system as a solvable market failure and a business opportunity. They provide the most detailed technical recommendations and are the strongest proponents of solutions like national FHIR endpoint directories, unified digital identity, and API-first architectures. Many frame their specific products as the answer.

Mandate full EHI access via FHIR APIs and TEFCA participation; create a national digital identity system; establish a "CMS Trusted App" marketplace; and focus certification on end-to-end usability, not just technical compliance.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Implementation burden and cost; patient safety risks from immediate release of uninterpreted, sensitive results; patient privacy and data security liability; low digital literacy among patient populations; and receiving large "data dumps" that hinder rather than help care.
specificPoints: They are on the front lines of both patient care and regulatory compliance. While supporting patient empowerment in principle, they are uniquely focused on the practical challenges and risks. They warn that disorganized data can "adversely affect patient care" and raise concerns about interpreting data without clinical context.

CMS-led patient and provider education campaigns; allow for reasonable delays or clinical mediation for sensitive results; fund digital literacy programs; ensure new tools integrate smoothly into clinical workflows; and prioritize usability for both patients and clinicians.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Low patient uptake of existing tools like the Patient Access API; ensuring technology investments provide real value and improve outcomes; navigating the transition to value-based care.
specificPoints: They bring a market-based view, noting that simply making an API available doesn't guarantee use. They suggest patients may prefer existing provider-mediated channels and that CMS needs to better demonstrate the value proposition of new tools.
Improve public education on the benefits of patient data access; support complementary access models (not just patient-directed apps); empower consumer choice to drive value.

noteworthyInsights.insight: A member of a Patient & Family Advisory Committee describes the trauma her children experience when she has to "revisit the past over and over" at each new provider appointment, which has triggered behavioral episodes. This reframes interoperability not as an efficiency issue, but as a critical component of compassionate, trauma-informed care.

A patient with Parkinson's Disease recounts how his wife, after numerous failed attempts to get records through official channels, had to "hack" multiple MyChart accounts to consolidate his data, without which he "almost lost his disability benefit." This story powerfully illustrates how the system’s failures have direct, severe, real-world consequences.

Kelee Petzelt, a patient and advocate, provides a devastating example of systemic failure: "Ask the daughter who buried her 48-year-old father a week before her wedding—because insurance delayed a PGx test that could’ve flagged a chemo interaction and bought him time." This crystallizes how data silos and administrative hurdles have life-or-death consequences.

Salesforce cites their research showing 70% of U.S. adults feel their needs aren't met by the current healthcare landscape and 65% find care coordination confusing, quantifying the widespread dissatisfaction.

The American Dental Association cites survey data showing that "7 out of 10 adults want their patient portal to contain both medical and dental records," providing strong consumer-driven evidence for breaking down the historical silo between medical and dental health information.

KuduCare introduces the concept of the patient-caregiver "dyad" as the "true unit of care for millions of Medicare households." This insight argues that policy and technology must be designed to support this relational unit, not just the individual patient in isolation.

A patient and TBI survivor calculates that managing their healthcare consumed "more than 75% of my 'cognitively available time' in my first year after my 2nd TBI." This quantifies the profound, often invisible, burden the system places on the sick.

Patient advocate Grace Cordovano points out that patients are labeled "non-compliant, non-adherent, poor historians, hysterical, difficult" when in reality, they are "exhausted by systems that constantly ask them to adapt...without ever showing that it matters." This reframes patient behavior as a rational response to a dysfunctional system.

Gary L Dickinson powerfully argues that "‘Patient-centric’ and ‘patient empowerment’ are contending buzzwords but are entirely peripheral considerations to many of the most highly-promoted HIT infrastructure ‘solutions’,” urging a fundamental reorientation of system design around the individual, not institutions.

A physician and EHR founder presents a contrarian view, arguing that patients "don’t really care about what’s in their charts." He contends the real patient desire is for their doctors to have seamless data access, reframing the problem from one of patient empowerment to one of provider enablement.

NOSSCR powerfully frames the practice of charging fees to caregivers and social workers for patient records as a "disability tax." This reframing highlights the inequity of the practice, where the most vulnerable individuals are financially penalized for seeking the assistance they need to access benefits.

A health tech company captures the chaos of the current system by stating patients have "become the general contractors of our own bodies but have none of the tools for the job." This metaphor perfectly encapsulates the theme of shifting an institutional burden onto individuals without providing the means to manage it.

mPulse points out that certification criteria heavily weighted toward APIs may "undervalue end-to-end user experience," shifting developer resources toward compliance rather than innovation in usability. This suggests a potential conflict between achieving technical interoperability and creating genuinely user-friendly products.

The American College of Physicians provides a crucial counterpoint to the "more data is always better" argument, warning that for clinicians, "receiving large quantities of disorganized, often duplicative, data without appropriate context can hinder" the ability to find actionable information and can "adversely affecting patient care."

emergingPatterns.pattern: There is a clear and powerful pattern where a commenter's role dictates their focus and urgency. Patients and caregivers with direct, personal experience of complex or chronic illness speak with a visceral passion about the human toll of the system's failures. Technology vendors focus on API standards and market barriers. Providers focus on workflow, liability, and clinical safety. A successful policy must bridge these different realities.

A critical pattern is the tension between empowering patients with tools and simply shifting the system's integration burden onto them. While many tech vendors promote apps for patient-led data aggregation, patient advocates and caregivers describe this as an exhausting, unsustainable responsibility that the system should solve itself.

A clear pattern emerges where technology vendors and interoperability groups focus on the technical how (APIs, FHIR standards, identity federation), while patients, caregivers, and clinicians focus on the experiential why (simplicity, trust, communication, context, safety).

The conversation is maturing beyond just giving patients access to data. A clear pattern emerges demanding that data be usable—intelligible (plain language), contextualized ("why" not just "what"), actionable, and presented in tools that are accessible to everyone, regardless of technical skill.

Multiple commenters identify negative knock-on effects. The 21st Century Cures Act has exacerbated "portalitis" without providing true interoperability. The immediate release of complex test results can cause significant psychological harm. A push for digital-only solutions risks deepening the digital divide. Mandating digital identity could erode the privacy and trust it's meant to build.

A subtle but persistent pattern is a deep-seated distrust in the system's incentives. Patients and advocates feel the system is designed for institutional profit and administrative convenience, not their well-being. This distrust fuels demands for absolute patient control and ownership over data.

keyQuotations.quote: You cannot code for care if you’ve never felt what it’s like to beg for it.
[The experience was a] health information breakdown at every handoff... This was not a niche technical problem; it was a fundamental barrier to his care and my ability to advocate for him.
Data delayed is data denied.

People aren’t disinterested in digital health. They are exhausted by systems that constantly ask them to adapt, input, troubleshoot, and give feedback, without ever showing that it matters, while labelling us “non-compliant, non-adherent, poor historians, hysterical, difficult, senile, drug-seeking, and frequent-flyers”.
We’ve become the general contractors of our own bodies but have none of the tools for the job.
Patients are the only consistent node across fragmented systems.
Without caregiver inclusion, we’re designing a system for theoretical patients—not real families.
The elephant in the room is that we’ve spent decades designing systems around the patient instead of with the patient.
[The system is] a patient whose condition is manageable, a family member holding it all together, and a provider boxed in by time, billing rules, and legal ambiguity.
How many patient portals must a caregiver attempt to gain access to? ... Lack of convenient, comprehensive healthcare information puts added stress on caregivers and puts patients at risk.
In my own experience... more than 75% of my 'cognitively available time' was spent pursuing my healthcare recovery.

While patient care may be the business of health care, addressing patient needs, promoting patient health and supporting patient autonomy and agency are not a priority within the 'system' as it operates today.

It’s very frustrating to continue to have to repeat the same information. Not only that, it’s triggering... Coming out of these appointments in the past, the boys have had episodes specifically because we have had to revisit the past over and over and not just focus on healing.”
HHS should affirm patients—not commercial entities—own their health data and can direct its use across platforms.

Patients, but for some small, educated fraction, don’t really care about what’s in their charts. But, they are continuously frustrated that every time they go to the doctor, they are repeating the same information...
Because of a loophole in the existing laws, these helpers are often not afforded the “reasonable fee” protections... In effect, this is a “disability tax.”

AMGA members have reported troubling cases where patients were notified of distressing test results before any communication with a provider could occur. These incidents have led to emotional harm and patient dissatisfaction.

Most patient-facing apps that are not affiliated with EHR vendors fall outside the protections of HIPAA and other applicable privacy laws. Notably, many patients do not realize that HIPAA does not apply to these tools.
The problem will not be solved until the time that the one entity who cares most to bring interoperability to the medical record is allowed to control it: the patient.

Children are not simply 'little adults'—their clinical needs, consent and privacy considerations, family caregiving structures, and developmentally appropriate care models all require tailored health information technology (HIT) solutions.
If a tool is hard to understand, confusing to navigate, or filled with jargon, people won’t use it, no matter how powerful it is.
The current digital health experience is fragmented and cruel, forcing patients to chase data, repeatedly explain their story, and fight for record access.
Healthcare should follow the patient, not the other way around.
It’s time to move beyond APIs that let us read records, and toward protocols that let us change lives.
Patients should not have to trade privacy for access.

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on privacy, security, and trust is defined by a deep and foundational mistrust, creating a powerful counter-narrative to data sharing initiatives. The core tension is a fundamental paradox: while nearly all stakeholders agree that a trustworthy digital ecosystem is essential, they are profoundly divided on how to achieve it. Technology vendors and many professional organizations see standardized digital identity, AI, and robust data exchange as the path to enhanced security and trust. Conversely, a large and vocal cohort of individuals and advocacy groups views these same technologies as an existential threat to privacy, liberty, and autonomy. The catastrophic Change Healthcare cyberattack was repeatedly cited as a watershed moment, exposing the system's fragility and magnifying fears. This has led to a dominant and widespread consensus that HIPAA is no longer sufficient, fueling a powerful call for a new, comprehensive federal privacy law to close the critical regulatory gap where consumer apps can misuse and monetize health data outside of its protections.

consensusPoints.text: Privacy and security are not optional features but are the absolute prerequisites for any functioning digital health ecosystem. This is consistently framed as the bedrock issue upon which all other data initiatives, and patient confidence itself, will either succeed or fail. For instance, beHuman Corp. stated, "Protecting health information, establishing trust, and respecting individual liberties are foundational issues." The Sequoia Project emphasized that "Trust in the system depends on patients feeling they have control over their sensitive information." The American Psychiatric Association noted, "Privacy and security must remain paramount for patients with mental health and substance use disorders, otherwise, trust in care risks being eroded." Finally, The Light Collective observed that "Patients are not anti-technology but have deep and justified mistrust from years of mishandling health data."

Stakeholders highlight the escalating threat of cyberattacks like ransomware. The Change Healthcare incident was frequently cited as a catastrophic example of the system's fragility, demonstrating how a single breach can paralyze critical national health infrastructure. AMGA commented that "Health systems and providers face rising threats from sophisticated cyberattacks that pose serious risks to both patient privacy and jeopardize critical clinical operations." Kelee Petzelt framed the Change Healthcare breach as exposing "the fragility of the infrastructure, paralyzing pharmacies, patients, and providers; it was a power imbalance, not just a cybersecurity issue." Point32Health added that "Ransomware attacks are a significant and increasing issue in healthcare, causing substantial financial losses and operational disruptions."

A broad coalition of knowledgeable stakeholders agrees that HIPAA, while essential, is insufficient for the modern digital age. They express grave concern that its protections do not extend to third-party apps and other non-covered entities. CHIME described this as creating a "separate and parallel but unequal universe which is at the heart of the privacy debate in healthcare." This is seen as a critical failure point that allows patient data to be monetized and misused, eroding trust. AHIP noted, "The risk of privacy breaches grows exponentially as more data elements are shared with non-HIPAA-governed third-party apps." Similarly, the American College of Physicians pointed out reports of "app developers selling patient data to third parties, failing to share their privacy policies with patients, or not following the policies they have published."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A broad coalition of knowledgeable stakeholders

consensusPoints.text: Commenters from individual activists to large trade associations assert that patient consent must be the cornerstone of data sharing. While the mechanics, scope, and implementation are a major source of debate, the core principle of informed, meaningful consent is widely held as non-negotiable for building trust. For example, one commenter stated, "All health information sharing should be based on explicit, direct patient consent." Connecting for Better Health recognized that "securing patient consent ensures individuals retain ultimate control over their personal health information." Patients Rising also argued that "Patient participation in digital health tools must be rooted in opt-in consent, not mandatory biometric capture or digital identity requirements."

areasOfDebate.topic: Mandatory Digital Identity
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether a standardized, universal digital identity is a necessary security measure for a modern healthcare system or an unacceptable threat to individual privacy, autonomy, and civil liberties.

positions.label: A Grave Threat to Liberty
positions.stance: Mandate or strongly encourage a universal digital identity is an unacceptable infringement on privacy, autonomy, and civil liberties.
positions.supportLevel: A large and passionate contingent of individuals, health freedom activists, and some physicians.
keyArguments: Constitutes a profound infringement on privacy, bodily autonomy, and individual sovereignty.
Creates a system of surveillance and control, making healthcare access conditional on compliance with a 'privacy wrecking system.'
Exacerbates the digital divide, excluding vulnerable populations (e.g., homeless, elderly) who lack technology access, trust, or required documentation.

positions.label: An Essential Enabler of Security
positions.stance: A universal, standardized digital identity is a foundational requirement for a modern, secure healthcare system.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of technology companies, many business groups, government contractors, and some provider/payer associations.
keyArguments: Enhances security by moving beyond weak passwords to multi-factor, biometrically-verified credentials (NIST IAL2/AAL2) and enabling Zero Trust architecture.
Reduces fraud and improves patient safety by ensuring the right person is accessing the right records.
Streamlines patient access by eliminating the 'multi-portal credential problem' with a single, trusted login.

areasOfDebate.topic: Patient Consent and Data Control
areasOfDebate.description: This debate contrasts the demand for absolute, granular patient control over data via explicit opt-in with the need for a more flexible, balanced framework that enables care coordination, research, and public health.

positions.label: Explicit Opt-In and Absolute Control
positions.stance: Patients must have absolute, granular control. Information should only be shared with explicit, affirmative opt-in consent for each specific purpose, with the right to revoke access and have data deleted.
positions.supportLevel: Most individuals, patient advocates, and some provider groups.
keyArguments: Health information is private property existing solely between the patient and provider; 'blocking of patient information protects them';
and any data exchange must be patient-directed with clear, real-time audit trails.

positions.label: A Balanced, Workable Framework
positions.stance: While patient consent is crucial, a restrictive, universal opt-in model creates friction that harms care coordination, value-based care, public health, and research. A framework balancing individual privacy with operational and societal needs is required.
positions.supportLevel: Most health plans, some provider organizations, HIEs, and technology vendors.
keyArguments: An overly restrictive model paradoxically increases privacy risks by forcing manual processes (e.g., faxing);
opt-outs hinder the ability of ACOs to manage population health;
and progress requires a balance between control and the need for data to flow for treatment and payment.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Role of Government
areasOfDebate.description: This debate focuses on whether the federal government should act as a central, trusted authority for setting standards and managing identity, or if its role should be minimized due to fears of surveillance, incompetence, and risk.

positions.label: Trusted Arbiter and Standard-Setter
positions.stance: Position CMS and federal agencies as central authorities for certifying apps, setting security standards, and managing identity frameworks to build trust.
positions.supportLevel: Many technology companies, consulting firms, and provider associations seeking clarity and standardization.
keyArguments: Federal endorsement (e.g., a 'Trusted App' registry) is necessary to build patient and provider confidence;
only a central authority can enforce harmonized rules;
and a government-backed identity solution (like Login.gov) can provide a trusted, no-cost option.

positions.label: Inherent Distrust and Source of Risk
positions.stance: Minimize the federal government's role as a data aggregator or controller due to fears of surveillance and incompetence.
positions.supportLevel: Many individuals, health freedom activists, and some technology companies.
keyArguments: Government systems are prime targets for hacking and are not inherently trustworthy;
centralizing data in government hands creates a risk of it being 'weaponized' against citizens;
and the market, not mandates, should drive innovation.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Loss of fundamental human and constitutional rights; mandatory technologies (digital IDs, biometrics); government and corporate surveillance; and the monetization of their data without consent.
specificPoints: Frames privacy not as a technical or compliance issue, but as a fundamental human and civil right. They express deep, often emotional, distrust of both government and corporate entities and provide powerful examples of how data sharing leads to direct harm and stigma.

Uphold informed, explicit opt-in consent as the inviolable standard; prohibit mandatory digital IDs or biometrics for societal access; pass strong new federal privacy laws; and provide patients with complete audit trails.
An individual who states, "Ultimately, protecting health data privacy is not just a technical issue—it’s a civil/human rights issue".
A patient advocate who says the answer isn't more apps, but building trust by listening.
A health freedom activist who opposes "all medical mandates to access society".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The immense operational and financial burden of cybersecurity; legal liability for data breaches; erosion of the physician-patient relationship due to lack of trust; and navigating the confusing "patchwork of privacy rules."
specificPoints: They are caught in the middle: legally liable for data breaches, financially burdened by new mandates, and operationally strained by vetting third-party apps, all while needing data to flow for care improvement. Several voice the cynical view that "privacy" is often used as a "smokescreen for interoperability" by vendors.

Stronger federal privacy laws that extend beyond HIPAA; federal leadership to harmonize cybersecurity standards; "safe harbor" provisions for organizations that meet standards; and funding to help smaller practices invest in security.
ACOG stresses that for their patients, confidentiality is essential to prevent violence or discrimination.
AAFP highlights that small practices lack the expertise to manage cybersecurity.
CHIME describes the "separate and parallel but unequal universe" of privacy regulation.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of standardized protocols (for identity, security); fragmented and conflicting state vs. federal regulations; liability uncertainty; and provider/patient reluctance to adopt new technologies.
specificPoints: Unwavering belief that technology is the solution to the trust deficit. They speak a language of standards, frameworks, and architecture (NIST IAL2, OpenID Connect, Zero Trust). They see a stable, predictable regulatory environment as essential for stimulating investment and innovation.

Mandate specific technical standards (e.g., OpenID Connect); create a national digital identity framework; incentivize secure cloud adoption; and develop clear, enforceable "rules of the road" for data sharing.
DirectTrust provides a deeply technical roadmap for a national digital identity.
Red Hat describes its alignment with post-quantum cryptography.
CareCard warns against a "monopolized digital gatekeeper" for identity.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Health Plans & Payer Associations (e.g., AHIP, BCBSA)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The systemic risk posed by non-HIPAA-covered apps; data misuse by bad actors on exchange networks like TEFCA; inconsistent state and federal privacy laws; and the high cost of implementing new technologies.
specificPoints: As major data holders and aggregators, they are highly attuned to systemic risks and compliance complexities. They support progress but advocate for a very cautious, measured approach that harmonizes rules before mandating action and provides "safe harbor" for compliance.

Harmonize privacy frameworks before expanding data exchange; ensure strong governance and enforcement for TEFCA; establish a federal cybersecurity "gold standard"; and provide clear guidance and incentives to address implementation costs.
BCBSA warns of "unregulated querying across the [TEFCA] network" and "insufficient safeguards against bad actors".
Point32Health calls for a consolidated federal cybersecurity strategy, citing billions in losses from breaches.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The American Board of Family Medicine makes the sharp observation that privacy concerns are often cynically weaponized by large health systems and EHR vendors as a justification for information blocking to protect market share.

The American Urological Association makes the sharp observation that privacy concerns are often cynically weaponized by large health systems and EHR vendors as a justification for information blocking to protect market share.

JSL Health Capital raises a crucial, counterintuitive risk: "Aggressive data sharing requirements without adequate safeguards may paradoxically lead to underdocumentation, as providers become reluctant to create records that might be inappropriately shared or misinterpreted."

Open City Labs provides a highly specific and technical breakdown of why AI agents pose unique security risks, including their need for persistent credentials (making them attractive targets) and their potential to systematically collect data far beyond their intended scope.

A patient's brief anecdote that his wife had to "'hack' multiple MyChart accounts to retrieve medical records" vividly illustrates how the failure of legitimate access channels forces users into insecure and desperate workarounds, highlighting the system's brokenness from a user perspective.

Mental Health America provides a harrowing example of a man who went to the ER for stomach pain; once his schizophrenia diagnosis was revealed, "a security officer was called, and he was treated as a danger, compromising his care." This illustrates the tangible, discriminatory harm that can result from data sharing without context or training.

Imagine Pediatrics points out that "Evolving privacy concerns post-Dobbs have reignited debates about opt-in versus opt-out consent models," showing how broader socio-political events are directly impacting health data policy debates and potentially restricting data availability.

CHIME powerfully frames the current regulatory landscape as a "separate and parallel but unequal universe" for entities covered by HIPAA versus those who are not, arguing this is "at the heart of the privacy debate."

Point32Health provided specific figures on the financial toll of cyberattacks, noting that UnitedHealth Group reported "$2.9 billion in losses from the Change Healthcare attack" and that breaches in 2023 cost the industry "$7.93 billion."

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Trust Paradox / Experience-Expertise Gap: A fundamental disconnect exists where technology companies and federal agencies propose more technology (AI, digital identity) to build trust and security, while a significant portion of the public, particularly those with lived experience, perceives this very technology as the primary source of their mistrust and insecurity.

The Post-HIPAA Consensus: There is a clear, emerging pattern among sophisticated stakeholders that HIPAA, while important, is no longer sufficient for a modern digital ecosystem filled with non-covered entities. The conversation is shifting from how to comply with HIPAA to what new federal law must be created to cover the gaps HIPAA leaves.

The Provider Squeeze: Physician groups and hospitals are consistently positioned as being caught in the middle. They are legally liable for data breaches, financially burdened by new technology mandates, operationally strained by vetting third-party apps, and educationally responsible for guiding worried patients, all while their own trust in the system is low.

From Compliance to National Security: Several commenters, particularly government contractors, are escalating the framing of cybersecurity from a HIPAA compliance issue to a matter of national security and critical infrastructure protection, arguing that public trust and national security are interdependent.

Unintended Consequences of Data Sharing: Multiple commenters highlighted that greater data access is not a panacea. It can lead to harm through stigma (Mental Health America), inappropriate denial of care (Emory Healthcare), under-documentation by fearful providers (JSL Health Capital), and increased provider liability (Cleveland Clinic, AMGA).

Differentiated Data Sensitivity: Multiple commenters highlight that a "one-size-fits-all" approach to privacy is unworkable. They consistently single out behavioral health data (governed by 42 CFR Part 2), reproductive health data, and prescription drug monitoring data as requiring distinct, more stringent safeguards.

A Two-Tiered Trust Model: A clear pattern emerged where established, non-profit, or community-based entities (like HIEs) position themselves as neutral "trust brokers," in direct contrast to for-profit data networks, which are viewed with suspicion for monetizing data and eroding trust.

keyQuotations.quote: [The] regulatory oversight framework governing those covered by HIPAA and those who are not has created a separate and parallel but unequal universe which is at the heart of the privacy debate in healthcare. Most patients-turned-consumers are completely unaware of how an app or a website intends to use their data, and many are under the false assumption that their data will continue to be safeguarded under HIPAA.
These are not radical positions—they are foundational principles of privacy, autonomy, and consent.
The Change Healthcare breach exposed the fragility of the infrastructure, paralyzing pharmacies, patients, and providers; it was a power imbalance, not just a cybersecurity issue.
Digital identity is the gateway to patient-centered interoperability.
I am very reluctant to use a digital health product provided by my payor because I do not trust or have transparency into how these applications are being monetized.

A foundational prerequisite for a streamlined and patient-centric digital health ecosystem is passage of a comprehensive federal law to protect consumer personal information that falls outside of HIPAA.
If people believe that sharing information will lead to harmful interactions with law enforcement, they will be less likely to seek help.
Privacy implications are real but are increasingly a smokescreen for interoperability.
When CMS asks how to encourage patient and caregiver interest in digital health tools, the answer isn’t in more apps, but rather it’s in how we show up, how we listen, and how we build trust.

Multi-factor Authentication verifies devices, not people. This is a known problem which MFA wasn’t built to address. It’s technology like CLEAR’s that gives us the automated ability to verify the human which closes a big security gap.
Patients should not have to trade privacy for access.
Ultimately, protecting health data privacy is not just a technical issue—it’s a civil/human rights issue.

Children are not simply 'little adults'—their clinical needs, consent and privacy considerations, family caregiving structures, and developmentally appropriate care models all require tailored health information technology (HIT) solutions.
We strongly discourage biometrics-based systems and centralized storage of private keys by federal entities.
The core challenge isn’t technical—it’s a trust problem.
Ob-gyns must maintain the confidentiality of documentation that, if shared without measure, could endanger women or make them more vulnerable to violence or discrimination.
Putting data in the names of the Federal Government only allows the data to be weaponized – like IRS Data.
I do not trust these types of privacy wrecking systems you are considering, so please oppose them!

Data safety poses another obstacle... There have been reports of app developers selling patient data to third parties, failing to share their privacy policies with patients, or not following the policies they have published.
There is no need for digital monitoring or surveillance nor digital devices. That is about control-against the Constitution and not to purpose of the US government.

sections.executiveSummary:
Public sentiment on provider and administrative burden is one of overwhelming and universal crisis. Clinicians, administrators, professional organizations, and even patients describe the current system as unsustainable, fueling a "silent public health crisis" of burnout that threatens the stability of the entire healthcare workforce. A stark dichotomy emerges between frontline providers, who describe a daily reality of fragmented workflows, unmanageable documentation, and severe financial strain, and technology vendors, who universally position their products as the solution.

The root causes are clear and consistently cited: a fragmented digital infrastructure of siloed EHRs and payer portals, a crushing volume of duplicative and misaligned regulatory requirements, and the high cost of technology that is often poorly designed and disruptive to clinical care. The HITECH Act and Meaningful Use program are frequently invoked as a cautionary tale of well-intentioned policy that inadvertently exacerbated the problem, creating a deep-seated skepticism toward new top-down mandates.

A critical finding is that this burden falls disproportionately on small, rural, specialty, and post-acute care providers, creating a dangerous digital divide that widens with each new unfunded requirement. The dominant recommendations call for a fundamental shift in strategy: CMS must move from imposing fragmented mandates to enabling true interoperability by harmonizing reporting requirements, automating manual processes through enforceable standards, providing direct financial and technical support to underserved providers, and shifting the burden of compliance and data processing from the front lines of care to technology vendors and centralized entities.

consensusPoints.text: Administrative Burden and Clinician Burnout Are at a Crisis Level, Threatening the Healthcare Workforce. Nearly every commenter describes the current administrative load as excessive, unsustainable, and a primary driver of clinician burnout and workforce shortages. This view is shared by physicians, nurses, administrators, professional associations, and technology vendors alike. UnitedHealth Group quantifies the impact: "Physician burnout is costing the US health care system an estimated $4.6 billion annually". The Smart Health Network Coalition and No Delays, No Surprises Coalition cite data that "Providers spend 15.5 hours per week on paperwork instead of patient care" and "40% of physicians are considering leaving practice due to burnout". The Texas Medical Association highlights the pervasiveness of work outside of work, known as "pajama time", while Citizens' Council for Health Freedom quotes sources stating, "For some physicians, 90% of their eight-hour shifts are spent on EHRs".

exceptions.text: While consensus on the problem is universal, proposed solutions diverge significantly, from deregulation to more robust, federally-led technology infrastructure.

consensusPoints.text: Fragmented Systems and Lack of Interoperability Are a Root Cause of Burden. A strong majority of stakeholders explicitly identify data fragmentation, lack of interoperability, proprietary "walled garden" systems, and disconnected workflows as the technical foundation for administrative waste and clinical inefficiency. The No Delays, No Surprises Coalition attributes the problem to "fragmented digital infrastructure" where each new mandate layers onto existing requirements without fixing the core issue. The American Association of Nurse Practitioners notes that "Providers face complexity from their own EHR, payer platforms, and others, leading to burnout and confusion". ChenMed describes the reality of manually aggregating data from "HIEs, EHRs, claims, faxes," leading to inefficiency and missed information. The American Urological Association describes how "inefficiencies from additional phone calls, forms, and faxes are frequently reported when trying to obtain information that should be immediately available via EHR".

exceptions.text: No commenters defended the current state of fragmentation.

consensusPoints.text: Small, Rural, and Post-Acute Care (LTPAC) Providers Are Disproportionately Harmed. A broad consensus exists among provider groups, associations, and even tech vendors that financial and technical burdens fall heaviest on smaller practices, rural health clinics, LTPAC facilities, and safety-net providers who lack the capital, IT staff, and resources of large health systems. The National Alliance for Care at Home states a key reason for slow adoption in LTPAC settings is their exclusion from HITECH Act investments. The North Carolina HIEA notes that small and rural providers operate on narrow margins, stressing, "The future of the health technology ecosystem must be one where no provider is excluded because of their size, location, or inability to pay". SOMOS Community Care highlights that vendors impose high fees for APIs, which "forced [APMs] to create redundant workflows or pay unsustainable costs; smaller/safety-net providers disproportionately affected".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A broad consensus exists among provider groups, associations, and even tech vendors

exceptions.text: No commenters argued against this point.

consensusPoints.text: Duplicative and Misaligned Requirements Create Significant Non-Value-Added Work. A strong consensus exists among providers, health systems, and professional associations that inconsistent and overlapping quality reporting, public health reporting, and administrative requirements across federal, state, and commercial payers are a major source of administrative waste. Ascension notes that "overlapping quality programs, delayed feedback cycles, and fragmented requirements... [create] a punitive rather than instructive environment". The California Medical Association points to "Inconsistent rules and requirements for quality measurement reporting, clinical documentation, and billing tasks across various payers and programs". The Blue Cross Blue Shield Association states that "payer-specific prior authorization systems... lead to inconsistent data formats, duplicative documentation, and manual work".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong consensus exists among providers, health systems, and professional associations

areasOfDebate.topic: The Role of Technology in Alleviating Burden
areasOfDebate.description: The Role of Technology in Alleviating Burden

positions.label: Technology is the Solution
positions.stance: Technology, particularly AI, modern APIs, and automation, is the primary way to reduce provider burden. Representative voices include beHuman Corp., Cohere Health, and Bayesian Health.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of technology vendors, many consultants, and some health systems.
keyArguments: AI can automate documentation, prior authorization, and reporting
standardized FHIR APIs reduce integration costs and friction
digital platforms can unify fragmented workflows and eliminate manual tasks

positions.label: Technology is a Source of Burden
positions.stance: Current technology, especially EHRs, is a major source of burden, and new, poorly integrated tools will only make it worse. Representative voices include the American Association of Nurse Practitioners, American Psychiatric Association, and The American Board of Family Medicine.
positions.supportLevel: A significant number of provider associations, individual clinicians, and health systems.
keyArguments: Clunky interfaces, workflow disruptions ("click-box medicine"), alert fatigue, and data overload from new tools exacerbate burnout
technology often prioritizes billing and compliance over clinical usability

areasOfDebate.topic: Best Path to Interoperability and Burden Reduction
areasOfDebate.description: Best Path to Interoperability and Burden Reduction

positions.label: Top-Down Mandates
positions.stance: CMS must take a strong lead by mandating specific standards (FHIR, USCDI+), automating reporting, and requiring participation in national frameworks to force the market toward interoperability. Representative voices include Precise Software Solutions, The Sequoia Project, and beHuman Corp.
positions.supportLevel: Many health IT vendors, systems integrators, and some professional associations.
keyArguments: Voluntary participation and market forces have failed to solve fragmentation.
Only federal mandates provide the certainty needed for private sector ROI and create a level playing field.

positions.label: Incentives and Reduced Mandates
positions.stance: Top-down mandates without flexibility and financial support will crush smaller practices. CMS should reduce prescriptive regulations, incentivize adoption, and allow for modular, function-specific solutions. Representative voices include MGMA, NAACOS, Houston Methodist, and athenahealth.
positions.supportLevel: Most provider groups, ACO associations, and those representing small, rural, or safety-net facilities.
keyArguments: One-size-fits-all requirements disproportionately harm resource-constrained organizations.
Current requirements (e.g., MIPS PI for ACOs) are "check-the-box" exercises that add burden without value.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Nature of Data Exchange
areasOfDebate.description: The Nature of Data Exchange

positions.label: Centralized Networks First
positions.stance: Prioritize national networks to reduce the complexity and cost of numerous point-to-point connections. Representative voices include Kno2, LLC and CAQH.
positions.supportLevel: Several tech vendors and HIE-focused organizations.
keyArguments: Reduces duplication of effort
accelerates access to more complete patient information
simplifies security and governance

positions.label: Point-to-Point APIs Vital
positions.stance: Direct, point-to-point API connections offer necessary control, performance, and customization that broad networks cannot. Representative voices include mPulse and DirectTrust.
positions.supportLevel: Some technology vendors with specific workflow integrations.
keyArguments: Direct connections allow for tailored, high-performance integrations for specific use cases
shared networks can be less flexible and slower to adapt

areasOfDebate.topic: Full EHI Access vs. Clinically Relevant Data
areasOfDebate.description: A debate exists between providing complete, unfiltered access to all Electronic Health Information (EHI) versus providing curated, clinically relevant data.

positions.label: Support Full EHI Access
positions.stance: Primarily technology vendors who argue algorithms need full context to be effective, as noted by Crescendo Health.

positions.label: Support Curated Data
positions.stance: Primarily physician groups who argue that exchanging all data will create "data overflow" and cognitive overload for clinicians, as argued by the American Psychiatric Association.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Unmanageable documentation time ("pajama time," "click boxes"); workflow disruptions from poorly integrated technology; the "moral injury" of systems prioritizing data entry over patient care; the time-consuming and variable processes for prior authorization, credentialing, and quality reporting (MIPS).
specificPoints: They provide the most direct, visceral, ground-level descriptions of how administrative tasks translate into burnout. They are deeply skeptical of technology not designed with their input and workflows in mind.

They propose to simplify, align, and automate reporting; shift compliance burdens to vendors; demand physician-centered design principles; provide financial and technical assistance to small practices.
The AAFP notes that physicians "are often so overburdened, they do not have time to seek out" new helpful tools.
A nurse details how FMLA paperwork delays risk patients' jobs.
An occupational therapist describes billing as "unnecessarily complicated" for small providers.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Health Care Industry, Hospitals & Health Systems
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The exorbitant cost of EHR systems, vendor fees for upgrades and APIs, and the lack of ROI for technology that improves quality but doesn't generate revenue. They are burdened by managing compliance with a multitude of fragmented quality programs.
specificPoints: They sit at the nexus of financial pressure, regulatory compliance, and clinical operations. They emphasize the institutional-level financial and operational barriers and the "punitive rather than instructive" nature of current quality reporting.

They propose to subsidize technology acquisition costs; reorient the Promoting Interoperability program away from ever-expanding requirements; centralize quality reporting calculation at CMS via Bulk FHIR; leverage TEFCA for public health reporting.
GNYHA notes that "For many under-resourced hospitals, a significant obstacle to using innovative applications... is the cost of upgrading EHRs".
The National Association of Community Health Centers (NACHC) details prohibitive costs (up to $49,000) for integrating a single tool into an EHR.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of standardized APIs, which increases development costs; information blocking and high fees from EHR vendors; unclear reimbursement pathways for their products; and slow provider adoption due to cost and workflow integration challenges.
specificPoints: They universally position their products (AI, workflow platforms, data analytics) as the solution to provider burden. They are the strongest advocates for technical standardization and API mandates, as this creates a more accessible and competitive market for them.

They propose to mandate open, certified APIs for all administrative and clinical workflows; create CMS-backed certification and directories for digital tools; provide direct reimbursement or financial incentives for providers to adopt their solutions.
Zocdoc points to EHR vendors' "walled garden" approach as a barrier to innovation.
Bayesian Health blames EHR vendors for "6-to-18-month queues for standard integrations".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The "patient administrative burden" of managing fragmented records across multiple portals ("portalitis"); chasing down referrals and test results; filling out duplicative paperwork; and the emotional and financial cost of care delays caused by system inefficiency.
specificPoints: This group powerfully reframes the issue, arguing that the burden is not confined to providers but is offloaded onto patients and their families, who are least equipped to handle it.
They propose to relieve patient administrative burden through seamless data sharing; create systems that are human-centered, not just provider- or payer-centered.
Grace Cordovano calls patient administrative burden a "silent public health crisis".
An anonymous TBI survivor calculates that 75% of their limited cognitive energy was spent on healthcare administration.

noteworthyInsights.insight: "Patient Administrative Burden" is a Public Health Crisis: Grace Cordovano powerfully articulates that the system's administrative friction is not just a provider problem but is offloaded onto patients and caregivers, who are least equipped to handle it. The Arthritis Foundation likens it to a "full-time job".

"Death by Patient Portal" - A Data-Backed Crisis: Dr. Ateev Mehrotra provides a data-driven analysis showing the exponential and uncompensated increase in patient portal messages, crystallizing how a tool designed for engagement has become a major driver of burnout.

The "Other Pocket Problem": Matt Bishop of Open City Labs brilliantly articulates how HHS agencies optimize their own compliance needs without considering the cumulative burden on providers who must report to multiple programs, leading to "compliance costs exceed[ing] program benefits."

The "Compliance Tax" on Hospitals: The Smart Health Network Coalition provides a stunning example of financial burden: "A typical 300-bed hospital spends '$40-60 million yearly just on regulatory compliance,' enough to hire 500 additional nurses." This reframes compliance from a necessary task to a massive opportunity cost.

Tangible Cost of Interoperability: Doctronic Physicians Group provided a specific quote from a QHIN showing a minimum monthly spend "significantly higher than the $10k per month," making the abstract concept of high costs concrete and shocking. Similarly, Accountable for Health cited EHR data extract costs of up to $45,000.

"Portalitis": Froedtert ThedaCare Health coined this memorable term to describe the "overwhelming burden we place on patients and caregivers" to manage multiple portals, perfectly capturing the patient-facing side of fragmentation.

The "Meaningful Use" Cautionary Tale: The Center for Telehealth and eHealth Law (CTeL) and others explicitly warn CMS not to repeat the mistakes of the Meaningful Use program, which successfully drove EHR adoption but at the cost of "workflow disruption and clinician burnout."

The Human Cost of Administrative Friction: A nurse provides a powerful narrative of how delays in FMLA paperwork—due to administrative hurdles—directly lead to patients risking job loss during a health crisis. This connects system inefficiency to devastating personal consequences.

Interoperability is a Human Problem: Cognitive Medical Systems insightfully reframes the issue: "interoperability isn't just a technical issue, but a fundamentally a human problem disguised as a technical challenge."

EHRs as Anti-Competitive Barriers: Multiple commenters describe how EHR vendors act as gatekeepers, imposing "prohibitively high and unjustified fees" for data access, framing the burden as an anti-competitive practice that stifles innovation.

The "Country Doctor Exemption" Fear: A commenter revealed a fear that adopting simple e-prescribing might trigger full HIPAA compliance, imposing massive new administrative burdens and showing the deep-seated fear of regulatory overreach in small practices.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Widening Digital Divide: A clear pattern emerges showing that policies and market forces are creating two classes of providers: large, well-resourced systems that can (grudgingly) absorb the costs of new technology, and a vast group of small, rural, specialty (mental health, PALTC), and safety-net providers who are being left further behind, unable to afford the "never-ending upgrade cycle."

The Provider vs. Vendor Dichotomy: A significant gap in perspective exists between those experiencing the pain and those selling the cure. Providers and their associations describe the problems of burden with visceral, experience-based detail, while technology vendors describe the solutions, almost always centered on their own products and platforms.

Unintended Consequences of Past Policies: The HITECH Act, ONC Certification, and the Meaningful Use program are consistently cited as having had negative downstream effects. HITECH's exclusion of certain provider types created the current digital gap. Certification, while spurring adoption, is blamed for poor usability and "click-box medicine." This history heavily colors the entire conversation.

ACOs and VBC Models at a Breaking Point: Commenters representing ACOs express acute frustration. They believe that despite being the most advanced models for value-based care, they are being saddled with new, duplicative, and burdensome fee-for-service-style reporting requirements (like MIPS PI) that run counter to the goal of empowering accountable care.

The Rise of the Intermediary: A notable pattern is the call to support intermediary entities—like HIEs, data management platforms, and even CMS itself (via centralized calculation)—to absorb the complexity of interoperability and reporting, rather than placing the full burden on every individual provider practice.

From Technical Problem to Financial/ROI Problem: Sophisticated commenters suggest the primary barrier to interoperability is no longer a lack of technical capability but a lack of a clear business case or Return on Investment (ROI) for providers to bear the cost and effort of implementation.

keyQuotations.quote: Administrative churn is not an inevitable cost of doing business—it’s a fixable policy failure.
Patient administrative burden is a silent public health crisis...
[Greater] utilization of health IT has actually increased burden on providers instead of streamlining clinical workflow.
Poor interoperability is a significant source of burden and burnout for clinicians and a growing threat to patient care quality and safety.
The HHS 'Other Pocket Problem': When Compliance Costs Undermine Program Goals

Three decades of well-intentioned regulations have created a compliance-driven system where each new mandate layers onto existing requirements without addressing the root cause—fragmented digital infrastructure.
For providers, technology should reduce friction -not introduce more.
Healthcare isn’t broken because we lack gadgets. It’s broken because we have allowed outdated systems, bureaucracy, and profit margins to overshadow a free-flowing data connection.
Help us focus on care, not compliance.
In my own experience... more than 75% of my 'cognitively available time' was spent pursuing my healthcare recovery.
That friction has a name, and it’s called 'portalitis'.
ACOs frequently have to work directly with EHR vendors to build data extracts, which can cost anywhere from $5,000 to $45,000.
Physicians feel that the administrative burden of fee-for-service billing for individual messages outweighs the low reimbursement per message.
When providers are focused on screens instead of patients, the risk of error rises, leading to missed information, medication mistakes, and unnecessary or repeated tests.
We were exhausted and frustrated, and I almost lost my disability benefit.
Hospital systems are on the brink of economic collapse... so there is a real urgency to the problem.
CMS cannot repeat these mistakes [of the Meaningful Use program] for technologies like AI at a time of soaring medical burnout...
If digital apps help physicians... no incentives will be needed... If digital apps add to burdens and fragmentation of workflow, it will add to burnout making adoption limited.
We feel that the primary challenges are not technical, but process oriented. The biggest barrier stems from the uncertainty around the ROI for providers...
Interoperability isn't just a technical issue, but a fundamentally a human problem disguised as a technical challenge.

Proprietary Constraints and Cost Barriers From Health IT Vendors: Vendors impose 'information blocking' (high fees for APIs/interfaces/exports)... smaller/safety-net providers disproportionately affected.
Reporting PI measures and meeting required objectives for this program are extremely burdensome and will serve only as a check-the-box exercise...
The future of the health technology ecosystem must be one where no provider is excluded because of their size, location, or inability to pay for a commercial solution.
It’s an unwinnable situation... unless we can safely and effectively use technology in all forms (from FHIR to AI) to massively scale the care our workforce is able to deliver.
Clinicians are burned out, often drowning in technology rather than fighting it.

8.themeDescription: Value-Based Care (VBC) and Payment Model Reform

sections.executiveSummary:
There is universal and emphatic consensus that the success of Value-Based Care (VBC) is critically dependent on a modern, interoperable data infrastructure that provides timely, comprehensive, and actionable information. However, stakeholders from every sector describe the current reality as a landscape of fragmented data, misaligned and eroding financial incentives, and crushing administrative burden. The central debate pits those who believe progress requires top-down mandates for technology standards (like TEFCA and FHIR) against a strong contingent of providers who argue that entities taking on financial risk need maximum flexibility to innovate.

While the vision of VBC is widely supported, its implementation is seen as deeply flawed. Providers feel they are "flying blind," asked to manage risk without the necessary data or financial support. The most dominant and urgent recommendations call for a fundamental restructuring of financial incentives to be stronger, more direct, and available upfront, thereby creating a compelling business case for the difficult operational and technological transformations required for VBC to succeed. Without a clear and sustainable ROI, stakeholders warn that the national shift to value will continue to stagnate, remaining a "hobby exercise" rather than a system-wide transformation.

consensusPoints.text: VBC Success is Critically Dependent on a Modern, Interoperable Data Infrastructure. This is the most foundational point of agreement. Providers, payers, and vendors state that access to comprehensive, longitudinal data (clinical, claims, pharmacy, SDOH) is an absolute prerequisite for population health management, risk stratification, and care coordination. Many risk-bearing providers feel they are "flying blind" due to data fragmentation and latency. This fundamental shift to VBC is "predicated on the seamless and secure sharing of information across providers, health plans, and CMS".

exceptions.text: While all agree on the need, there is significant debate on how to build and pay for this infrastructure (see Areas of Debate).

consensusPoints.text: Current Financial Incentives are Insufficient, Misaligned, and Unsustainable. Commenters consistently argue that current payment models do not adequately support the transition to or sustainability of VBC. Incentives are described as weak, unstable, retrospective, and actively eroding. They do not cover the high upfront costs of technology and practice transformation. For example, Ochsner Health noted that current Medicare payment for Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM) covers only 15% of their costs, while others pointed to the expiration of the Advanced APM bonus as a major disincentive that has "eroded incentives for Medicare APM participants". The market continues to reward "acute, reimbursable events... not upstream, daily improvements".

Quality Measurement in VBC is Overly Burdensome and Needs Modernization. The administrative burden of inconsistent, manual, and duplicative quality reporting is a major pain point that diverts resources from patient care. One MGMA poll found that 94% of members reported VBC has not lessened their regulatory burden. Providers describe submitting "overlapping quality data to multiple payers in different formats, portals, and timelines". There is a strong consensus for simplifying, aligning, and automating reporting by moving to digital quality measures (dQMs).
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all provider organizations and many health IT vendors

consensusPoints.text: Digital Health Tools are Essential but Underfunded Enablers for VBC. Most commenters view technology—including AI, remote monitoring, analytics platforms, and patient engagement tools—as a critical enabler for VBC at scale. These tools are seen as necessary for managing populations and creating efficiencies. However, their adoption is crippled by the lack of clear and sustainable reimbursement pathways. As one group noted, without a strong payment framework, the adoption of innovative tools will occur inequitably, limiting them to large systems with capital.

areasOfDebate.topic: Driving Technology Adoption: Mandates vs. Flexibility

positions.label: Mandate Standards
positions.stance: Mandate universal adoption of specific standards (e.g., FHIR APIs, TEFCA) and Certified EHR Technology (CEHRT) for all VBC and APM participants to ensure a baseline of interoperability and create a level playing field.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by HIEs/QHINs, interoperability vendors, standards organizations, and some large health systems.
keyArguments: Voluntary participation has failed to scale
mandates are necessary to ensure all providers have access to complete patient data
prevents data silos and creates a predictable environment for tech development.

positions.label: Incentivize Performance & Provide Flexibility
positions.stance: Avoid prescriptive technology mandates, especially for entities taking on downside risk. Instead, align financial incentives to reward outcomes and allow the market and providers to choose the most effective tools.
positions.supportLevel: Overwhelmingly supported by provider associations (AMA, AMGA, AAFP, NAACOS), VBC collaboratives, and some health IT firms.
keyArguments: Mandates risk "baking in outdated technologies" and stifling innovation
one-size-fits-all requirements are burdensome and deter participation from smaller/rural practices
organizations with downside risk already have powerful incentives to be efficient and should be granted "greater regulatory flexibility."

areasOfDebate.topic: The Nature and Impact of "Value"

positions.label: Aspirational Goal
positions.stance: VBC, when properly implemented with the right data, tools, and patient-centered focus, is the best path toward achieving the Quintuple Aim (better outcomes, lower costs, better experience, provider well-being, equity).
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of commenters, including providers, tech companies, and associations.
keyArguments: Successful ACOs have demonstrated significant savings for Medicare
technology enables proactive, preventive care that aligns incentives with health, not just sickness.

positions.label: A Flawed Premise
positions.stance: The term 'value' is an ill-defined euphemism for cost-cutting that prioritizes profit over quality, leading to care restrictions, an erosion of patient trust, and provider burnout.
positions.supportLevel: A vocal minority of patient advocates, physicians, and individuals.
keyArguments: VBC "prioritizes money over quality of care," leading to premature discharges and limited treatment choices.
The system "interferes with care" and if it "points to revenue over outcomes, the system is lost".

areasOfDebate.topic: Role of Medicare Advantage (MA)

positions.label: MA as VBC Vehicle
positions.stance: View MA plans as a primary and effective mechanism for advancing VBC, as they are already at-risk entities with incentives to manage population health and integrate technology.
positions.supportLevel: Held by companies operating MA plans and their technology partners.
keyArguments: MA plans can offer supplemental benefits and integrated care platforms
they are an organized market for VBC tools.

positions.label: MA as an Obstacle & Data Black Hole
