positions.stance: Mandate universal FHIR use for all data exchange, enforce it with penalties, and make it a condition of CMS program participation.
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority of technology vendors, digital health innovators, and policy-focused individuals.
keyArguments: The market has failed to produce interoperability voluntarily due to misaligned incentives
"checkbox compliance" proves that only a strong "forcing function" with financial consequences will drive vendors and providers to build high-quality, functional APIs
mandates level the playing field and unlock innovation faster

positions.label: Flexible Incentives & Pragmatic Transition
positions.stance: Use financial incentives, quality metric bonuses, and technical assistance to encourage FHIR adoption, allowing for a phased transition.
positions.supportLevel: Most health plans, provider associations, HIEs, and some EHR vendors.
keyArguments: Rigid mandates can be overly burdensome, especially for smaller or under-resourced entities
a "rip-and-replace" approach is technically and financially infeasible
incentives create a positive business case, driving more organic and sustainable adoption

areasOfDebate.topic: Technical Approach: FHIR-Only vs. Multi-Standard Coexistence

positions.label: Phase Out Legacy
positions.stance: Set clear deadlines to phase out older standards like C-CDA, HL7v2, and Direct Messaging, requiring all exchange via modern FHIR APIs as soon as possible.
positions.supportLevel: A significant number of technology-forward vendors, payers, and consultants.
keyArguments: Supporting multiple standards creates complexity, cost, and redundant workflows
it holds back progress and allows entities to remain on outdated technology
a clean break is necessary for a truly modern ecosystem

positions.label: Pragmatic Coexistence
positions.stance: Continue to support existing, widely-used standards (HL7v2, C-CDA) while encouraging a gradual transition to FHIR.
positions.supportLevel: Health Information Exchanges (HIEs), many large EHR vendors, and provider associations.
keyArguments: Existing standards are the backbone of current, real-world data exchange and work well at scale
forcing a complete overhaul is prohibitively expensive and disruptive
focus should be on getting core FHIR use cases right first

areasOfDebate.topic: The Role and Readiness of Bulk FHIR

positions.label: Essential for Scale Now
positions.stance: Mandate and prioritize the implementation of Bulk FHIR for population health, quality reporting, research, and AI.
positions.supportLevel: Most health data analytics companies, AI developers, and population health advocates.
keyArguments: Bulk FHIR is the only way to get the large-scale data needed for value-based care, AI model training, and public health
the current model of single-patient API calls is "architecturally unsustainable" and inefficient

positions.label: Immature & Risky for Mandates
positions.stance: Bulk FHIR standards and implementations are not ready for prime time, and mandating it now would be a misallocation of resources.
positions.supportLevel: A vocal group of HIEs, provider associations, and some EHR vendors.
keyArguments: Real-world adoption of Bulk FHIR is very low and does not work in a meaningful or scalable way today
focusing resources on it detracts from improving more pressing data quality and transport issues

areasOfDebate.topic: FHIR Versioning and Stability

positions.label: Freeze on R4
positions.stance: Freeze ONC certification on FHIR R4 through 2027 to provide a stable, predictable foundation for the industry to build upon.
positions.supportLevel: One highly detailed technical comment.
keyArguments: A rushed mandate to FHIR R5 or R6 would cause "massive churn with negligible clinical upside," breaking every existing SMART on FHIR app, and doubling implementation overhead for vendors.

positions.label: Continue Advancement
positions.stance: Implicitly, continue moving forward with newer versions (R5, R6) to leverage the latest capabilities, using a managed process like SVAP.
positions.supportLevel: Several commenters reference R5 or "R4+" as the target.
keyArguments: The ecosystem must keep pace with modern standards to avoid being locked into outdated technology.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: "Checkbox compliance" rendering APIs useless; vendor lock-in from proprietary APIs and exorbitant fees; inconsistent standards implementation; a lack of robust enforcement by regulators creating an unlevel playing field.
specificPoints: They are the group most acutely aware of the opportunity cost of a fragmented system and provide the most granular, on-the-ground evidence of implementation failures. They see potential innovation being actively stifled.

Proposed solutions include strong government mandates for FHIR and Bulk FHIR; API-first certification criteria ("API CEHRT"); strict enforcement with public penalties; a national provider directory with FHIR endpoints.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: High cost and administrative burden of implementation, especially prohibitive vendor fees; lack of direct financial ROI; APIs that don't integrate into clinical workflows; "asymmetric mandates" that require them to build APIs that others aren't required to use; deep-seated distrust of payers.
specificPoints: They bear the direct financial and operational burden of implementation. They see the promise but are deeply wary of unfunded mandates and data being used against them by payers.

Proposed solutions include direct financial incentives/grants for adoption; ensuring all ecosystem players (payers, labs, pharmacies) are also required to use standardized APIs; clear, payer-agnostic implementation guides; stronger enforcement against vendor information blocking.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Low provider and patient adoption of the APIs they are required to build, turning a compliance exercise into a sunk cost; fragmentation caused by EHR vendor-specific platforms that bypass open standards.
specificPoints: They worry about making significant investments in "roads that no one drives on" and are vocal about the need for demand-side incentives to ensure providers use the tools they build.

Proposed solutions include requiring or incentivizing providers to use the APIs payers build; allowing for the use of intermediaries/clearinghouses to scale connections; and fully transitioning nationwide frameworks like TEFCA to FHIR.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Policy focus on FHIR overlooks their existing, functional, and scalable infrastructure based on legacy standards; the high cost and complexity of a full FHIR migration.
specificPoints: They serve as a reality check against pure "FHIR-first" ideology, arguing that their current systems are the functional backbone of interoperability for many and that a disruptive forced migration could have significant unintended consequences.

Proposed solutions include a layered approach that leverages existing standards while encouraging FHIR where it adds value, and leveraging HIEs as statewide "FHIR translators" to reduce the burden on individual providers.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Specialty & Underserved Sector Advocates (LTPAC, Dental, Behavioral Health)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Their sectors are consistently overlooked and left behind in interoperability mandates and vendor development priorities, creating a "digital bridge to nowhere."
specificPoints: They highlight that one-size-fits-all medical-centric policies fail to address their unique workflows, vendor markets, and resource constraints, widening the digital divide.

Proposed solutions include explicitly including long-term care, dental, and behavioral health providers and vendors in FHIR mandates and incentive programs, and providing plain-language guidance and direct financial support.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Educational Barrier: SAFI Clinical Informatics Group argues that "the greatest barrier to the success of FHIR-enabled interoperability is not technical, but educational," reframing the problem from one of code to one of human capital and workforce readiness.

The "Digital Bridge to Nowhere": The American Dental Association powerfully illustrates the plight of underserved sectors. Payers are building FHIR capabilities, but since dental software vendors have not been required to adopt FHIR, there are no end-users to connect to, making the investment futile.

The "Negative Feedback Loop" of Compliance: FHIR IQ provides a brilliant model for why "checkbox compliance" occurs: Mandates lead to costly implementation with no direct ROI -> vendors build low-quality APIs -> developers find useless data and build no apps -> lack of apps means no incentive to improve APIs -> the policy goal fails.

The Payer-Provider Trust Deficit: The Greater New York Hospital Association articulated a critical non-technical barrier: hospitals are reluctant to grant payers direct API access due to "longstanding trust issues," fearing the data will be used for audits and denials rather than care coordination.

A Concrete Data Point on ROI: In a sea of concerns about cost, Providence offers a rare, positive, quantitative outcome: "When we switched from exchanging supplemental data through proprietary legacy processes to utilizing the (g)(10) FHIR output...we saw an increase of 40% gap closure across our Value-Based Care contracts."

Quantitative Data on API Failures: Flexpa provides invaluable quantitative data on the state of payer APIs, reporting that "Only 41% of payers achieved >95% authorization success" and "Over 10 million Medicaid members lack access due to non-compliance."

"Portal-API Data Parity": Caregiver James Younkin introduces a simple, powerful, and easily understandable principle: any data visible in a patient portal must also be available via the certified FHIR API.

The Risk of Standards Whiplash (R4 vs. R6): CareEvolution provides a compelling, technical deep-dive into the dangers of moving too quickly from FHIR R4 to R6, warning it would break every production app and create "massive churn with negligible clinical upside."

The Administrative Data "Walled Garden": Zocdoc makes a crucial distinction: while clinical data is increasingly standardized, administrative data like provider schedules remains largely unregulated, allowing EHR vendors to lock down this information for competitive advantage.

The HIE Reality Check: HIEs like CRISP and Contexture serve as a crucial counter-narrative, reminding policymakers that while FHIR is the future, existing standards form the current, functional backbone of interoperability for many.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Great Divide: "Forcing Function" vs. "Pragmatic Incentives": A clear pattern divides commenters into two camps: 1) The "Forcing Function" Camp (mostly tech vendors, startups, policy experts) who believe the market is broken and only aggressive mandates and enforcement will achieve interoperability, and 2) The "Pragmatic/Incentive" Camp (mostly providers, payers, HIEs) who are more sensitive to cost and disruption, advocating for incentives, flexibility, and respect for existing infrastructure.

The Interoperability Standoff (Adoption Asymmetry): A circular "chicken-and-egg" pattern of blame is evident. Payers state providers aren't using the APIs they are mandated to build. Providers and tech vendors state that other key data holders (labs, pharmacies, LTPAC) are not mandated to provide APIs at all, creating critical data gaps. This misalignment is a central theme.

Evolution from "What" to "How": The conversation has matured from focusing on what standard to use (FHIR is the clear winner) to how to implement it effectively: through national directories, stronger enforcement, clearer financial models, API-first certification, and better technical support. The problem is no longer a lack of a standard, but a lack of a functioning market and ecosystem around that standard.

Unintended Consequences of Mandates: Multiple commenters warn that poorly designed or unfunded mandates result in "minimal compliance" or "checkbox compliance" that fulfills the letter of the law but not the spirit of interoperability. A second unintended consequence is that vendors may meet the letter of the law with a free, basic FHIR API while developing proprietary, fee-based "premium" platforms that offer the actual functionality, creating a two-tiered system.

Sectoral Disparities: A clear pattern emerged showing that long-term/post-acute care, dentistry, and behavioral/community health feel consistently marginalized by medical-centric interoperability policies. They face unique vendor markets and resource constraints that are not addressed by current rules, widening the digital divide.

keyQuotations.quote: [The] EHRA proposal is transparently self-serving, reflecting vendors’ reluctance to allow data to leave their proprietary systems... a matter of national security.
[T]he greatest barrier to the success of FHIR-enabled interoperability is not technical, but educational.
When we switched from exchanging supplemental data... to utilizing the (g)(10) FHIR output... we saw an increase of 40% gap closure across our Value-Based Care contracts.
[Carriers] are building roadmaps but express concerns about limited dental end-users due to lack of adoption by dental software vendors, creating a 'digital bridge to nowhere'.
The emergence of platforms like Epic’s Payer Platform... highlights the risk of fragmenting interoperability and placing unnecessary barriers on payer-provider data exchange.
Many hospitals are reluctant to grant payers any level of access to their EHRs, due in large part to longstanding trust issues stemming from historically adversarial relationships.
When standards are only required in one direction, it creates an imbalance that limits true interoperability and discourages investment in scalable solutions.
API activation costs are costly; often $5K–$20K per endpoint.
The single biggest risk of moving the certification baseline from R4 to R6 in the next cycle is massive churn with negligible clinical upside.
Mandate 'Portal-API Data Parity': Any data visible in a provider's patient portal must be available via their certified FHIR API.
Oscar has seen fewer than 100 calls on its provider access API... Instead, many providers rely on alternate, often obsolete, processes like fax and phone calls...
Current interoperability is an illusion based on superficial standards adoption.
A significant setback to transparency, competition, and public accountability is one EHR vendor's effort to bypass public APIs and compel payers to purchase their proprietary payer platform...
Spending time and resources encouraging bulk FHIR could take away resources that could more directly impact interoperability and patient health.

Our predominant way of obtaining complete medical records for users still relies on leveraging the HIPAA right of access through 'old-fashioned' record requests because data typically available through FHIR APIs is incomplete.
Requiring payers to develop standardized APIs without adopting strong incentives to drive utilization is an inefficient approach to interoperability.
The current model of polling for data one resource at a time is architecturally unsustainable and may soon create an immense burden on both developers and provider systems.
We do not believe it is the government’s role to pick winners and losers in the standards space.
Only when FHIR-based exchange is required for TEFCA exchange will all participating entities fully transition away from document-based exchange.

1.2.themeDescription: Improving FHIR API Functionality and Performance

sections.executiveSummary:
There is an overwhelming and universal consensus that the current state of FHIR API implementation is failing to deliver on the promise of interoperability. Stakeholders from all sectors describe a landscape of unreliable, inconsistent, incomplete, and poorly performing APIs that constitute "compliance without interoperability." This "checkbox compliance" has created a façade of functionality that masks a dysfunctional reality, stifling the innovation and improved care the regulations were meant to foster.

Key tensions exist between the strong demand from providers and innovators for more advanced, bi-directional (read/write) and scalable Bulk FHIR APIs versus profound concerns from EHR vendors and medical associations about their technical readiness, performance burden, and clinical liability. While Bulk FHIR is seen as essential for population health and value-based care, its current implementations are almost universally described as non-functional at scale. The dominant recommendation is for CMS and ONC to move beyond theoretical, vendor-controlled testing and enforce real-world functionality through robust, continuous monitoring, mandatory performance SLAs, public scorecards, and tying data quality to payment and accountability measures.

consensusPoints.text: API Implementations are Inconsistent, Unreliable, and Incomplete. A near-unanimous view across all stakeholder groups is that real-world FHIR API implementations are plagued by severe quality, reliability, and consistency issues. Stakeholders agree that varied interpretations of the FHIR standard have led to a proliferation of certified APIs that are functionally unusable, incomplete, and unreliable in production environments. This is the single most pervasive point of agreement in this theme. Representative Examples: Medcurio, Inc. noted, "FHIR-only compliance has produced a proliferation of interfaces that meet the letter of the law, but fail the lived experience of interoperability." Zus Health stated, "The main risk with APIs is availability without reliability... EHR vendors respond with incomplete results despite having FHIR APIs (e.g., heart rate visible in EHR but not via API)." Health Data Aggregator said, "Credentialed API pulls vary widely in the quality and quantity of available information, despite CURES Act requirements." Advocate Health found, "Vendors interpret FHIR specifications differently, leading to inconsistencies in data structure and formatting."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A near-unanimous view across all stakeholder groups

consensusPoints.text: Stricter Performance Standards and Real-World Testing Are Necessary. A strong majority of commenters, particularly technology vendors, provider organizations, and professional associations, explicitly called for ONC and CMS to mandate enforceable performance standards, or Service Level Agreements (SLAs). There is broad agreement that certification must evolve to include real-world testing, continuous monitoring, and public transparency to hold vendors accountable for the actual performance of their APIs in production, not just in sanitized test environments. Representative Examples: The Texas Medical Association recommended, "CMS should incorporate API performance Standards and Service-Level Agreements (SLAs) into the ONC Health IT Certification Program. These API performance standards should include minimum uptime thresholds (>99.99%), maximum response times (≤500 milliseconds for most endpoints), and data-freshness requirements." HealthLX suggested to, "Require Real-World Testing and Transparency as part of the 21st Century Cures Act’s API condition of certification, including publishing API performance scorecards." Zus Health argued, "The test suite must accurately reflect live EHR platform performance, not just sandboxes, as issues often arise outside sanitized environments." A Health Data Analytics Company stated a "'Neighborhood Watch' mechanism [is needed] to ensure performance standards are met by encouraging stakeholders to publicly identify EHR vendors that fail to enable effective data exchange."

Bulk FHIR Functionality is Critically Flawed in Practice. While many stakeholders see Bulk FHIR as essential for population health and value-based care, there is overwhelming consensus among those with hands-on experience that current implementations are broken, non-performant, and unusable at scale. Commenters cited numerous failures, including exports that never complete, extreme time delays, missing or corrupted data, and artificial limits on cohort size that defeat the purpose of bulk transfer. Representative Examples: The SMART Health IT team, developers of the standard, confirm that research shows "EHR vendor Bulk FHIR implementations are inadequate, with large vendors underinvesting and discouraging public API adoption." A Nonprofit Technology Organization found that, "Only one out of several tested CEHRT Bulk FHIR implementations met data availability and reliability requirements," with common issues being jobs that "never complete," "fail before completion," or return "corrupted or missing data." Providence, an integrated health system, detailed that their testing was unsuccessful, with queries for a single patient running for "days without completion" and cohort limits of 1,000 patients being insufficient for real-world use.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: overwhelming consensus among those with hands-on experience

exceptions.text: No commenters defended the current state of Bulk FHIR. However, EHR vendors attribute the problems to low real-world adoption, lack of provider infrastructure investment, and the inherent performance strain, while users of the technology demand stronger mandates to force vendors to fix the broken implementations.

consensusPoints.text: Data Timeliness (Latency) is a Major Barrier. A significant cohort of commenters stressed that data delays—from weeks-long lags in claims data via Blue Button 2.0 to non-real-time API responses—severely limit the data's utility for clinical decision-making, care coordination, and patient engagement. Representative Examples: Twilight Technologies, Inc stated, "Blue Button 2.0 claims data 'can be delayed by several weeks,' severely 'limiting its utility for real-time care decisions, especially during discharge or acute transitions'." As caregiver James Younkin bluntly stated, "Data delayed is data denied." NextGen Healthcare noted, "Provider directory data and cost data need to be accessible on a real-time basis for it to be useful and actionable in the present."

areasOfDebate.topic: Bi-Directional (Read/Write) API Mandates

positions.label: Mandate Now
positions.stance: A strong majority of technology vendors and several provider specialty groups call for mandating full read/write API capabilities to unlock innovation and transform EHRs into active, collaborative platforms.
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority
keyArguments: Read-only access stifles innovation for tools that need to schedule patients, update care plans, or automate workflows; bi-directional exchange is essential for real-time care coordination and reducing provider burden.

positions.label: Proceed with Caution
positions.stance: A smaller but highly influential group, including major medical centers and physician associations, warns that "write" interoperability carries immense clinical liability and data integrity risks that must be addressed before any mandate.
positions.supportLevel: A smaller but highly influential group
keyArguments: Covered entities must retain control over what enters the legal medical record to avoid an "ambiguous, unverified source of truth"; this creates an "unknown liability framework" for clinicians if data enters the record without their knowledge and magnifies risks of data corruption and cyberattacks.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Future of Bulk FHIR

positions.label: Double Down and Mandate Now
positions.stance: Mandate and enforce robust Bulk FHIR implementations because it is critical for population health, quality reporting, and research.
positions.supportLevel: A majority of commenters who see its potential, including ACOs, technology vendors, public health advocates, and providers.
keyArguments: The potential value outweighs the current problems; federal mandates will force vendors to fix broken implementations and invest in performance; it is essential for moving away from inefficient single-patient queries.

positions.label: Pump the Brakes and Incentivize First
positions.stance: Pause or reconsider broad Bulk FHIR mandates until standards mature and implementations are proven to be stable and performant; focus on funding pilots and creating business cases first.
positions.supportLevel: A significant minority, including EHR vendors, their trade association (EHRA), and some health information professionals.
keyArguments: The technology is not ready for scalable implementation due to immaturity and "growing pains"; it places a major performance and cost burden on provider infrastructure not architected for it; forcing it now will increase costs without clear benefit.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: APIs that are unreliable, inconsistent, incomplete, and slow ("compliance without interoperability"); vendor gatekeeping through friction in app registration and authentication (e.g., short-lived refresh tokens); lack of real-world test environments and responsive support channels for bug fixes; artificial limits on Bulk FHIR.
specificPoints: They provide the most detailed, granular, and damning evidence of how certified APIs fail in production. They see these failures as a direct barrier to building scalable products and articulate how poor performance creates a negative feedback loop that stifles the entire ecosystem.

Proposed solutions include mandating stringent, testable performance SLAs; requiring public performance dashboards; streamlining app registration and mandating long-lived refresh tokens; mandating support for advanced capabilities like Bulk FHIR and Subscriptions; and prohibiting information blocking via poor performance.
Novelcare documents how only one of over a dozen EHRs had a working Bulk FHIR API.
Primary Record provides a vivid example of a broken API connection taking 47 emails to resolve.
Redox details extreme frustration with authentication and rate limits.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Healthcare Providers (Hospitals, Health Systems, ACOs, FQHCs)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The failure of APIs, especially Bulk FHIR, directly impedes population health management and value-based care. Data is often incomplete or delayed, harming clinical workflows, patient care, and quality reporting. They also bear the burden of performance degradation and implementation costs.
specificPoints: They connect abstract technical failures to concrete operational and clinical consequences. They provide firsthand accounts of Bulk FHIR failing at small scales, let alone for entire populations.
Proposed solutions include strong government mandates forcing EHR vendors to deliver performant and reliable APIs. Many support funding and technical assistance for implementation.
Providence and Emory Healthcare give firsthand accounts of Bulk FHIR failing.
NAACOS stresses that Bulk FHIR is a "major challenge" for ACOs.
Johns Hopkins adds a critical warning about the liability of "write" access.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Professional & Trade Associations (AHIMA, AMA, WEDI, etc.)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring standards are mature and stable before being mandated; managing implementation timelines and cost burdens for their members; avoiding conflicting requirements and addressing liability risks.
specificPoints: These groups often take a more measured, policy-focused view, balancing the desire for progress with the practical realities of implementation. They often act as a voice of caution.

Proposed solutions include phasing in requirements based on industry readiness; working closely with SDOs to mature standards; providing clear implementation guides and testing tools; and addressing legal and liability frameworks before mandating high-risk functionality like "write" access.
The AMA and AHIMA are notably cautious.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The high cost and technical burden of implementing immature standards (like Bulk FHIR) without clear provider demand or financial incentives. They worry about the performance impact of high-volume data requests on production systems.
specificPoints: They frame the problem as one of market maturity and infrastructure readiness, rather than vendor unwillingness. They advocate for a more cautious, incentive-driven approach before new mandates are imposed.

Proposed solutions include CMS focusing on creating policy drivers and incentives to build demand for advanced APIs before mandating them. They advocate for flexibility, pilot programs, and investment in testing tools.
The HIMSS EHRA comment is the most comprehensive articulation of this view.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of real-time, frictionless access to complete health information; data lags that impact critical care decisions; APIs that are technically available but practically unusable or provide less data than patient portals.
specificPoints: They bring a powerful, human-centered focus, illustrating the real-world consequences of data gaps and delays with compelling personal stories.

Proposed solutions include mandating real-time data access ("Contemporaneous Access Rule"); ensuring APIs provide data parity with patient portals ("Portal-API Data Parity"); and supporting bi-directional functionality for wellness apps.
Grace Cordovano and James Younkin provide powerful testimony on the human cost of data delays.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The complexity and cost of implementing FHIR APIs, inconsistent security protocols, and the burden of storing large amounts of data.
specificPoints: Oscar Health's admission that FHIR API development is so complex they had to outsource it, despite being a technology-focused company, is a stark indicator of the standard's implementation difficulty.
Proposed solutions include making APIs bidirectional, testing APIs at scale, and finding solutions to mitigate bulk data storage burdens.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Architectural Root of Bulk FHIR's Failure: The Health Care Transformation Task Force points out that most EHR vendors implemented Bulk FHIR on their live, transactional application layer instead of their reporting data layer. This architectural choice is cheaper but guarantees poor performance and creates operational risks, leading vendors to artificially limit its use.

The Caregiver's Plea: The personal comment from caregiver James Younkin crystallizes the human impact of these technical failures with the phrase "Data delayed is data denied." His specific recommendations for "Portal-API Data Parity" and a "Contemporaneous Access Rule" are directly born from lived experience.

The Liability of "Write" Access: Johns Hopkins makes a crucial and sophisticated distinction between "read" and "write" interoperability, warning that the latter could create an "ambiguous, unverified legal medical record" and an "unknown clinical liability framework." This is a critical counterpoint to broad calls for bi-directional mandates.

The 1-in-12 Success Rate: Novelcare reports that of over a dozen EHR vendors they tried to integrate with, only one had a Bulk FHIR implementation that worked "out of the box." This data point is a powerful indictment of the current certification process's inability to ensure real-world functionality.

A Damning Indictment from a Tech-Savvy Payer: Oscar Health, a technology-driven insurer, stated, "The complexity and rigidity of existing FHIR-based API requirements have led Oscar to outsource these APIs, despite our preference to build technology in-house". This suggests the standards are so difficult to implement correctly that even capable tech companies are struggling.

The "47 Email" Problem: Jean Ross of Primary Record provides a stunningly concrete example of ecosystem failure: a major EHR vendor's configuration change broke data flows, and it took 47 emails across three companies to diagnose and fix, illustrating the lack of monitoring, accountability, and support.

The Stark Performance Gap: The SMART Health IT team notes that a federally-funded academic partner built a highly performant Bulk FHIR interface in weeks, which "substantially outperforming certified interfaces from major EHR vendors," implying the problem is a lack of will, not technical feasibility.

Unsustainable Architecture: Unite articulates a powerful systemic risk, arguing that the current requirement for apps to "poll" for data one resource at a time is "architecturally unsustainable" and will lead to "systemic performance degradation and potential infrastructure collapse" as the ecosystem grows.

Intra-Vendor Interoperability Failure: Civitas Networks for Health highlights that one of the most persistent problems is a lack of interoperability between different versions of the same EHR vendor's software, pointing to a failure in vendors' backward compatibility and version management.

emergingPatterns.pattern: EHR Vendor vs. The Ecosystem: A clear and consistent pattern emerged pitting the perspective of EHR vendors (via the EHRA) against virtually all other stakeholders. While the ecosystem demands immediate mandates for performance and functionality, the EHRA advocates for a slower, incentive-driven approach, highlighting a fundamental tension.

The Gap Between Certification and Reality: Numerous commenters highlighted a chasm between a product being "certified" and being functionally useful in a real-world, production environment. This "check-the-box" phenomenon suggests the current certification process is insufficient as it fails to test for performance, reliability, and usability at scale.

Proprietary APIs Outperforming Mandated APIs: Several commenters noted that vendors often offer superior performance and functionality through their own proprietary, fee-based APIs, while the mandated, standards-based APIs lag significantly. This pattern suggests a potential conflict of interest and a lack of vendor commitment to open standards.

The Negative Feedback Loop: A recurring pattern described how poor APIs lead to low developer and user adoption, which in turn gives vendors little business incentive to invest in improving the APIs, creating a vicious cycle that stifles the entire ecosystem.

Experience Correlations: A clear pattern emerges where commenters with direct, hands-on software development and integration experience provide the most urgent and detailed critiques of API failures, while stakeholders with a higher-level policy view tend to be more focused on implementation costs, timelines, and standards maturity.

keyQuotations.quote: [The] Existence of an API does not equal availability which in turn does not equal usability.
Data delayed is data denied.
FHIR-only compliance has produced a proliferation of interfaces that meet the letter of the law, but fail the lived experience of interoperability.

Across every EHR vendor Novelcare has attempted to integrate with, except for one, at least one function required by ONC’s g(10) criteria did not work when we started. Many still do not work to this day.

For 'write' interoperability, covered entities and their associated legal medical records must retain control over when new data enters the record due to immense risks such as creating an ambiguous, unverified legal medical record and an unknown clinical liability framework.
When testing a single patient with multiple years of history within our organization – The bulk query ran for a period of days without completion.
The complexity and rigidity of existing FHIR-based API requirements have led Oscar to outsource these APIs, despite our preference to build technology in-house.

A configuration change by a major EHR vendor earlier this year caused all medication and lab data to stop flowing into Primary Record across multiple patient accounts without notification, taking 47 emails across three companies (EHR vendor, API intermediary, Primary Record) to diagnose and resolve.
The current model of polling for data one resource at a time is architecturally unsustainable and may soon create an immense burden on both developers and provider systems.

Almost every EHR vendor API has some element of information blocking. These include cumbersome onboarding processes for apps, proprietary logins, stale access tokens, throttled performance, and incomplete data.
Technology that tests well in a vendor-controlled setting can and does break down in real-world use.
The indifference EHR vendors have toward patient access when consumer FHIR application developers report an issue with their endpoint needs to be addressed.

The primary reason Bulk FHIR APIs are not adequately responsive is that the data is extracted directly from the EHRs’ production environments. These systems are architected to support EHR operations, not bulk data sharing.
Open standards-based APIs need to be as polished, reliable, and well-supported as proprietary software; otherwise, they will always remain niche products.
Varied, poor-quality, and unreliable implementations undermine the value of the standard.

Critical functions such as auto-filling notes, scheduling patients, and adding functionality to the workflow within the EHR require write access, which is not currently required today... This effectively stifles innovation...
Most healthcare organizations treat interoperability as a compliance exercise rather than a useful functional capability.

1.3.themeDescription: Enabling Actionable Workflows with Advanced APIs

sections.executiveSummary:
There is an overwhelming and unified consensus among all stakeholder groups that healthcare interoperability must evolve beyond passive, read-only data access to enable actionable, event-driven workflows. The public input clearly shows a demand to transform health information from a static record into an active agent for change. Commenters universally support mandating a suite of advanced API capabilities—specifically FHIR Subscriptions for real-time notifications, CDS Hooks for workflow integration, full CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) access, and standardized APIs for high-burden administrative tasks like prior authorization and scheduling.

The primary tension lies not in the goal, but in the execution. A strong push from technology innovators, patient advocates, and app developers to aggressively mandate these capabilities is countered by significant cautions from EHR vendors and provider organizations about the profound security, liability, and patient safety risks of "write" access, as well as the technical and financial burdens of implementation. Key debates center on the pace of mandating write access—with a nuanced, risk-stratified approach emerging as a viable path forward—and the best technical strategy for population-level data exchange (Bulk FHIR). The most significant barrier identified, beyond technology, is a combination of EHR vendor business practices that stifle innovation and the fundamental "trust factor" required to allow third-party applications to interact with core clinical systems.

consensusPoints.text: Existing Read-Only APIs Are Insufficient for Meaningful Innovation. There is a virtually universal consensus across all stakeholder groups—providers, payers, tech vendors, and patient advocates—that the current focus on simple, read-only data access is a necessary but insufficient first step. A powerful agreement exists that to build next-generation applications, reduce burden, and enable proactive care, APIs must evolve to support interactive, bidirectional, event-driven, and administrative workflows. This is seen as the essential next step for interoperability.

Mandatory Event Notifications Are Crucial for Proactive Care. A strong majority of stakeholders explicitly call for mandated, real-time event notifications to move from an inefficient "pull" model (constant polling) to an efficient "push" model. Technologies like FHIR Subscriptions and webhooks are seen as critical for enabling timely care transitions (e.g., ADT alerts), reducing readmissions, and supporting proactive care coordination in value-based care.

exceptions.text: While support is broad, some providers like Advocate Health caution that event notification requirements must be implemented without creating undue burden on hospitals.

consensusPoints.text: High-Burden Administrative Workflows Must Be Automated via APIs. There is overwhelming consensus that high-friction, high-cost administrative processes are prime targets for API-based automation. Commenters universally support mandating standardized, real-time electronic workflows, with two use cases cited most frequently: Prior Authorization (PA): Stakeholders like The Cigna Group, American Osteopathic Association, and FinThrive universally identified PA as a major source of burden and care delays and called for mandating the use of standardized APIs, specifically referencing the HL7 Da Vinci Project Implementation Guides (CRD, DTR, PAS). Appointment Scheduling: A large and diverse group of commenters including Curai Health and the AOA identified interoperable scheduling as a critical need to improve care navigation and reduce administrative waste.

areasOfDebate.topic: Mandating Bi-Directional (Write) API Access

positions.label: Mandate Now
positions.stance: Require certified APIs to support full CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) capabilities to enable truly interactive applications, reduce manual data entry, and allow patients to contribute to their own records. Without write access, EHRs remain passive repositories, not collaborative platforms.
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority of technology vendors, patient/caregiver advocacy groups, and some individual providers.
keyArguments: Read-only access perpetuates a passive patient role and creates a one-way street, preventing apps from closing workflow loops (e.g., scheduling, intake forms, submitting patient-reported data).
It is the logical and necessary next step for interoperability.

positions.label: Caution, Control, and Nuance
positions.stance: Acknowledge the need for bi-directional exchange but emphasize immense security, liability, and patient safety challenges. A one-size-fits-all mandate is premature and dangerous without resolving trust, governance, and technical frameworks.
positions.supportLevel: EHR developers and their associations; clinically-focused organizations.
keyArguments: Allowing third-party apps to write data directly into a clinical EHR poses significant trust and security risks and could compromise data integrity.
A more nuanced, risk-based approach is needed; the safety risk of writing a medication order is vastly different from writing a care plan update.
A blanket mandate could stifle low-risk innovation or create unacceptable safety hazards.

areasOfDebate.topic: Best Approach for Population-Level Data Access

positions.label: Aggressive Mandate of Bulk FHIR
positions.stance: Mandate and accelerate the use of Bulk FHIR APIs for population health, quality reporting, research, and analytics.
positions.supportLevel: The majority of tech vendors, payers, researchers, and value-based care organizations.
keyArguments: It is the most efficient standard for large-scale data extraction; it shifts the burden of quality metric calculation from providers to payers; it enables scalable analytics and AI model training.

positions.label: Phased, Cautious, or Alternative Approaches
positions.stance: Bulk FHIR has major performance, cost, and technical issues that must be addressed through pilots and further development before broad mandates. Alternatives may be better.
positions.supportLevel: Some providers, technical experts, and at least one major EHR vendor association.
keyArguments: Performance with large patient groups is problematic (some EHRs cap groups at ~5000 members); it requires significant IT infrastructure and skilled personnel; some argue that optimizing real-time, single-patient transactional APIs could be more efficient and less burdensome for many use cases if the underlying network is fast enough.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Removing technical and regulatory barriers to innovation, especially EHR vendor information blocking through high fees or proprietary systems; lack of standardized APIs for essential workflows (write-access, notifications, scheduling); inefficient "polling" data access models.
specificPoints: They are the strongest and most unified voice advocating for mandating the most advanced capabilities now. They see the technology as largely ready and policy as the primary bottleneck preventing a competitive, innovative market.
Mandate a wide suite of open, certified APIs including full CRUD, FHIR Subscriptions, CDS Hooks, scheduling, and Bulk FHIR; enforce strict penalties for information blocking.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing administrative burden (especially prior authorization and scheduling); improving care coordination through timely notifications (ADT); ensuring new technologies integrate smoothly into clinical workflows without increasing cognitive load, cost, or patient safety risks.
specificPoints: Their support is pragmatic and use-case driven. They balance the desire for innovation with the realities of clinical workflow and resource constraints. Large health systems highlight the complexity of integrating external tools with internal logic, while smaller practices are more focused on basic API access.
Universally support APIs to automate prior authorization and scheduling. Propose a nuanced, risk-based approach to write access to balance innovation and safety.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing the burden on patients/caregivers who manually coordinate care; making health information and tools actionable, not just informational; enabling direct, proactive interaction with the healthcare system through apps.
specificPoints: This group provides a powerful, human-centered vision for what technology should enable, focusing relentlessly on empowerment and the end-user experience. They articulate the frustration of fragmented care and the need for tools that are dynamic and interactive.
Mandate open APIs that mirror all functions available in a patient portal ("API parity"); enable patient-initiated data correction and contribution via write-enabled interactions.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Streamlining operations, particularly the costly and inefficient prior authorization process; gaining access to clinical data for quality measurement and value-based care programs without overburdening providers.
specificPoints: Their comments often focus on the Payer-Provider relationship and see advanced APIs as a key tool for both administrative efficiency and executing value-based care strategies.
Strongly support mandating Prior Authorization APIs. Promote payer use of Bulk FHIR APIs to extract clinical quality data, shifting the calculation burden from providers.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Visionary Analogy: "Waze for Healthcare". Patient advocate Grace Cordovano powerfully frames the need for dynamic, personalized, and proactive digital tools by comparing the desired functionality to the Waze driving app. This analogy captures the essence of moving from a static map (the EHR record) to real-time, intelligent guidance.

Risk-Stratified Write Access. Aledade, Inc. provides a critical, nuanced insight: not all "write" access is equal. They propose that API controls should be granular and differ based on the data's safety implications (e.g., low-risk for updating a care plan vs. high-risk for ordering a medication). This offers a pragmatic path forward that balances innovation with patient safety.

Shifting the Paradigm from Data Access to Workflow Automation. Parachute Health argued the goal isn't just "access to data" but building infrastructure for systems to communicate and solve workflow problems, requiring "interoperable workflows, not just more granular data formats." This reframes the entire purpose of API expansion.

The "Trust Factor" as the Core Barrier to Write Access. The HIMSS Electronic Health Record Association succinctly identified the core challenge to bi-directional interoperability not as a technical problem, but as one of "trust," as providers would have to allow independent applications with which they have no relationship to write data into their core clinical systems.

The Future is Dynamic AI Agents, Not Static Apps. Caregiver and Health IT professional James Younkin offers a forward-looking perspective, arguing the goal is not just supporting registered apps, but enabling "millions of patients and caregivers interacting with generative AI applications — agents that are created and configured dynamically, at the moment of need."

Data-backed Observation on Proactive Monitoring. BioIntelliSense provided compelling study data showing their continuous monitoring system's alerts preceded clinical deterioration events by an average of 14.8 hours. This is a powerful, concrete example of how real-time, event-driven data enables proactive intervention.

In-Place Computation vs. Bulk Data Transfer. Selfiie proposes a paradigm shift for analytics: "It would be significantly better for data holders to respond to algorithms in place (in-place computation) rather than shipping data around, especially if PHI leakage can be guaranteed against."

Closed-Loop Referrals Require a Transactional Layer. Findhelp made a critical distinction that USCDI "documents an event but does not standardize the real-time, bidirectional communication needed for workflows like scheduling or referrals," highlighting the need for a transactional API layer.

Creative Enforcement: Make the Customer Liable. Dan Wilson of Moxe Health suggests a novel market-based enforcement mechanism: allow any Covered Entity to authorize their Business Associates to use any workflow their own employees can, and make the Covered Entity liable for any misuse. This would pressure providers to demand better APIs from vendors.

emergingPatterns.pattern: From Data Access to Workflow Integration: The discourse has clearly shifted from "Can I get the data?" to "Can I do something with the data in a way that fits my workflow and solves a problem?" This is evident in the repeated, specific calls for APIs for scheduling, prior authorization, and care plan updates.

The Mandate Impasse: A clear pattern emerges where technology innovators, patient advocates, and app developers push for aggressive, comprehensive mandates to break the status quo, while incumbent EHR vendors and many provider groups urge caution, emphasizing workflow usability, implementation costs, and proven value to avoid creating unfunded mandates with low adoption.

Unintended Consequences of Partial or Poorly Designed Rules: A subtle but critical pattern is the warning against piecemeal solutions. Wolters Kluwer's analysis of CDS Hooks, Parachute Health's warning against data-element-focus over workflow-focus, and Novelcare's warning that Bulk FHIR performance rules could be gamed all highlight that a half-measure could be worse than no measure.

Administrative and Clinical Convergence: Commenters consistently blur the line between clinical and administrative data, arguing that workflows like scheduling, intake, and prior authorization are inseparable from the clinical experience and must be supported by the same interoperability standards.

Provider Setting Variations: Large, sophisticated health systems are already using advanced tools internally but highlight the complexity of integrating external apps into their customized logic (Northwestern Medicine). In contrast, organizations serving post-acute care or smaller practices are more focused on getting reliable access to basic standardized APIs for care transitions and administrative relief.

keyQuotations.quote: It’s time to move beyond APIs that let us read records, and toward protocols that let us change lives.
We need real-time, dynamic, context-aware guidance, just like how Waze reroutes drivers based on traffic, construction, or accidents ahead.

No one ever died due to an erroneous CRM system entry. However, it cannot be the case that every write access request is treated like it is an order for medication. This would greatly limit innovation, particularly with regards to AI.
Anything that patients can do in their EHR portal needs to have a matching open API to allow them to complete those functions in an application of their choice.

The challenge isn’t simply 'access to data' — it's building infrastructure where systems of record can communicate... Solving for these requires interoperable workflows, not just more granular data formats.
Instead, the challenge in enabling patients’ selected third-party products to facilitate these processes arises more from the 'trust' factor...

Ultimately, enabling full read/write interoperability is essential to transforming EHRs from passive repositories into active, collaborative platforms that empower patients, providers, payers, and developers alike.
Data in motion only reduces burden when it lands at the right time where decisions are made.
[The future is] millions of patients and caregivers interacting with generative AI applications — agents that are created and configured dynamically, at the moment of need.

The prior authorization process is complementary to the prescribing workflow; within a single workflow, physicians should be able to... View prior authorization requirements... [and] Submit prior authorizations.
The absence of event-based APIs/webhooks forces inefficient constant polling for data.

The challenge with only supporting the order-sign hook... is that the clinician will only be accessing the clinical decision support services much later in their workflow, when a treatment decision may have already been made.
Current APIs provide snapshots, but healthcare is a continuous story, requiring APIs that support narrative understanding, not just transactional reporting.
Some larger EHRs are equipped to provide advanced APIs but their proprietary nature often prevents them from being made available for use by other vendor partners or they are priced unaffordably.

sections.executiveSummary: There is overwhelming, near-universal consensus that the U.S. Core Data for Interoperability (USCDI) is a valuable and successful foundation for nationwide data exchange. However, there is an equally strong and widespread agreement that its current scope is critically insufficient to support comprehensive patient care, value-based models, clinical research, or advanced AI-driven innovation. The central tension lies between the urgent, cross-stakeholder demand for a rapid and broad expansion of USCDI's data elements—especially for SDOH, behavioral health, specialty care, and clinical notes—and the profound concerns from implementers (providers, payers, and EHR vendors) about the unsustainable cost, complexity, and pace of a monolithic, one-size-fits-all expansion. The dominant recommendation is to move toward a more strategic, predictable, and modular expansion of USCDI, one that accommodates specialty-specific needs while simultaneously addressing the severe real-world gaps and inconsistencies in the implementation of the existing standard.

consensusPoints.text: USCDI is a valuable but insufficient foundation. A vast majority of commenters (over 95%) expressed the dual sentiment that USCDI has been a significant and positive step in standardizing data for exchange, but that its current scope is far too limited for modern healthcare needs. As stated by McKesson Corporation, it has established a baseline but falls short for complex use cases, particularly in specialty care and value-based care. The National Health Council agrees USCDI is crucial but finds its scope omits critical patient-centered data.

exceptions.text: A small number of commenters either felt the current USCDI was sufficient for their needs, such as OtisHealth, Inc., or opposed any mandate for USCDI expansion on principle.

consensusPoints.text: There is a strong, cross-stakeholder demand to expand USCDI. A strong majority of commenters (~75%) made specific recommendations for expansion. The most frequently requested additions form a consistent set of priorities across stakeholder groups: Social Determinants of Health (SDOH), mentioned by dozens of commenters like Socially Determined and Civitas Networks for Health; Behavioral and Mental Health Data, a critical gap noted by groups like the American Psychological Association and NeuroFlow; Unstructured Data & Clinical Notes, with calls from organizations like the American Urological Association and Lantana; Pharmacy and Medication Data, requested by the American College of Cardiology and Health Science Partners; Patient-Generated Health Data (PGHD), supported by Rune Labs and Adherium; and Specialty-Specific Data, with advocacy from fields like oncology and maternal health from groups such as CareQuest Institute and Delfina Care.

The burden and pace of expansion create significant challenges. Many commenters, particularly technology vendors, payers, and provider groups, warned that simply adding more elements to a monolithic standard creates significant problems. Key challenges cited include the high cost and burden of implementation, technical complexity, the disruptive pace of annual version updates, and the difficulty of reaching stakeholder consensus. The EHR Association (EHRA) noted the difficult cadence of updates, the American Hospital Association (AHA) expressed concern over aggressive timelines, and the Blue Cross Blue Shield Association (BCBSA) cited implementation challenges from the frequency and volume of updates.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most technology vendors, payers, and provider associations highlighted these challenges.

consensusPoints.text: Implementation variability and gaps undermine the standard's value. A significant number of commenters, particularly those with real-world integration experience, noted that even when data elements are included in USCDI, their inconsistent implementation by EHR vendors and providers leads to data gaps, poor quality, and unreliability, severely undermining the goal of standardization. For example, Imagine Pediatrics noted that major networks consistently lack the full expected USCDI dataset, Moxe Health pointed to slight variations in implementations, and CORMAC Corp. cited significant variance in code mappings between EHR vendors.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong consensus among technology integrators, HIEs, and digital health companies.

areasOfDebate.topic: Expansion Strategy: How to Grow USCDI
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to expand USCDI as a single, comprehensive standard or through optional, modular extensions.

positions.label: Comprehensive Core
positions.stance: Mandate a continually expanding, single, unified USCDI standard that all certified health IT must support.
positions.supportLevel: Implicitly supported by most patient advocates, researchers, and app developers; explicitly by a few organizations like The Joint Commission.
keyArguments: Ensures a high, consistent floor of data is available everywhere
drives universal adoption of critical new data elements like SDOH
avoids a confusing patchwork of standards and prevents siloing data by use case

positions.label: Modular Extensions
positions.stance: Keep the core USCDI relatively small and stable, while developing optional, use-case-specific modules (like USCDI+) that vendors can certify to based on their specialty or market.
positions.supportLevel: Explicitly proposed by a strong majority of EHR vendors, trade associations, specialty providers, and some large health systems.
keyArguments: Reduces burden on specialty EHRs (e.g., behavioral health, oncology, dentistry) who don't need to support irrelevant data
allows for more agile development of new data sets without disrupting the entire ecosystem
prevents an 'all-or-nothing' approach that stalls progress

areasOfDebate.topic: Pace of USCDI Updates
areasOfDebate.description: Debate on the ideal frequency of USCDI version updates, balancing the need for rapid innovation with implementation stability.

positions.label: Accelerate
positions.stance: Accelerate the review and approval process for new USCDI versions to a quarterly or more frequent cadence.
positions.supportLevel: A vocal minority, primarily digital health innovators.
keyArguments: The current annual process is too slow to keep pace with technology and clinical needs
data elements are stuck in draft stages for too long, hindering progress

positions.label: Slow Down
positions.stance: Adopt a more predictable and longer update cycle (e.g., every two to three years) to reduce implementation burden and increase stability.
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority of payers and provider associations.
keyArguments: Aggressive timelines and frequent updates overwhelm vendors and providers, leading to versioning issues and inconsistent adoption.
A slower, more evidence-based process would ensure stability.

areasOfDebate.topic: Data Access Philosophy: Standardized Core vs. Comprehensive EHI
areasOfDebate.description: Debate on whether to prioritize perfecting a structured USCDI core or to enable broader access to all Electronic Health Information (EHI), including unstructured data.

positions.label: Prioritize Structure
positions.stance: Focus on the incremental expansion and enforcement of a well-defined, structured, and universally implemented USCDI. Full, unstructured EHI is too messy and unstandardized to be useful and would hinder interoperability.
positions.supportLevel: Large EHR vendors, some provider associations, and technically-focused commenters.
keyArguments: Structured data is essential for automation, accuracy, and safe clinical use
a 'data dump' of full EHI overwhelms receiving systems
a reliable core must be established first

positions.label: Prioritize Access to Full EHI
positions.stance: USCDI is inherently too limited and will always be a 'lowest common denominator.' The priority should be to enable API-based access to the complete Electronic Health Information (EHI), leveraging modern tools like AI/NLP to process unstructured data.
positions.supportLevel: A vocal group of data analytics firms, AI companies, patient data access companies, and some payers.
keyArguments: The most valuable clinical context is in unstructured notes, which USCDI omits
limiting access to USCDI severely constrains clinical utility and innovation
advances in AI make processing unstructured data more feasible

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The rapid, monolithic cadence of USCDI updates is unsustainable; one-size-fits-all requirements burden specialty vendors; lack of clear, granular implementation guidance leads to inconsistent interpretations; the need for better ONC testing tools.
specificPoints: They experience the technical burden of implementation and often treat USCDI as a compliance 'floor' or 'ceiling,' stopping data sharing at the minimum required.
They highlighted the confusion between USCDI and USCDI+ publications and an imbalance where they must certify but payers and apps do not.

Proposed solutions: A modular approach to certification (core + optional extensions), a more predictable and slower update cycle (e.g., every two years), consolidation of USCDI and USCDI+ into a unified framework, and more robust implementation guides and test decks from ONC.
Flatiron Health highlighted being forced to implement pediatric data elements irrelevant to their adult oncology EHR.
Ensora Health forcefully argued that forcing behavioral health providers to adopt medical-centric elements is inequitable.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: USCDI lacks the specific data elements needed for their specialty (e.g., oncology, dentistry, nephrology, maternal health, post-acute care); lack of vocabulary standards for existing elements hinders interoperability; EMR vendors use USCDI as a ceiling to limit data sharing.
specificPoints: They experience the direct clinical impact of data gaps and connect them to patient safety and care quality. The American Urological Association noted that treatment decisions depend on longitudinal data like operative reports, which are often missing.

Proposed solutions: Expand USCDI with specific, clinically-validated data elements; mandate that all USCDI elements have associated vocabulary standards; slow down the update cycle to allow for adaptation.
The Texas Medical Association notes that '57 out of 132 data elements in USCDI v6 lack correlating vocabulary standards'.
AHCA/NCAL pointed out that post-acute care providers were largely excluded from HITECH incentives and thus lack the certified EHRs needed to use USCDI.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The current USCDI is far too narrow, omitting the majority of a patient's story, including full clinical notes, images, SDOH, functional status, and PGHD. Data access is fragmented and incomplete.
specificPoints: They focus on the 'lived experience' of patients, providing the most granular and compelling examples of missing data needed for self-management, care coordination, and understanding the whole person.
They are less concerned with technical challenges and more focused on the end result.
Proposed solutions: Radically expand USCDI to include the full HIPAA 'designated record set'; mandate inclusion of PGHD and data reflecting quality of life and functional status.

Patient advocate Grace Cordovano provided a highly specific list of missing data, including 'chemotherapy infusion notes' and 'where pathology samples are being stored,' which are routinely inaccessible.
An Occupational Therapist argued for tracking factors like 'Cognition, Mobility, Being Pain Free, Engagement, Confidence'.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The 'frequency and volume of updates' to USCDI create significant implementation and versioning challenges. They also note the lack of administrative and quality-related data elements needed for value-based care.
specificPoints: They raised a distinct privacy concern that expanding USCDI to include sensitive PII (like family relationships) could be risky when shared via Patient Access APIs to non-HIPAA-covered third-party apps, as noted by AHIP.
Proposed solutions: Extend USCDI update intervals to a three-year cycle, require evidence-based rationale for additions, and prioritize adding USCDI+Quality elements to the baseline.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Variability in implementation by providers and EHRs means that even when USCDI is supported in theory, the data is not consistently available in practice.
specificPoints: As intermediaries, they see the real-world data flows (or lack thereof) and can contrast the standard's intent with on-the-ground reality.
Proposed solutions: Mandate broader implementation and activation of USCDI sharing by providers, not just certification by vendors; use HIEs to help normalize and exchange new USCDI+ data classes.

HealthShare Exchange (HSX) notes that while USCDI is comprehensive, 'variability in data-items received from healthcare providers' is a major issue, and the standard is 'not yet fully implemented by EHR vendors and healthcare providers'.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Data-backed Observation of Failure: Flexpa's `State of the Payer Patient Access API Report` provides damning, quantitative evidence that theoretical compliance with USCDI is not translating to functional, real-world interoperability. Their finding that nearly half of payers have a broken endpoint for coverage data shows the gap between regulation and reality is a chasm.

Insider Critique of the Process: A commenter identifying as the lead editor for the US Core Implementation Guide for over 10 years provides a rare and powerful critique from within the standards development community. Their assertion that the USCDI expansion process has become policy-driven rather than evidence-based, leading to under-defined elements, is a crucial insight into *why* implementation may be so inconsistent.

The 'Floor vs. Ceiling' Dilemma: Multiple commenters powerfully articulated that many vendors and data holders treat the USCDI minimum as a 'ceiling' for data sharing, not a 'floor' to build upon. This creates a compliance-driven mindset that actively suppresses the sharing of valuable data that falls just outside the standard, even when permitted.

Unintended Consequence as a Weapon: A physician describes how an EMR company is exploiting the limited scope of USCDI v1, using it as a justification under the 'Manner Exception' to refuse to provide a patient's full medical record. This shows how a 'floor' can be perversely used as a 'ceiling' to block information access.

The Specialty Provider's 'Inequity': Ensora Health, a vendor for behavioral health, provided a unique and compelling perspective, arguing that the one-size-fits-all USCDI forces their clients to subsidize the data needs of medical/surgical care. Their quote, "In essence, you’re asking a community behavioral health center to subsidize ventilator settings for ICU patients. That's not interoperability, that’s inequity," crystallizes the argument for modularity.

Patient-Level Granularity of Missing Data: Patient advocate Grace Cordovano provided an exceptionally detailed list of missing data from a patient/caregiver perspective, including 'tumor board notes,' 'chemotherapy infusion notes,' and the physical location of pathology samples. This moves the conversation from abstract data classes to tangible, critical information needed to navigate care.

USCDI as a 'Data Dump' vs. a 'Transactional Standard': Findhelp draws a critical distinction: USCDI is good for documenting that an event *happened* (a diagnosis was made), but it is not designed to support the transactional workflows needed for care, like scheduling a referral. This clarifies why simply adding more data elements may not solve core workflow interoperability problems.

AI as Both a Problem and Solution: A recurring insight was that the rise of AI is driving the need for more comprehensive data *beyond* USCDI. At the same time, several commenters suggested that AI/NLP could be part of the *solution* for making unstructured data (like notes) accessible and usable, complementing the structured USCDI approach.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Implementer vs. Consumer Tension: A clear pattern emerges where the 'consumers' of data (patients, app developers, researchers, public health) are unified in their call for 'more data, faster.' In contrast, the 'implementers' (EHR vendors, providers, payers) are unified in their call for 'a more manageable, predictable, and less burdensome process.' This fundamental conflict is the central challenge for policymakers.

Specialty vs. Generalist Divide Fuels Modularity Push: Views differ significantly based on the scope of practice. Generalist EHR vendors focus on managing complexity, while specialty groups (oncology, dental, occupational therapy, behavioral health, post-acute care) are adamant that the 'one-size-fits-all' approach is deeply flawed, imposing irrelevant requirements while failing to include the data most critical to their patients. This divide is the primary driver behind the strong push for a modular framework.

The Problem has Matured from 'What' to 'How Well' and 'If at All': The commentary shows a mature evolution in the interoperability discussion. Early critiques focused on *what* data should be shared. The dominant concerns now are about *how well* it is implemented (inconsistently, with poor quality) and *if* it is being implemented at all in the real world (the gap between certification and reality).

Administrative Data is a Major Blind Spot: A recurring theme, particularly from payers and clearinghouses, is that USCDI's clinical focus completely excludes critical administrative data (e.g., prior authorization status, claims details, scheduling) that is essential for operational workflows and creating a complete patient picture.

keyQuotations.quote: [USCDI's] real-world impact on app builders has been inconsistent due to gaps in format and adoption. It sets the floor but not the ceiling for what’s needed to build understandable, supportive, and timely care tools.

Many payers have implemented Patient Access APIs that claim USCDI compliance while omitting core data elements or implementing them in non-standard ways. This selective implementation creates the illusion of standardization while perpetuating the data fragmentation that USCDI was designed to address.

As the lead editor of US Core over 10 years... the process for expanding USCDI has increasingly prioritized policy over whether health information systems capture a data element, and whether its exchange has been empirically proven to benefit patient care. As a result, the USCDI data elements are underdefined and require interpretation at the specification level.

Data holders treat USCDI as a ceiling, not a floor. The moment an element is absent from the enumerated list, many providers and payers simply omit it - even when the Cures Act’s ‘all EHI’ clause allows, and patients expect, broader sharing.
Full EHI is junk. That’s the kindest four-letter word I can use to describe it. USCDI is standardized and understandable outside of a proprietary context.
The reliance on ICD, CPT, and DRG codes leaves no structured space for capturing patient goals, relationship continuity, or the nuanced behavioral insights that nurses routinely generate.

USCDI cannot be a monolith for both FHIR API and C-CDA criteria... The scope of USCDI (v3 or v4) is broader than many HIT products can support organically. Requiring the full USCDI is inappropriate for specialty EHR developers (e.g., physical therapists, mental health specialists).
In essence, you’re asking a community behavioral health center to subsidize ventilator settings for ICU patients. That's not interoperability, that’s inequity.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Ensora Health, Health IT Vendor for Behavioral Health

keyQuotations.quote: A reliable core of standardized data exchange must be established and functioning consistently before attention is turned to expanding into other data areas or more complex interoperability challenges.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Federation of American Hospitals (FAH), Trade Association

keyQuotations.quote: Occupational Therapy and the rehab professionals are about how well people LIVE, what they do, the quality of their life. Cognition, Mobility, Being Pain Free, Engagement, Confidence, Satisfaction, Lack of Fear, Adaptability: please add these factors into your tech processes.

Given that laboratory data comprise 90% of clinical records and support 80% of diagnosis we strongly reiterate our requests for the inclusion of the USCDI Data Element Test Kit Unique Identifier and the USCDI Data Element Instrument Unique Identifier in USCDIv6.
An EMR company used the 'Manner Exception' to refuse providing a full medical record, citing only the need to provide content required by USCDI v1.

Significant gaps remain in the representation of rich, contextual clinical information. Key data types such as medication administration, longitudinal care plans, social risk factors, and narrative clinical notes are either missing or inadequately defined.

The primary limitation of USCDI is that it is not designed to support the transactional layer of interoperability required for actual care pathways. It documents an event but does not standardize the real-time, bidirectional communication needed for workflows like scheduling or referrals.

More than 50% of relevant health data—including patient-reported outcomes, side effects, genomics, wearables, mental health, dental, physical therapy, and social determinants of health—is either scattered or uncaptured altogether.
The AHA remains concerned with the aggressive timeline for transitioning to USCDI v4 requirements...This aggressive timeline may put smaller hospitals at risk for noncompliance.
Expand USCDI to include 'where pathology samples are being stored... 'how much viable tissue is available', and 'who to contact to request pathology samples, slides, records'.
isolating certain data classes to one use case may limit the impact and create additional burdens.
keyQuotations.sourceType: The Joint Commission, Healthcare Accreditation Body

sections.executiveSummary: There is overwhelming, cross-stakeholder consensus that the lack of harmonized clinical and administrative terminology is a critical barrier to safe, efficient, and innovative healthcare. The current state of "semantic chaos"—where data is exchanged without consistent meaning—undermines clinical care, burdens providers, stifles the potential of AI and analytics, and creates immense administrative waste. While all stakeholders agree on the problem, debates emerge on the implementation approach. The dominant recommendation is for CMS and ONC to take a more forceful, prescriptive role by mandating foundational standards like SNOMED CT, LOINC, and RxNorm and, most significantly, establishing and requiring the use of a single, authoritative National Terminology Service to create a non-negotiable semantic foundation for the entire U.S. healthcare ecosystem. A clear tension exists between practitioners seeking immediate fixes for foundational problems and technology visionaries urging a leap forward to more advanced frameworks like formal ontologies and openEHR to build a future-proof, AI-ready system.

consensusPoints.text: Lack of Semantic Interoperability is a Core Systemic Failure - Virtually all commenters agree that the absence of standardized clinical and administrative vocabularies is a primary obstacle to progress. They describe a system where data may be structurally exchanged (e.g., via FHIR) but lacks consistent meaning, a condition described as "semantic chaos" or "semantic scatter." This renders the data unusable for analytics, AI, or even basic clinical continuity and is a universal pain point across all stakeholder types.

Strong Support for Foundational Code Sets (SNOMED CT, LOINC, RxNorm) - A strong majority of commenters explicitly endorse the broader and more consistent adoption of established terminologies like SNOMED CT for clinical findings, LOINC for labs and observations, and RxNorm for medications. These are seen as the non-negotiable building blocks for semantic interoperability that CMS should promote, incentivize, or mandate across all systems.

exceptions.text: Some commenters, while not opposing these standards, argue they are insufficient for their specific domains (e.g., nephrology, dentistry, wound care, EMS) and require extensions or complementary standards.

consensusPoints.text: Federal Government Must Lead and Enforce Standards - A vast majority of commenters believe that market forces alone have failed to produce semantic interoperability and that CMS and ONC must take a stronger, more prescriptive leadership role. This includes defining standards, mandating them through certification and payment rules, and establishing governance. The continued use of local, proprietary codes by vendors is frequently cited as a key driver of fragmentation that undermines national standards and can only be solved through federal action.

exceptions.text: A lone commenter suggests that industry can adopt standards effectively without direct government intervention, citing the history of HL7.
