To become viable, commenters overwhelmingly urge the government to accelerate the transition to FHIR, urgently expand and mandate use cases for payers, and provide substantial financial and technical support to lower prohibitive adoption barriers.

consensusPoints.text: A vast majority of commenters across all stakeholder groups—including technology vendors, providers, payers, and associations—view TEFCA as the most credible, necessary, or best opportunity for achieving nationwide data exchange under a unified trust agreement. However, there is a shared sentiment that its real-world impact is minimal to date. Commenters frequently describe its value as "nascent," "theoretical," or "not yet fully realized," citing low participation, limited exchange volumes, and a disconnect between the framework's launch and tangible benefits. For example, a Business/QHIN commenter stated, "TEFCA is the backbone of equitable, national-scale data exchange". A provider noted that TEFCA presents an "opportunity to inaugurate a single, interoperable national framework". Yet, a Tech Vendor commented that "TEFCA’s potential for widespread patient and provider access remains largely unrealized", and another provider noted, "TEFCA’s value is still theoretical for Terros Health patients".

A powerful consensus among commenters, particularly technology vendors, payers, and health IT experts, is that TEFCA's reliance on older, document-based exchange (C-CDA, IHE/SOAP) is a major limitation. There is a universal call for an accelerated, mandatory transition to modern, API-based HL7 FHIR exchange to align with modern standards, reduce complexity, enable more granular and innovative use cases, and support analytics, value-based care, and AI. As AHIP urges, ASTP should "phase out document-based exchange (e.g., IHE) and require FHIR API exchange as soon as practicable". A vendor notes that "Only when FHIR-based exchange is required for TEFCA exchange will all participating entities fully transition away from document-based exchange". Another warns that the current architecture "lacks support for FHIR write operations, bulk data access, and cohort-level queries, which are essential".

An overwhelming chorus of stakeholders who address patient access agree that the IAS Exchange Purpose, a key promise of TEFCA, is currently failing in practice. They cite a combination of providers' fear of HIPAA liability for inadvertent disclosure (crippling patient matching), technical barriers, high participation costs for consumer apps, and inconsistent implementation by QHINs as reasons why patients are not yet benefiting from a "single on-ramp" to their data. One commenter states, "The promise of IAS in TEFCA is unrealized... Very few patients or Personal Health Records (PHRs) can use IAS successfully due to 'patient matching' issues". CRISP Shared Services states that IAS is the one "transformative capability" of TEFCA, but its implementation is so weak that the rest of the framework largely duplicates existing networks.

An overwhelming majority of commenters state that TEFCA's current mandatory Exchange Purposes (Treatment and IAS) are too limited to drive widespread adoption. There is a powerful chorus calling for the rapid expansion to include Payment, Healthcare Operations, Public Health, and Public Benefits Determination to make the network viable, sustainable, and valuable for essential stakeholders, especially payers. For example, The Cigna Group urges officials to "implement the Payment and Healthcare Operations Exchange Purposes (XPs) within TEFCA as soon as possible". NOSSCR calls for a "public benefits use case" to streamline disability determinations.

Across nearly all stakeholder groups, there is widespread agreement that the cost, complexity, and technical requirements for joining TEFCA are significant barriers, particularly for smaller providers, rural facilities, behavioral health, long-term care facilities, and safety-net clinics. Commenters warn this is creating a "digital divide" and undermining the goal of a truly nationwide network. For example, one comment states, "High costs for participation, integration, and maintenance remain a barrier for TEFCA adoption, especially for rural and smaller providers". A PACE program states HIE/QHIN access is "prohibitively expensive...costing up to $75,000 annually".

areasOfDebate.topic: Participation in TEFCA: Mandatory vs. Voluntary
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether to mandate participation to achieve critical mass and universal connectivity or to keep it voluntary to avoid imposing undue burdens, preserve choice, and mitigate risks.

positions.label: Mandatory Participation
positions.stance: Mandate TEFCA participation, especially for payers, labs, and providers receiving federal funds, to achieve critical mass and universal connectivity.
positions.supportLevel: A strong contingent of tech vendors, QHINs, payers, and some providers.
keyArguments: The voluntary model is the primary policy limitation and has failed to achieve network effects
mandates are necessary to drive both supply of and demand for data
universal connectivity is a patient safety imperative.

positions.label: Voluntary Participation
positions.stance: Keep TEFCA participation voluntary to avoid imposing undue burdens, preserve choice, and mitigate risks.
positions.supportLevel: Major physician associations, some medical groups, and privacy-focused organizations.
keyArguments: Mandates would be burdensome, especially for smaller practices
trust and value should drive adoption, not coercion
mandating a single framework introduces unacceptable risk in the event of a breach and could stifle innovation from competing models.

areasOfDebate.topic: TEFCA's Fundamental Architecture: Improve the QHIN Model vs. Abandon It
areasOfDebate.description: The debate focuses on whether TEFCA's QHIN-based "network of networks" is the right approach that needs improvement, or if the architecture is fundamentally flawed and should be replaced with a more direct or patient-centered model.

positions.label: Improve the QHIN Model
positions.stance: TEFCA's "network of networks" brokered by QHINs is the right approach but needs significant improvement, such as mandates, expanded use cases, and better technology.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of commenters who see TEFCA as the national strategy.
keyArguments: Simplifies connectivity by allowing a "connect once" model
establishes a unified trust framework and governance
leverages existing infrastructure
the federal government has already invested heavily in this path.

positions.label: Abandon/Radically Change the QHIN Model
positions.stance: The QHIN architecture is fundamentally flawed—a complex, anti-competitive "scatter model" that creates unnecessary, costly intermediaries and serves institutional interests over patients.
positions.supportLevel: A vocal and highly critical minority, including technology critics, patient advocates, and some HIEs.
keyArguments: QHINs add useless cost, complexity, and security risks
the model favors and entrenches large, oligopolistic vendors
it contravenes the Cures Act's goal of patient control and should be replaced with a decentralized model based on direct FHIR APIs or a "centered model" around the patient.

areasOfDebate.topic: Role of Existing Networks: National Utility vs. Complementary Framework
areasOfDebate.description: This debate considers whether TEFCA should become the single national utility, replacing other networks, or if it should act as a complementary framework that coexists with specialized regional and private networks.

positions.label: TEFCA as National Utility
positions.stance: TEFCA should be the single national framework, consolidating or deprecating redundant networks like Carequality, CommonWell, and local HIEs to reduce fragmentation, cost, and confusion.
positions.supportLevel: Several tech vendors, QHINs, and large EHR vendors.
keyArguments: Multiple networks create redundant costs and overlapping governance
a single backbone is more efficient
TEFCA is uniquely designed to replace legacy frameworks with a government-backed trust agreement.

positions.label: TEFCA as Complementary Framework
positions.stance: Existing networks (especially HIEs) should coexist with and complement TEFCA, as they serve different, more specialized, or more effective use cases (e.g., local needs, push notifications, higher data quality).
positions.supportLevel: Many HIEs/HDUs, clearinghouses, and some tech vendors.
keyArguments: HIEs handle local governance, nuanced consent, and critical services like ADT alerts better than TEFCA
a one-size-fits-all model misses nuance and destroys existing, functional infrastructure
TEFCA should fill gaps, not replace what works.

areasOfDebate.topic: Core Strategy: Mandate & Expand vs. Focus & Incentivize
areasOfDebate.description: The strategic debate is whether to drive adoption by mandating participation and broadly expanding TEFCA's use cases, or to first focus on perfecting the core functions of Treatment and Individual Access with incentives.

positions.label: Mandate & Expand
positions.stance: Require participation and broaden TEFCA's scope to cover more use cases (payment, operations, public health) to create a sustainable business model and solve more problems simultaneously.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from many providers, payers, and integrated tech firms.
keyArguments: Voluntary participation has failed
interoperability cannot be solved piecemeal
adding payer and public health use cases is essential for value-based care.

positions.label: Focus & Incentivize
positions.stance: Keep TEFCA "laser-focused" on its core functions of Treatment and Individual Access, fix those first, and let the market solve other use cases.
positions.supportLevel: A significant minority, including some specialized tech vendors and industry analysts.
keyArguments: Expanding TEFCA now will dilute focus, increase complexity, and doom the entire project
trying to support contentious payer use cases will alienate providers and stall adoption.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: TEFCA provides little to no value or ROI in its current provider-centric state; it lacks mandatory use cases for Payment and Health Care Operations; it creates non-reciprocal data sharing obligations; providers can charge prohibitive fees for data.
specificPoints: They experience a lack of reciprocity, being required to respond to queries while provider responses to their queries are often voluntary or nonexistent. They feel their essential use cases for value-based care are an afterthought.

Immediately prioritize and mandate Payment and Health Care Operations as required Exchange Purposes; prohibit providers from charging fees for data exchange; accelerate the move to FHIR; establish clear, enforceable reciprocal data sharing rules.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The prohibitive cost, complexity, and lack of a clear return on investment for participating; potential for payers to use data for adverse coverage determinations; liability concerns; administrative burden of connecting to a duplicative framework.
specificPoints: They are on the front lines, bearing the financial and workflow burdens of implementation. Small, rural, and specialty providers (behavioral health, long-term care) are especially concerned about being left behind.

Provide direct financial incentives, grants, and technical assistance for onboarding; keep participation voluntary but link to VBC incentives; ensure strong privacy guardrails and clear liability protections.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The voluntary nature of TEFCA, which limits network effects and business cases; slow technical convergence on FHIR; administrative friction and inefficient onboarding processes; inconsistent requirements imposed by different QHINs.
specificPoints: As the builders of the infrastructure, they provide the most detailed technical critiques. QHINs, in particular, see themselves as essential and advocate for policies that drive volume and sustainability. Some vendors express fear of QHINs run by competitors acting as "chokepoints."
Mandate TEFCA participation and tie it to CMS programs; accelerate the FHIR roadmap; expand and mandate Exchange Purposes; streamline and automate QHIN onboarding, vetting, and oversight.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient access through TEFCA (Individual Access Services) is a failure in practice; the framework is opaque, lacks meaningful patient control, and was not designed for patients; the "pay to play" model disadvantages consumer apps and widens the digital divide; governance is dominated by industry, not patients.
specificPoints: They bring the direct experience of being unable to access their own data and highlight the complete lack of patient awareness of TEFCA's existence. They view TEFCA through a rights and privacy lens.

Prioritize fixing IAS; mandate that consumer apps cannot be charged to participate; build in robust, user-friendly patient controls (e.g., audit logs, granular consent); give patients a stronger voice in governance.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: TEFCA duplicates their existing, functional infrastructure and drains resources; the one-size-fits-all national approach ignores local needs, governance, and innovation; lack of a clear role or funding for HIEs as on-ramps to TEFCA.
specificPoints: As operators of existing networks, they have deep concern about being rendered obsolete by a duplicative federal framework. They argue they provide essential "last-mile" services (like ADT alerts) that TEFCA lacks.
Fund HIEs to serve as TEFCA on-ramps; recognize state/regional networks as valid complements; design TEFCA to fill "white space" rather than replace existing, functional exchanges.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The most provocative critique reframes the entire initiative: "TEFCA is information blocking to prevent a modern digital health economy. TEFCA’s brokers limit access, TEFCA’s IHE protocols limit exchange and TEFCA’s document payloads limit use." This came from a Health IT Company.

Anna McCollister articulates the central paradox crippling Individual Access Services (IAS). Providers solve patient matching for other providers daily, but the "OCR stance on inadvertent data release" creates a chilling effect that stops them from doing the same for patients, rendering a key TEFCA feature inert. This highlights a critical policy conflict, not a technical one, as noted by The Sequoia Project Workgroup.

A health IT COO from Metriport Inc. points out that "providers operating direct-pay or cash-based models are categorically prohibited from Treatment-based exchange" under TEFCA rules, as they don't have a "treatment relationship" in the same way. This creates a new barrier to care coordination that didn't exist before.

A patient advocate from PFCCpartners provides a stunning insight: "I was unaware of TEFCA and had to use ChatGPT to understand it... None of the 14 PFAnetwork members directly mentioned TEFCA." This demonstrates the framework is completely invisible to the people it's meant to serve.

The Indiana HIE argues that TEFCA's announcement in 2016 "diverted attention and resources for innovation away from... private sector networks and slowed the development of new interoperability functions," suggesting the promise of TEFCA may have paradoxically hindered progress.

Multiple commenters, including Particle Health and Selfiie, raise the alarm that QHINs, particularly those operated by dominant EHR vendors, can act as "chokepoints" or "gatekeepers," controlling data flow and extending "EHR wars" into the new framework, thereby stifling competition.

CommonWell Health Alliance, a QHIN, makes a compelling case that TEFCA's greatest strength is governmental. The presence of ONC as a "non-conflicted arbiter" to resolve disputes between competitive entities is a critical governance function that private networks lack.

Commenters provide rare, specific pricing details, such as a minimum QHIN spend of over "$10,000 per month" or a "$75,000 annually" fee for a small program, making the abstract concern of "cost" a concrete, impossible barrier.

Gary L Dickinson of EHR Standards Consulting provides a radical critique, arguing TEFCA's entire "scattered" network-of-networks model is an intractable problem. He proposes abandoning it for a "centered" model built around a patient-controlled record, reframing the debate from "how to improve TEFCA" to "why are we building TEFCA at all?"

Citizen Health Inc. provides a specific, alarming example of how TEFCA's promise can be broken: "One influential QHIN suggests that IAS requests through TEFCA should only return FHIR endpoints... not the actual records," completely defeating the purpose of a single on-ramp.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Facility-type Patterns: A clear digital divide emerges. Large, integrated health systems are optimistic early adopters, advocating for expansion. In contrast, smaller practices, rural/safety-net providers, and post-acute/specialty clinics are overwhelmed by prohibitive costs and complexity, and are far more skeptical.

Payer-Provider Asymmetry: A clear pattern of tension exists where payers feel TEFCA is designed for and by providers, with non-reciprocal data sharing obligations and fee structures that disadvantage them. Providers, in turn, express concern about payers misusing their clinical data.

HIEs vs. QHINs Tension: A persistent tension exists between state/regional HIEs and the national TEFCA framework. HIEs argue they provide essential, complementary services and are being marginalized by TEFCA's top-down, one-size-fits-all, EHR-centric design.

"Insider" vs. "Outsider" Perspectives: There is a clear divide between commenters who are designated QHINs or their partners ("insiders") and those who are not ("outsiders"). Insiders support the model but are frustrated by slow adoption. Outsiders are deeply critical of the fundamental architecture, seeing it as an anti-competitive "club."

Unintended Consequences: Multiple commenters identify the risk of TEFCA inadvertently concentrating power in the RCE and QHINs, extending "information monopolies," and creating new barriers, such as excluding direct-pay physicians from exchange.

Provider-Centric Design Flaw: A pattern emerges from payers, patient advocates, and others that TEFCA was fundamentally designed for provider-to-provider exchange, resulting in other key stakeholders feeling their use cases are afterthoughts, poorly supported, and not prioritized.

keyQuotations.quote: TEFCA is information blocking to prevent a modern digital health economy. TEFCA’s brokers limit access, TEFCA’s IHE protocols limit exchange and TEFCA’s document payloads limit use.
The Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA) currently treats patients as passive subjects of data exchange, not active participants.
[TEFCA's] voluntary participation model... is the primary policy limitation of TEFCA.
Health plan participation in TEFCA has been hampered by limited use cases, cumbersome policies, and outdated technical standards.
The promise of IAS in TEFCA is unrealized... Very few patients or Personal Health Records (PHRs) can use IAS successfully due to 'patient matching' issues.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Anna McCollister, The Sequoia Project's Consumer Engagement Strategy Workgroup

keyQuotations.quote: As a first-year PACE program serving ~100 participants, we face an impossible choice... Pay up to $75,000 annually for HIE/QHIN access, which is unjustifiable at our scale. This pricing structure creates care disparities.
TEFCA is a prime example of how the US Government has demanded, effectively enabled and enforced the separation of individuals and their health information.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Gary L Dickinson, EHR Standards Consulting

keyQuotations.quote: With the exception of Individual Access, TEFCA has thus far duplicated existing frameworks and networks, increasing overall administrative burden without providing additional value...

The announcement of TEFCA in 2016 somewhat diverted attention and resources for innovation away from not-for-profit and private sector networks and slowed the development of new interoperability functions...
Information blocking by large health IT entities creates information monopolies... with QHINs acting as chokepoints that can control data flow and limit competition.
I was unaware of TEFCA and had to use ChatGPT to understand it...This raises questions about whether patients know if their providers participate in TEFCA or HIEs.

Any attempt by CMS or ONC to regulate the architecture of shared networks now risks solidifying outdated paradigms. It will freeze innovation and entrench TEFCA, FHIR, and HL7—legacy standards that cannot support the performance needs of modern value-based care.

[A patient] travels from Ohio to Florida for the winter... has a critical care event, and their primary care team is not notified for weeks or months until a claim appears. In this value-based world, this fragmented system results in a failure for our patients and our care teams.
Universal connectivity is not just a technical goal, it is a patient safety imperative and the foundation for the innovative, patient-centered healthcare system CMS envisions.

2.2.themeDescription: Establishing a Nationwide Provider and Endpoint Directory

sections.executiveSummary:
Public input reveals a virtually unanimous consensus on the critical need for a centralized, accurate, and publicly accessible national provider and endpoint directory, viewing it as a long-overdue piece of infrastructure essential for enabling scalable interoperability. This support is fueled by universal frustration with the profound inaccuracy of current directories, which leads to patient harm, "ghost networks," and billions in administrative waste.

While the need for a directory is undisputed, key debates center on how to implement it. Commenters are divided on governance models (federal control vs. public-private partnership) and funding mechanisms (free public good vs. tiered-fee structures). However, a sophisticated consensus is emerging around a federally-led, federated architectural model that would serve as a "directory of directories," connecting to and leveraging existing, trusted data sources rather than building a monolithic government database.

Across all stakeholders, there is an adamant insistence that any new system must reduce provider administrative burden by consolidating and replacing duplicative reporting requirements. The dominant thrust of recommendations calls for a phased implementation of a free, API-first directory that builds on existing infrastructure (like CAQH and TEFCA), has strong data governance, and ultimately functions as a foundational trust and identity framework for the entire digital health ecosystem.

consensusPoints.text: There is virtually unanimous agreement across all stakeholder types—including providers, payers, technology vendors, advocacy groups, and patients—that a centralized, accurate, and publicly accessible national directory of provider FHIR endpoints is a foundational and essential piece of national health IT infrastructure. A strong majority of commenters characterize the current lack of a directory as a fundamental barrier to scalable interoperability, often using the analogy that it is as critical to FHIR as DNS was to the internet. Quantitative analysis of one batch of comments showed direct support from at least 61 of 64 commenters.

exceptions.text: The Federation of American Hospitals (FAH) questioned if a directory would be redundant if TEFCA achieves its goals. MRO Corporation expressed significant security concerns that a directory could enable PHI phishing, suggesting authentication is a more critical issue.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters, particularly payers, providers, and patient-facing technology companies, cite the profound inaccuracy and fragmentation of existing provider directories as a major crisis. This is seen as the root cause of patient frustration, "ghost networks," care delays, administrative waste, and operational failure. Over half of all commenters specifically cited data inaccuracy as a primary driver for creating a national directory, providing striking data and real-world examples.

An overwhelming majority of commenters, led by provider associations like the AMA, AHA, and MGMA, stress that a national directory must not create new administrative burdens. They strongly advocate that it should consolidate duplicative data requests from numerous payers and replace, not supplement, existing reporting requirements. The vision is for a single, "one-and-done" update mechanism where providers can update their information once and have it populate everywhere.

areasOfDebate.topic: Directory Governance
areasOfDebate.description: Who should run the national provider directory?

positions.label: Federal Stewardship
positions.stance: A federal entity, such as CMS or ONC, should publish, maintain, and govern the directory as a neutral, authoritative public utility.
positions.supportLevel: A plurality of commenters, particularly technology vendors and advocacy groups.
keyArguments: Ensures neutrality and avoids commercial interests or vendor lock-in
CMS/ONC has the regulatory authority to mandate participation and ensure standards
Aligns with other federal data initiatives like NPPES.

positions.label: Public-Private Partnership
positions.stance: The directory should be a collaborative effort between the government and private sector entities.
positions.supportLevel: A significant portion of commenters, especially large trade associations, health plans, and some technology firms.
keyArguments: Leverages existing private-sector infrastructure and expertise (e.g., CAQH, DirectTrust) to accelerate development
Can be more agile and responsive to market needs
Avoids creating a large government-run operation.

positions.label: Federated Model
positions.stance: A national directory should be a "network of networks" or "directory of directories," building upon and connecting existing frameworks like TEFCA, QHINs, state/regional HIEs, and private utilities.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from standards bodies, interoperability networks, and many large providers and EHR vendors.
keyArguments: Avoids creating a single, monolithic federal system
Leverages trusted local infrastructure and relationships
Builds on established trust frameworks and governance.

areasOfDebate.topic: Directory Funding
areasOfDebate.description: Who should pay for the national provider directory?

positions.label: Public Good/Free Access
positions.stance: Basic access to the directory should be free, funded by the federal government as core infrastructure.
positions.supportLevel: A vast majority of commenters, especially technology vendors, innovators, provider groups, and public health advocates.
keyArguments: Maximizes adoption and equity, removes barriers to entry for startups and small practices, and stimulates broad innovation. The cost is a public investment that will be offset by system-wide savings.

positions.label: User Fees/Tiered Model
positions.stance: Users who benefit commercially from the directory should contribute to its cost through fees, often in a hybrid model.
positions.supportLevel: A notable minority, primarily technology vendors and some public-private partnership proponents.
keyArguments: Ensures long-term financial sustainability without relying solely on federal appropriations. Many propose hybrid models where basic access is free but fees are charged for high-volume commercial use or premium features (e.g., real-time analytics).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Endpoint discoverability and lack of a single source of provider data is the single biggest technical barrier to scalable interoperability; fragmented, proprietary directories stifle innovation and competition.
specificPoints: Emphasize the need for the directory to be machine-readable, API-first, and publicly accessible to unlock the value of prior legislative investments in FHIR. Many presented their existing products as blueprints or solutions for a national directory.

Strongly advocate for a federally-led, free or low-cost directory. They provide the most detailed lists of required technical metadata, recommend using AI for data hygiene, and often favor a tiered funding model for sustainability.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The overwhelming risk of being saddled with yet another administrative reporting burden; the negative impact of inaccurate data ("ghost networks") on patient trust and access to care.
specificPoints: A pragmatic focus on the practical workflow challenges of updating information across dozens of different systems. They insist that the individual clinician is often not the right person to manage this data; the practice/organization is.
The directory must be "burden-free", consolidate existing requirements, have an easy-to-use update portal, and leverage existing tools they already use, such as CAQH for credentialing.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Administrative waste and cost of maintaining duplicative, inaccurate provider directories; compliance risks and member dissatisfaction.
specificPoints: Frame the directory as a tool to streamline credentialing, network management, and claims processing. While strongly supportive, they highlight the "monumental effort" required and the complexities of state-specific requirements.

Favor a public-private partnership or federated model to ensure the directory meets their business needs. They advocate for a "safe harbor" from accuracy penalties if they use the national directory in good faith.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring the directory is built on open standards (FHIR), has strong governance, empowers patients, and integrates with existing national frameworks like TEFCA.
specificPoints: Take a broad, ecosystem-level view, often bridging the technical details with policy goals. They champion robust data content, including accessibility and languages spoken, and advocate for expanding the directory to include social service organizations.
Often recommend a public-private collaboration with strong federal oversight, using specific Implementation Guides (e.g., HL7 VhDir), and a phased implementation approach.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The real-world struggle and frustration of finding an in-network provider who is accepting new patients and has the right specialty.
specificPoints: They provide powerful, personal anecdotes that ground the technical discussion in human impact. Their requests are simple and direct: make it easy to use, make it accurate, and make it free.
A single, searchable website as easy to use as medicare.gov.

noteworthyInsights.insight: "DNS for Healthcare" Analogy: Several technically-focused commenters (The FHIR Department; AEGIS.net) powerfully articulated the need by comparing the directory to the Domain Name System (DNS) for the internet. This crystallizes its role as essential, background infrastructure that translates a human-readable name (Provider NPI) into a machine-routable address (FHIR Endpoint URL), making the entire system work seamlessly.

"DNS for Healthcare" Analogy: Several technically-focused commenters (The FHIR Department; AEGIS.net) powerfully articulated the need by comparing the directory to the Domain Name System (DNS) for the internet. This crystallizes its role as essential, background infrastructure that translates a human-readable name (Provider NPI) into a machine-routable address (FHIR Endpoint URL), making the entire system work seamlessly.

The Directory is an Identity Challenge: A recurring insight, articulated most clearly by Cognitive Medical Systems and Professional Credentials Exchange, is that this is "fundamentally an identity challenge." The goal is not just to list an address, but to reliably verify who a provider is and what they are qualified and authorized to do, elevating it from a lookup service to a foundational trust infrastructure.

Federated Governance Model: Epic proposed a highly sophisticated model: CMS should own the core identity (NPI via a reformed NPPES), but contextual data should be owned and updated by its authoritative source (e.g., payers attest to network status, providers attest to practice location). This federated approach with unambiguous data stewards presents a viable path through the "monolithic database" problem.

The Compelling Failure of the Status Quo: The secret shopper analysis from Zocdoc showing a 96.4% failure rate for finding and booking an appointment through Medicare Compare is a powerful, data-backed indictment of the current system's usability for beneficiaries.

Incentivizing Accuracy Through Payment: Availity provided a critical insight: mandates to update directories often fail because there is no tangible incentive for providers. They argue that the only way to ensure provider engagement and data accuracy is to directly tie the directory update process to core financial transactions like claims adjudication.

The Human Cost of Inaccuracy: Multiple commenters provided visceral accounts of how directory failures aren't just an administrative hassle. Imagine Pediatrics described how they directly cause vulnerable patients to be "lost to care," while a social worker (Hera) described making 20 phone calls just to find an in-home physical therapist.

Directory as "Accountability Infrastructure": One individual commenter (James Cummings) reframed the directory not just as technical infrastructure, but as "accountability infrastructure." Without a public, authoritative list of endpoints, it is impossible to enforce existing interoperability mandates.

Expanding the Directory's Scope: Several commenters (Pair Team; NACHC) advocated for expanding the "provider" directory to include Community-Based Organizations (CBOs) and other social service entities, creating a unified resource for both clinical and non-clinical care coordination.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Federated Model as the Preferred Path: While a "centralized directory" is the common term, the emerging pattern is a strong preference for a federated architectural model over a single, monolithic, government-built database. This model leverages distributed data stewardship while providing a unified point of access, representing a sophisticated consensus on how to balance authority with practicality.

The Consolidation Imperative: Leverage, Don't Rebuild: Across nearly all stakeholder groups, there is a strong pattern of demanding that this initiative consolidate and simplify the landscape. The call is to build upon existing, proven infrastructure (e.g., TEFCA, CAQH, state/regional HIEs) rather than starting from scratch. The fear that this will become "just one more directory" is a powerful, unifying sentiment.

Evolution from "Phone Book" to "GPS" and Navigation Tool: The prevailing analogy evolved in the comments. While many initially described it as a simple "phone book" or "DNS" for healthcare APIs, more sophisticated comments reframed it as a "GPS for health data" and a patient navigation tool, implying a more dynamic system with richer data (quality, scheduling, reviews) to empower consumer choice.

Focus on Organizational vs. Individual Endpoints: A sophisticated pattern emerged, particularly from payers and large network operators (BCBSA, CommonWell), clarifying that endpoints are typically associated with organizations (practices, hospitals, health systems) rather than individual clinicians. The directory's data model must accurately reflect these complex hierarchical relationships.

Unintended Consequences of Poor Data: Multiple commenters connected the dots between inaccurate provider directories and a cascade of negative downstream effects: frustrated patients, delayed care, surprise bills, flawed network adequacy analysis, and increased risk of fraud, waste, and abuse.

keyQuotations.quote: [A national directory is] as essential to FHIR/Interoperability as DNS was to making the World Wide Web viable.

The single greatest practical impediment to patient data access is the inability to reliably locate the correct Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR®) Application Programming Interface (API) endpoint.
Our own secret shopper analysis of Medicare Compare revealed a dismal 3.6% success rate in booking a new patient appointment, meaning the current tool fails seniors more than 96% of the time.
Work on provider directories must reduce—not contribute to—provider reporting burden.

The United States lacks a single, reliable source of truth for the most basic information about physicians... This causes significant problems throughout the ecosystem—patients can be referred to the wrong physician location and insurers can inadvertently deny legitimate claims for payment from physicians whose information is out of date.
Mapping a provider to one or more endpoints is not just a directory challenge it’s fundamentally an identity challenge.
CMS must treat the NPD not merely as infrastructure, but as accountability infrastructure. If a provider cannot be digitally reached, the system fails.
Care coordinators report that provider directories are 'more often wrong than right'.

If we want to shift more patients to age in place and in community, we have to build a more interoperable database for providers caring for patients in community, to make 'finding me an in home physical therapist for zip code 10001 that takes Original Medicare' a click of a button vs 20 phone calls.

Patients often struggle to find providers who not only accept their insurance benefits but also accept new patients. Dental payer-based provider directory APIs frequently contain outdated or incomplete data, resulting in 'ghost' networks.

Humana’s experience with provider directory accuracy efforts demonstrates that this is a monumental effort. Leaving seemingly minor items unresolved could lead to continuing data issues and and increased burden on providers and payers...
This would be a game changer for the industry. Keeping provider information up to date is currently a significant pain point and costs the ecosystem a great deal of money.

CMS should require the adoption and exchange of digitally signed, primary source verified credentials as a foundational layer of any national provider directory initiative — ensuring that directories not only identify who a provider is but reliably represent what that provider is authorized and qualified to do.
Beneficiaries should have the option to find a provider they can relate to, if they so choose, and more robust data in directories would enable beneficiaries to choose the best provider for them.

2.3.themeDescription: Defining the Role of Health Information Exchanges (HIEs)

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment views Health Information Exchanges (HIEs) as a valuable, yet deeply flawed, component of the health data ecosystem. There is broad consensus that HIEs serve a critical role as trusted regional data hubs, especially for smaller providers and public health, but suffer from extreme variability in performance, participation, data quality, and cost. The central debate is whether to invest in modernizing and standardizing HIEs as foundational infrastructure that complements national frameworks like TEFCA, or to phase them out in favor of more scalable national networks. Dominant recommendations urge CMS to mandate core capabilities like FHIR APIs and patient access portals, tie funding to performance, and formally integrate HIEs into the national ecosystem to resolve their current fragmentation.

consensusPoints.text: HIEs are valuable regional assets with an ongoing role. A strong majority of commenters, including HIEs, technology vendors, providers, and associations, agree that HIEs play a vital and complementary role to national networks like TEFCA. They are valued for their deep local relationships, ability to serve smaller providers, and support for regional use cases like public health. A significant minority argues this role is diminishing or obsolete.

HIE performance, capabilities, and data quality are highly inconsistent. Nearly all commenters who discussed HIE performance, from HIEs themselves to frustrated providers and vendors, acknowledged that the landscape is marked by extreme variability. Issues cited include inconsistent provider participation, fragmented data, varying data formats and quality, and a wide gap between high-performing HIEs and ineffective ones.

exceptions.text: High-performing HIEs like CRISP, Manifest MedEx, and IHIE were consistently cited as positive models that demonstrate what is possible.

consensusPoints.text: HIEs must adopt modern standards, particularly FHIR APIs. There is overwhelming agreement that to remain relevant and effective, HIEs must transition from legacy data formats (HL7v2, C-CDA) to modern, API-based exchange using FHIR. This is seen as essential for integration, patient access, and supporting innovation.

Patient access to HIE data is currently poor but should be mandated. A strong consensus exists among patient advocates, app developers, and even some HIEs and provider groups that direct patient access to HIE-aggregated data is a major gap. Many recommend mandating that HIEs provide patient portals and/or FHIR APIs for patient-facing applications.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Future Viability and Role of HIEs
areasOfDebate.description: The Future Viability and Role of HIEs

positions.label: Essential Infrastructure
positions.stance: HIEs are foundational, trusted, and locally-responsive entities that should be strengthened, funded, and integrated as permanent, complementary parts of the national ecosystem (e.g., as "Health Data Utilities").
positions.supportLevel: The majority of commenters, including nearly all HIEs/HDUs, many tech vendors, provider associations, and public health groups.
keyArguments: HIEs provide "last-mile" connectivity for small/rural providers; they are trusted, neutral, non-profit conveners; they support critical local use cases (like ADT alerts and public health) that TEFCA doesn't. They can serve as on-ramps to TEFCA for under-resourced organizations.

positions.label: Obsolete Intermediary
positions.stance: HIEs are a transitional, fragmented, and increasingly redundant model that will be (and should be) superseded by scalable national networks like TEFCA and direct vendor APIs.
positions.supportLevel: A significant and vocal minority, particularly national network operators, some large health systems, and health IT experts.
keyArguments: HIEs create costly redundant infrastructure and administrative burden; their point-to-point model is not scalable; national frameworks provide more uniform standards and governance; their role is diminishing with the advent of APIs.

areasOfDebate.topic: Governance and Funding Model
areasOfDebate.description: Governance and Funding Model

positions.label: Public Utility Model
positions.stance: HIEs should be treated as public utilities, with state designation, stable public funding (e.g., Medicaid enhanced match, grants), and potentially mandated provider participation to ensure sustainability and comprehensive data.
positions.supportLevel: Most HIEs, state governments, and many provider associations.
keyArguments: Public funding ensures focus on public good over profit, supports under-resourced providers, and creates stability. Mandated participation overcomes fragmentation and ensures a complete community health record.

positions.label: Competitive Market Model
positions.stance: HIEs should compete with other data exchange solutions (QHINs, vendors) in a free market. Subsidies are unnecessary and distort the market; providers should choose the most efficient solution.
positions.supportLevel: Some technology entrepreneurs and market-focused commenters.
keyArguments: Competition drives innovation and efficiency; providers should not be forced to connect to multiple redundant networks; the market should determine the best model.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring their long-term sustainability and relevance in the face of TEFCA; overcoming fragmented funding; dispelling the notion that they are obsolete.
specificPoints: They position themselves as trusted, non-profit, locally-governed "cornerstones" of interoperability that provide essential services TEFCA does not, such as proactive ADT alerts, data normalization, public health partnerships, and "last-mile" connectivity. They advocate strongly for a formal "Health Data Utility" model with public funding and state designation.

Federal and state funding to support their role as public infrastructure; formal integration into the national ecosystem as TEFCA on-ramps and sub-participants; propagation of high-performing models like CRISP, IHIE, and Manifest MedEx.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of standardization (data formats, APIs, governance) across HIEs increases development complexity and cost; inconsistent data quality hinders the value of their products; slow HIE integration timelines stifle innovation.
specificPoints: This diverse group sees HIEs as both critical partners and frustrating bottlenecks. Interoperability platforms (e.g., Rhapsody, InterSystems) view HIEs as key clients and infrastructure components. App developers (e.g., Selfiie) see HIEs as vital data sources for patients. National network operators (e.g., Surescripts) view them as redundant competitors.
Mandate HIE adoption of FHIR APIs; standardize data quality and consent models; integrate HIEs into TEFCA as regional nodes; some suggest phasing them out entirely in favor of national networks.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: High cost of HIE participation, especially for small, rural, and safety-net providers; variability in data availability and usefulness (data is often incomplete, not integrated into workflows, or easier to get via fax/phone); administrative burden of connecting to multiple, inconsistent networks.
specificPoints: Providers have the most direct, on-the-ground experience with the utility (or lack thereof) of HIE data. Large systems in well-served states find them highly valuable, while others find them functionally useless. Post-acute and specialty providers feel excluded.

Subsidize or provide financial incentives for HIE participation; mandate that EHR vendors provide easy, low-effort HIE interfaces; improve data quality and workflow integration; standardize networks to reduce complexity.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of direct access to their own data held by HIEs; data accuracy issues (e.g., blended data with other patients); privacy violations from data sharing without granular consent.
specificPoints: This group focuses intensely on patient rights, control, and privacy. They see HIEs as opaque data repositories that primarily serve providers and payers, and they demand direct access and control.
Mandate HIEs provide patient portals and FHIR APIs; grant individuals granular control over what data is shared and with whom; allow individuals to correct or remove their data from HIEs.

noteworthyInsights.insight: A technology vendor for mobile medical services (Beyond Lucid Technologies) offers a sharp warning that the push to create state-designated "Health Data Utilities" could create monopolies that exclude innovative or non-traditional data sources, such as prehospital data from EMS, leading to a "'separate and unequal' purgatory" for crucial data.

A patient (Tim Moon) shares that his wife "has worked for years to enable HIE in Minnesota, but Epic hospitals refuse to cooperate, which leaves patients like me vulnerable." This single sentence powerfully illustrates the real-world impact of information blocking and the failure of HIEs in some regions.

Dr. Scott Rifkin cites a specific University of Pennsylvania study published in the American Journal of Managed Care demonstrating that CRISP's partnership with a data platform for nursing homes led to a marked decrease in hospital admissions, providing concrete evidence for the value of a high-performing HIE.

Manifest MedEx provides a compelling re-framing of HIEs, arguing they are not just "data pipelines" but "data refineries—collecting, combining, and cleaning clinical, administrative, and increasingly social data from thousands of sources." This shifts the value proposition from simple exchange to active data curation.

Patient advocates present a fundamental opposition to the entire HIE concept, viewing it as an instrument of non-consensual data sharing that violates privacy, a stark contrast to the provider and vendor view of HIEs as tools for care coordination.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic Variations: There is a clear divide between commenters from states with high-performing HIEs (e.g., Maryland, Indiana, California) who see them as essential and replicable, and those from states with fragmented or non-functional HIEs (e.g., Minnesota, as cited by one patient) who see them as failures. This suggests that "HIE" means very different things depending on location.

Facility-type Patterns: Small, rural, safety-net, and specialty (e.g., dental, dialysis, behavioral health) providers consistently report being excluded from or priced out of HIEs. In contrast, large, multi-state health systems are more concerned with the burden of connecting to multiple different state HIEs. This highlights an equity and access gap in the current HIE landscape.

The "Health Data Utility" (HDU) Movement: A clear pattern emerges of HIEs rebranding and repositioning themselves as "Health Data Utilities" (HDUs). This is a strategic move to frame their services as essential public infrastructure deserving of public funding and state designation, countering the narrative that they are becoming obsolete.

Unintended Consequences of National Frameworks: Multiple commenters, particularly HIEs themselves, raise the concern that a myopic focus on TEFCA could inadvertently destroy the valuable local infrastructure and relationships that HIEs have built over decades, losing critical services like ADT alerts and public health partnerships that TEFCA currently doesn't support.

keyQuotations.quote: [HIEs] should complement, not compete with, TEFCA.
My wife has worked for years to enable HIE in Minnesota, but Epic hospitals refuse to cooperate, which leaves patients like me vulnerable.

Regional HIEs are no longer as beneficial as nation-wide exchanges, yet many regional HIEs continue to press policymakers for financial or regulatory support to sustain their systems, despite the fact that these are rapidly becoming obsolete.
In a fully connected environment, the HIE model becomes unnecessary.

Pay up to $75,000 annually for HIE/QHIN access, which is unjustifiable at our scale. This pricing structure creates care disparities: large health systems can access complete histories while small PACE programs treating vulnerable patients cannot afford the same tools.
Even if you spend lots of dev time, and money, connecting to all of these exchanges (like we did), you're still left with the tough problem of making this medical data actually usable for providers.
The challenging economics of HIEs are purely because CMS provides far more incentives to delivery systems to NOT share data.

It could be disastrous for Mobile Medical services...if HIEs become HDUs and are 'anointed' as a sole statewide entity, because some states have already shown themselves to be insular, ignoring or even rejecting calls to interoperate with Mobile Medical services...
High-performing, not-for-proﬁt HIEs ﬁll critical service gaps left by national networks and frameworks like TEFCA and commercial vendor networks.

To achieve widespread improvement, states should be required to designate and support statewide HIEs or HDUs, with statutory mandates for provider participation to ensure comprehensive data coverage and reduce friction among stakeholders.
HIEs have fallen short due to fragmentation, high cost, or low participation.

The federal government has made significant investments in DHIN and organizations like it, and there is a current and actionable opportunity for the federal government to leverage HIEs and the work that has already been done...

Give individuals granular control over their health data stored by HIEs, instead of all-or-nothing consent forms for data sharing. This should include the right to remove some or all data from the HIE.
It allows untold numbers of third parties into private lives and confidential conversations—virtually, through public and private HIEs...
Through TEFCA deployment, state HINs should join TEFCA or be phased out.
HIEs’ role is evolving, not diminishing; they are redefined as regional/domain-specific intermediaries adding unique value beyond generalized exchange.
We cannot stress enough the importance of non-profit self-sustaining HIEs... who are committed to not monetizing the data we are trusted to protect.

sections.executiveSummary: Public comment reveals near-universal support for making pandemic-era Medicare telehealth flexibilities permanent, with an overwhelming majority of patients, providers, and associations describing telehealth as essential for healthcare access. The primary tension exists between this broad consensus and a minority concern that virtual care can lead to misdiagnosis and lower quality care. The dominant recommendation is for CMS and Congress to act decisively to provide policy certainty by removing geographic restrictions, continuing audio-only coverage, and addressing regulatory barriers like state-based licensure to prevent a catastrophic loss of access for millions of beneficiaries.

consensusPoints.text: Make Pandemic-Era Telehealth Flexibilities Permanent
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Near-universal support across all stakeholder types, including patients, providers, hospitals, technology vendors, and professional associations. Of the 45 comments provided, 44 expressed strong support for continuing or expanding telehealth, with only one raising direct opposition.

exceptions.text: One individual commenter strongly disagreed, citing personal experience with a potential misdiagnosis and arguing telehealth leads to insufficient care. While this view is a distinct minority, it represents the primary counter-argument to the overwhelming consensus.

consensusPoints.text: Telehealth Is Essential for Access in Rural and Underserved Communities
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, particularly providers, advocacy groups, and health systems serving rural populations, emphasized this point.

exceptions.text: Many commenters raising this point also highlighted that its effectiveness is dependent on broadband access, underscoring the need for permanent audio-only options.

areasOfDebate.topic: Overall Efficacy and Safety of Telehealth
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether telehealth is a safe, effective, and indispensable tool for expanding patient access, or if it risks leading to inadequate care and misdiagnosis due to the lack of an in-person physical examination.

positions.label: Essential for Access
positions.stance: Telehealth is a safe, effective, and indispensable tool for expanding patient access, improving care continuity, and overcoming significant logistical barriers.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of commenters (44 of 45), including patients, providers, health systems, and associations.
keyArguments: Overcomes barriers of geography, transportation, cost, and disability, allowing vulnerable populations to receive consistent care.
Clinical outcomes are equivalent or better for many services, particularly behavioral health, with lower no-show rates.
Enables innovative care models like Hospital at Home, virtual supervision, and remote specialist consultations that improve efficiency and address workforce shortages.

positions.label: Leads to Misdiagnosis
positions.stance: Telehealth, by its nature, can lead to inadequate care, misdiagnosis, and inappropriate prescriptions due to the lack of an in-person physical examination.
positions.supportLevel: A single, but well-articulated, individual commenter.
keyArguments: Absence of a physical exam prevents proper diagnostic assessment.
Can result in unnecessary or incorrect medications being prescribed.
Necessary diagnostic testing may be omitted, leaving root causes of symptoms untreated.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Maintaining access to care despite mobility limitations, disabilities, transportation issues, and high travel costs.
specificPoints: A patient with Cerebral Palsy notes using Zoom "saves me time and resources" as a wheelchair user.
A senior in assisted living without a car calls the benefit a "godsend" for accessing providers an hour away.
A TBI survivor describes how the travel burden for over 100 annual encounters consumed "more than 75% of my 'cognitively available time'," much of which could have been handled virtually.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Policy uncertainty hindering long-term investment in technology and infrastructure; state-based licensure restrictions creating barriers to care; workforce burnout and shortages.
specificPoints: Fairview Health Services states, "The most significant barrier to access for digital health care is the lack of a permanent and stable federal regulatory framework".
The Massachusetts Association of Behavioral Health Systems highlights that virtual programs achieve "similar clinically equivalent outcomes" and improve staff retention.
Johns Hopkins identifies "state-based licensure restrictions as one of the most meaningful barriers to technology opportunities," forcing a "patchwork of state-specific rules".

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Technology Vendors and Digital Health Companies
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Limited or uncertain reimbursement for virtual services; regulations that prohibit virtual-only program delivery (e.g., Medicare Diabetes Prevention Program); lack of payment for asynchronous care.
specificPoints: WeightWatchers provides data that 85% of its 100,000+ telehealth clinic patients live outside urban areas, demonstrating the reach of virtual specialty care that would otherwise be inaccessible.
The Diabetes Advocacy Alliance argues that prohibiting fully virtual, CDC-recognized programs from Medicare participation "suppresses the supply of MDPP services and restricts access".

noteworthyInsights.insight: Unexpected Connection to Provider Safety: The AAMC and ATA Action compellingly argue that requiring providers to enroll their home address for billing discourages telehealth adoption due to real privacy and safety concerns, an angle that connects administrative rules to workforce willingness to participate.

Compelling Personal Example of 'Cognitive Budget': A TBI survivor quantifies the burden of in-person care by stating it consumed over 75% of their limited daily 'cognitively available time.' This provides a powerful, human-centered metric for the value of virtual visits beyond just time and money saved.

Data-Backed Observation on Behavioral Health Dominance: The Massachusetts Telemedicine Coalition (tMED) cites state data showing that 9 of the top 10 diagnosis categories for telehealth spending were for behavioral health conditions, providing concrete evidence of telehealth's outsized importance in mental healthcare.

Telehealth as a Superior, Not Just Substitute, Modality: WiserCare makes the case that telehealth is often a better modality for Advance Care Planning (ACP) because it facilitates longer, more thoughtful conversations and makes it easier to include family members. This counters the narrative that virtual is always a step down from in-person.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic Variations: Commenters consistently differentiate the needs of rural vs. urban patients. For rural patients, telehealth solves for distance and specialist shortages. For urban patients, it solves for transportation issues, mobility impairments, and lost work time. This highlights the universal, though varied, utility of telehealth.

Unintended Consequences of Administrative Rules: Multiple commenters highlight how seemingly minor regulations create major barriers. The provider home address rule is a prime example. Another is the rule preventing CDC-recognized virtual-only diabetes prevention programs from enrolling in Medicare, effectively shutting out innovators in the space.

Experience Correlations: Support for telehealth is directly proportional to the barriers an individual or group faces with in-person care. Patients with disabilities, providers in rural areas, and mental health advocates—groups for whom access is most challenging—are the most passionate and detailed proponents.

A Two-Pronged Problem: Access and Investment: The public comment reveals two distinct but intertwined justifications for permanence. For patients, it's a matter of immediate access to care. For providers and industry, it's a matter of strategic certainty needed for long-term investment. Policymakers are being told they must solve both problems.

keyQuotations.quote: The most significant barrier to access for digital health care is the lack of a permanent and stable federal regulatory framework around digital health. Patients should be able to access telemedicine from their home and providers should be allowed to provide the service and be reimbursed. Making the digital health changes permanent will avoid all the inefficiencies and anxiety each deadline causes for patients and care providers.

I use the Medicare Telehealth benefit to access my physician. I don’t have a car so I have to rely on family and friends to travel 45 minutes to an hour one way to see my providers. This benefit is a godsend, unfortunately, this will expire on September 30, 2025.

CMS is placing too much emphasis on Telehealth or Telemedicine as though it enhances patient care and access, I disagree it leads to inadequate/insufficient care, misdiagnosis, unnecessary medication, no medication, not the right medication, no testing.

In my own experience, having only 45 minutes of focus time twice a day and having over 100 encounters in my first year after my 2nd TBI, more than 75% of my 'cognitively available time' was spent pursuing my healthcare recovery.

Requiring reporting of practitioner’s home addresses for enrollment is likely to discourage practitioners from providing telehealth services from their home, limiting access to care. Additionally, practitioners have expressed privacy and safety concerns associated with enrolling their home address.

Of the more than 100,000 WW Clinic patients (as of March 2025) served through our telehealth platform, approximately 85% live outside of urban areas, including 20% who live in rural areas, where we bring to the doorstep specialty obesity services that would not otherwise be accessible.
Currently, Medicare members are not afforded the same virtual treatment options as non-Medicare members, unless they are able to pay out of pocket.
The remote digital direct supervision provision was invaluable to allow remote supervision of all sorts of procedures that are mostly safe but required synchronous direct supervision.
Virtual PHP achieves similar clinically equivalent outcomes such as reduction in symptoms, improved functioning, and participant satisfaction as in-person care.
Telehealth is typically a better modality than in-office, enabling longer conversations to explore goals of care tradeoffs, include family members, and better utilize experts in ACP.

3.2.themeDescription: Reforming Remote Monitoring (RPM/RTM) Reimbursement and Rules

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on Remote Monitoring (RPM/RTM) reflects a deep paradox: while commenters universally see these services as essential for proactive, modern healthcare, they express profound frustration with Medicare's current reimbursement and rules, which they describe as financially unsustainable, clinically arbitrary, and a direct barrier to patient access and innovation. The key tension lies between stakeholders advocating for immediate, targeted fixes to the fee-for-service system—such as eliminating the 16-day rule and increasing payments—and those pushing for a more fundamental shift to value-based models that reward outcomes over volume. The dominant thrust of recommendations is a call for CMS to urgently reform its policies to stabilize payment, eliminate restrictive billing requirements, and remove patient cost-sharing to unlock the full potential of remote care.

consensusPoints.text: Current billing rules, particularly the 16-day-per-month data transmission requirement, are clinically arbitrary and a major barrier to adoption. A strong majority of commenters, including professional associations, providers, and trade groups, explicitly condemn this rule. They argue it misaligns with clinical needs, where monitoring may be intermittent or required for shorter periods, and creates a rigid, all-or-nothing reimbursement cliff. ATA Action calls the requirement "arbitrary and lacks clinical relevance." The Bipartisan Policy Center notes it creates "administrative burden without adding value." Houston Methodist agrees, stating it "often lack[s] clinical relevance and limit[s] appropriate use."

exceptions.text: No commenters defended the 16-day rule.

consensusPoints.text: Patient cost-sharing is a significant deterrent to patient participation in RPM/RTM programs. A broad coalition of stakeholders, including large health systems, community health centers, and digital health providers, identifies copayments as a primary reason for low patient engagement and program drop-off. Ochsner Health provides compelling data that 43% of beneficiaries opted out of RPM programs when cost-sharing was reinstated post-PHE. The National Association of Community Health Centers (NACHC) states, "Patients should not have to choose between crucial RPM services and necessities such as food, housing, and transportation." Jefferson Health concurs that programs "often suffer from low patient engagement due to patient cost sharing requirements."

Current reimbursement rates are inadequate, declining, and fail to cover the true costs of technology and clinical services. Nearly all commenters who discussed payment levels, from individual clinicians to large health systems and device companies, argue that current rates are financially unsustainable. They cite falling reimbursement, the high cost of devices, and uncompensated software and clinical workflow costs. The Association of American Medical Colleges (AAMC) notes that RVUs for RPM have "decreased each year since their adoption." Babyscripts provides a stark cost comparison, showing that a cellular BP cuff can cost $70-120 per month while the national payment amount for the device code is only $43.02. The Remote Monitoring Leadership Council argues the current structure "does not accurately capture the costs of providing high-quality RPM services with robust data capabilities."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters who discussed payment levels

consensusPoints.text: The scope of covered conditions and use cases for RPM/RTM is too narrow. Many commenters, particularly from the device and digital health industries, argue for expanding coverage to more chronic conditions, preventive use cases, and specific functions like medication adherence. Medsense Health advocates for a "general RTM code for medication adherence," noting that challenges are not confined to the currently covered respiratory and musculoskeletal systems. The American Osteopathic Association (AOA) urges payment for preventive monitoring, such as CGMs for prediabetic patients. The Alliance of Wound Care Stakeholders recommends expanding codes to include "more wound-related use cases."

areasOfDebate.topic: Reimbursement Model Reform
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to improve the existing Fee-for-Service (FFS) model or shift entirely to value-based or outcome-driven payment models.

positions.label: Fix Fee-for-Service (FFS)
positions.stance: This position advocates for modifying the existing CPT code structure to make it more flexible and financially viable.
positions.supportLevel: The majority of commenters, including provider associations (AHA, AMGA, AAMC), digital health companies (Plethy, Babyscripts), and trade groups (ATA Action).
keyArguments: This is the most direct path to immediate relief
it addresses specific, well-defined problems like the 16-day rule and inadequate device payment
it works within the existing, familiar billing framework, which is easier for providers to adopt.

positions.label: Shift to Value/Outcomes
positions.stance: This position argues for moving away from FFS billing entirely, toward models that pay for outcomes, episodes of care, or a fixed monthly rate.
positions.supportLevel: A smaller but influential group of digital health innovators, health systems, and policy-focused organizations.
keyArguments: FFS and time-based billing punish efficiency and don't reward the value of AI-driven, asynchronous care
outcome-based models align incentives with patient health and curb spending on low-value monitoring
per-beneficiary-per-month (PBPM) payments simplify administration.

areasOfDebate.topic: Appropriate Duration of Monitoring
areasOfDebate.description: Conflict between allowing clinician discretion for monitoring duration versus imposing time limits to control costs and ensure value.

positions.label: Provider Discretion
positions.stance: This position holds that monitoring duration should be flexible and determined by clinical need without arbitrary limits.
positions.supportLevel: Most providers and digital health companies operating in the current FFS system.
keyArguments: Clinical needs vary greatly by patient and condition
imposing time limits interferes with the clinician-patient relationship
continuous monitoring provides ongoing value for many chronic conditions.

positions.label: Time-Limited Monitoring
positions.stance: This position suggests that RPM/RTM should be time-limited to the period of most active management, with requirements for reassessment.
positions.supportLevel: A think tank focused on healthcare value.
keyArguments: Evidence shows RPM is most effective for a focused period
indefinite billing can lead to prolonged, low-value monitoring and enormous spending
limits would incentivize providers to achieve stability efficiently.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Healthcare Providers (Hospitals, Health Systems, Medical Groups)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Financial unsustainability due to low/decreasing reimbursement that doesn't cover device and staff costs (AAMC, Ochsner); patient access barriers from cost-sharing (Jefferson Health, Ochsner); and restrictive, clinically irrelevant billing rules (16-day rule, time tracking) that create administrative burden and hinder adoption (Houston Methodist, Fairview Health Services).
specificPoints: They emphasize the operational and workflow challenges, such as the need for dedicated staff (Houston Methodist), the data deluge overwhelming providers (Johns Hopkins), and the disincentive to treat co-morbid patients when only one RPM program can be billed (Ochsner).

Eliminate the 16-day rule (AAMC, Houston Methodist), waive patient cost-sharing (Ochsner, NACHC), increase reimbursement to reflect true costs (AHA, Ascension), and allow billing for multiple RPM programs per patient (Ochsner).

Ochsner Health’s comment provides a powerful case study, detailing how cost-sharing led 43% of beneficiaries to opt-out of programs and how their 4:1 ROI in a Medicaid pilot demonstrates the value stunted by current Medicare rules.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Unclear and insufficient reimbursement pathways that stifle innovation and market access (Behaivior, Marani Health); outdated payment rates that don't reflect technology costs (Babyscripts); and narrow coverage definitions that exclude many use cases like medication adherence or new digital therapeutics (Medsense Health, AugMend Health).
specificPoints: These commenters focus heavily on the misalignment between how their technology delivers value (asynchronously, via software, for prevention) and how Medicare pays for it (synchronously, based on device hardware, for treatment of specific diagnoses). Many provide detailed data on cost-effectiveness and improved outcomes from their platforms.

Expand coverage to new conditions and use cases (Rune Labs, Medsense Health); create new codes for software, AI, and digital therapeutics (Cadence, AugMend Health); update device reimbursement to match modern costs (Babyscripts); and eliminate restrictive time/day requirements (Cadence, Plethy).

Cadence presented a cost and utilization analysis of over 5,000 patients showing $1,308 annual savings per patient and a 27% reduction in hospital admissions, arguing for modernizing reimbursement to capture the value of their software-driven approach.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The 16-day rule is a major, arbitrary barrier (ATA Action, AMGA); reimbursement is too low and declining, especially in rural areas (ATA Action, BPC); patient cost-sharing limits access (NACHC); and rules are overly complex and confusing (AAPA, CMA).
specificPoints: These groups synthesize the experiences of their broad memberships, providing a unified voice on the most pressing and widely felt issues. They often connect policy flaws to systemic problems like provider burnout and health equity.

Adopt the new AMA CPT codes for tiered monitoring duration (ATA Action); eliminate geographic adjustments for digital services (BPC); waive co-insurance for vulnerable populations (NACHC); and allow RPM/RTM for new patients, not just established ones (AHA).
The Bipartisan Policy Center provides a comprehensive, data-driven overview of the key problems, citing OIG reports and highlighting the damaging effect of geographic adjustments on rural care.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Current rules incentivize low-value, indefinite monitoring (Peterson Center); lack of coverage for prevention limits proactive care (AOA); and reimbursement hurdles block access for specific patient populations like those with arthritis or kidney disease (Arthritis Foundation, ASN).
specificPoints: These groups often take a systems-level view, focusing on aligning payment with clinical value and long-term health goals rather than just transactional billing. They bring specific patient population needs to the forefront.

Tie reimbursement to evidence-based, condition-specific duration limits (Peterson Center); cover RPM for preventive care (AOA); and create specific payment pathways for chronic conditions currently bundled into other payments, like ESRD (ASN).

The Arthritis Foundation cites a soon-to-be-published study showing RTM plus physical therapy significantly reduced pain and increased adherence, demonstrating a clear evidence-based case for removing reimbursement hurdles.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Risk of Low-Value, Indefinite Monitoring: The Peterson Center on Healthcare provides a crucial counter-narrative, arguing that while RPM is valuable, current FFS rules create incentives for perpetual monitoring beyond the point of clinical benefit. They warn, "Absent policy change, future spending potential on these codes is enormous and not tied to the demonstration of clinical value." This highlights the tension between expanding access and ensuring fiscal responsibility.

The 'De Facto Manager' Burden: The Remote Monitoring Leadership Council offers a unique operational insight: "A remote monitoring provider who has taken responsibility for a patient’s chronic condition becomes the de facto manager of all of that patient’s urgent and chronic conditions." This uncompensated work, driven by the responsibility to respond to any abnormal vital sign, means the cost and complexity of providing high-quality RPM is far greater than what is currently reimbursed.

Punishing Success: Sutter Health powerfully frames the problem with time-based billing and copays for chronic disease management: "This approach punishes success and contradicts the goal of long-term chronic disease management," as patients are penalized for engaging in care when their condition is stable.

Compelling Personal Example: The Bipartisan Policy Center shares the story of Ebony Ford, who used a home blood pressure cuff during her second pregnancy to manage pre-eclampsia. This real-world example of how RPM enabled real-time medication adjustments and a healthier outcome provides a tangible, human face to the policy debate.

Data-Backed ROI: Ochsner Health shared data from a Medicaid pilot showing a 4:1 return on investment for hypertension and a 3:1 return for diabetes patients, with an estimated total ROI of $6.1 million. This concrete evidence of savings makes their argument that current Medicare rules are stifling cost-effective care extremely persuasive.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic Variations: There is a clear pattern of concern that current policies disproportionately harm rural communities. Commenters like the Bipartisan Policy Center, ATA Action, and Homeward Health all argue that geographic payment adjustments for digital services are illogical and create significant barriers to care in areas that need it most.

Disease-Specific Gaps: A recurring pattern is the identification of specific clinical areas where RPM/RTM is highly valuable but poorly supported by current rules. This includes end-stage renal disease (ASN, Vantive Healthcare), wound care (Alliance of Wound Care Stakeholders), maternal health (Marani Health, Delfina Care), and mental/behavioral health (Society for Digital Mental Health, KuduCare).

Unintended Consequences of Siloed Billing: The rule allowing billing for only one device per month (CPT 99454) is identified by multiple commenters (Ochsner, Babyscripts) as an unintended barrier to comprehensive care for patients with multiple chronic conditions, forcing providers to either absorb the cost of additional devices or offer incomplete care.

FFS vs. Value-Based Tension: A clear fault line is emerging. While most commenters offer fixes within the Fee-for-Service framework (as it's the current reality), a vocal and innovative minority (Sword Health, Ochsner, Peterson Center) argues that these are just patches and a fundamental shift to value-based or outcome-based payment is necessary to truly align incentives and technology.

keyQuotations.quote: [The 16-day data requirement] can disincentivize RPM use and fails to recognize the clinical value of more limited patient engagement.

Unfortunately, when we issued our annual reconsent to treat... 43% of beneficiaries who had been participating in the programs during the PHE decided to opt-out... when they learned that they could face 20% cost-sharing requirements.
The current Medicare fee-for-service payment structure does not accurately capture the costs of providing high-quality RPM services with robust data capabilities.

For example, the cost of an FDA-cleared cellular blood pressure cuff with direct EHR integration can range from $70-80 per patient per month... Per CMS, the non-facility national payment amount for 99454... is $43.02.
