noteworthyInsights.insight: Data Quantifying Access Friction: CommonWell Health Alliance provides a critical piece of data: "Anecdotally from our consumer application members, we have heard that 70% of users quit the process when presented with their portal login screen." This quantifies the impact of password/portal fatigue.

Data Quantifying Low API Usage: Oscar Health provides a stark reality check, stating they have seen "fewer than 100 calls on its provider access API, and even fewer on its patient access API," suggesting availability does not equal utilization.

Data Access as a Trauma Trigger: A caregiver with Imagine Pediatrics provides a stunning insight that forcing children with complex medical histories to constantly repeat their story for new providers is not just an inconvenience, but is "triggering" and causes "episodes specifically because we have had to revisit the same traumas over and over".

Data Access as a "Disability Tax": NOSSCR powerfully frames the issue of charging fees for records as a "disability tax," pointing out a loophole where helpers for disabled individuals are charged hefty fees to obtain data for public benefit applications, a cost passed to the vulnerable patient.

Compelling Personal Example of Data Loss: A commenter from HealthLX powerfully illustrates the consequences of siloing: "I’ve experienced the impact of following my primary provider from one network to another... Years of my data living in one health system did not follow him... This data was digitally shredded, impacting my personal patient journey. I consider this criminal behavior...".

The "Scare Screen" as a Business Tactic: Unite articulates a crucial insight: that frightening warning messages from EHRs are not just a well-intentioned attempt to protect patients, but "a highly effective business strategy that uses Fear, Uncertainty, and Doubt (FUD) to manipulate patient choice" and stifle competition.

"Data delayed is data denied": This phrase from caregiver James Younkin powerfully encapsulates the patient perspective that delays in receiving lab results are not a minor inconvenience but a fundamental denial of their right to timely, actionable information.

"Portal-API Data Parity": This recommendation from James Younkin is a simple, elegant policy idea: mandate that anything a patient can see in a portal must be available via the API. This would close a significant loophole.

The "Final Mile" Problem: Jean Ross of Primary Record frames the issue not as a lack of legal rights, but as an unfinished "ﬁnal mile, from legal access to usable record," which still leaves families juggling logins and paper binders.

Unexpected Data Silo Spotlight: The Dental Standards Institute compellingly argues that oral health data is almost entirely excluded from interoperability efforts, despite its importance to overall health. Patients should not have to "beg" for their dental records.

The Contrarian View: Dr. Chris Kreis argues that "patients, but for some small, educated fraction, don’t really care about what’s in their charts." He posits that what patients truly want is for their doctors to have a single, consolidated chart.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Caregiver as Unpaid System Integrator: A clear and pervasive pattern across dozens of comments is the immense burden placed on family caregivers. They are consistently depicted as the de facto health information integrators, forced to manually bridge the gaps between siloed systems at great personal, emotional, and financial cost.

The Tech Vendor vs. Incumbent Culture Clash: A strong narrative thread shows new technology vendors and advocates pushing for radical openness (full EHI, national directories), while simultaneously accusing incumbent EHR vendors and some provider systems of information blocking, non-compliance, and anti-competitive behavior.

The "Build It and They Won't Come" Reality: A pattern of comments from payer organizations points to a significant gap between the supply of Patient Access APIs and patient demand/use. This suggests that technical solutions alone are insufficient and must be paired with massive education, awareness, and usability efforts.

Specialty-Specific Data Gaps: A clear pattern emerged where professional organizations highlighted the exclusion of their specific data types from interoperability conversations. This was most prominent for medical imaging (RSNA), dental records (ADA), and medical device data (Abbott, Rising T1DE Alliance).

The "Chicken-and-Egg" Dynamic: A key systemic barrier is a "persistent chicken-and-egg dynamic—where limited utility slows adoption and limited adoption constrains utility." This suggests that incremental improvements may be insufficient; a significant leap in utility is needed to break the cycle.

TEFCA: Promising in Theory, Lagging in Practice for Patients: While many organizations acknowledge TEFCA's potential for Individual Access Services (IAS), there is a strong pattern of comments noting that its real-world impact on patient-initiated data requests is currently minimal.

keyQuotations.quote: [The current system is an] inconvenient and cumbersome time sink for patients and caregivers.
Data delayed is data denied.

It’s very frustrating to continue to have to repeat the same information. Not only that, it’s triggering. I have to have my boys with me and my boys have to sit with me and revisit the same traumas over and over.”

One oncologist told us that his patients have learned about having cancer: From a smartphone notification in the middle of a business dinner; While reading a bedtime story to a 3-year-old child; and During a rush-hour commute.
I am the only person looking out for my safety and accordingly, should have access to everything.
The greatest opportunity for health IT lies in requiring API access to all Electronic Health Information (EHI)... empowering patients with complete, usable access to their health information.

I’ve experienced the impact of following my primary provider from one network to another... Years of my data living in one health system did not follow him... This data was digitally shredded... I consider this criminal behavior...
Anecdotally from our consumer application members, we have heard that 70% of users quit the process when presented with their portal login screen...
Oscar has seen fewer than 100 calls on its provider access API, and even fewer on its patient access API.

This practice by developers of certified health IT of injecting frightening warning messages... is a highly effective business strategy that uses Fear, Uncertainty, and Doubt (FUD) to manipulate patient choice.

The 21st Century Cures Act opened a historic door... Yet the families and frontline clinicians we serve still juggle multiple logins and portals, chase faxed records, and rely on paper binders because the ﬁnal mile, from legal access to usable record, remains unﬁnished.
Without immediate access to complete medical records, we start care blind.
Because of a loophole in the existing laws, these helpers are often not afforded the 'reasonable fee' protections... In effect, this is a 'disability tax'.
For too long, we’ve made it difficult for them and then blamed them for not interacting with their data.
Electronic health record systems were not designed to solve this problem. They were created as billing and documentation tools for institutions—not as relationship tools for people.
With the ongoing emergence of AI tools, the entirety of a patient's EHI will be extracted soon--with, or without, the cooperation of the health IT vendors.
My wife has worked for years to enable HIE in Minnesota, but Epic hospitals refuse to cooperate, which leaves patients like me vulnerable.
The data is owned by the patient—not the EHR vendor—and noncompliance should result in loss of certification and significant penalties.

5.2.themeDescription: Patient's Right to Correct and Contribute to Medical Records

sections.executiveSummary: There is an overwhelming consensus across all stakeholder types that the current system for correcting medical record errors is broken and patients urgently need the ability to correct inaccuracies and contribute their own data. Commenters, particularly patients and their advocates, describe existing records as dangerously inaccurate, with current correction processes being ineffective, burdensome, and frequently ignored. The primary debate is not if patients should have this right, but how: a more radical proposal for direct "write" access into the record versus a more widely supported, structured process for submitting corrections and annotations. The dominant recommendation is for CMS and ONC to mandate a standardized, FHIR-based electronic pathway for patient-initiated amendments and contributions.

consensusPoints.text: The current process for correcting medical records is fundamentally broken. Commenters describe the existing, often paper-based, process as inaccessible, confusing, time-consuming, and ultimately ineffective.

exceptions.text: There were no comments defending the status quo.

consensusPoints.text: Patients must have the ability to contribute to their health records. This support spans every stakeholder category, from individuals and advocacy groups to tech vendors, physicians, and health systems. The rationale extends beyond just fixing errors to enriching the record with patient-generated health data (PGHD), goals, preferences, and symptom tracking.

A standardized, technology-based solution is required. Most stakeholders, especially technology vendors, health IT professionals, and professional associations, agree that any new process must be electronic, integrated into workflows, and based on modern standards. FHIR is universally cited as the standard to use for this purpose.

areasOfDebate.topic: Mechanism for Patient Input
areasOfDebate.description: The primary area of debate centers on the mechanism and scope of patient contribution to the medical record.

positions.label: Direct Write Access
positions.stance: Patients should have the ability to write directly in their visit notes or medical records, akin to a "Wikipedia model."
positions.supportLevel: A passionate minority of individual patients and advocates.
keyArguments: This is the only way to ensure valid and accurate data is recorded and to bypass provider intransigence
current "addendum" processes are insufficient as they are obscure and ignored
it is the most direct way to counter deliberate omissions or errors.

positions.label: Formal Correction Process
positions.stance: Patients should use a standardized electronic process to request amendments, add annotations, or contribute structured data, which is then reviewed and integrated by the provider.
positions.supportLevel: The strong majority of tech vendors, professional associations, health systems, and policy experts.
keyArguments: This approach is more technically feasible and aligns with existing legal (HIPAA) and clinical frameworks
it maintains the integrity of the provider's original note while ensuring the patient's voice is captured
it ensures patient-contributed data is clearly labeled and distinguishable from provider-entered data.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient safety risks from rampant, uncorrected errors; powerlessness in the face of a system that ignores correction requests; the downstream consequences of bad data, including misdiagnosis, denial of care, and inability to get insurance.
specificPoints: They provide visceral, often traumatic, personal stories of harm. They articulate a deep sense of distrust, with some suggesting errors are sometimes intentional.
The right to correct their record is framed as a fundamental issue of safety and respect.

Mandate easy-to-use digital tools for direct corrections or annotations; create centralized reporting mechanisms for harm events (Patients for Patient Safety US); ensure patient-submitted corrections appear alongside the original, disputed entry.
A caregiver narrowly prevented a "deadly adverse event" after finding her mother was about to be given discontinued medications listed in the EHR.
A patient was misdiagnosed with cancer, and the error remains in their record, impacting their ability to get life insurance.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of clear standards and regulatory mandates for 'write' capabilities, which stifles innovation; the need for full 'CRUD' (Create, Read, Update, Delete) APIs, not just read-only access; ensuring patient-contributed data is structured and distinguishable.
specificPoints: They frame the issue as a technical and architectural problem that is solvable with the right standards and incentives.
They see patient contribution not just as a right but as a valuable, underutilized data stream that can fuel new products and AI tools.

Mandate a FHIR Implementation Guide for patient corrections; include 'write' capabilities and patient data reconciliation in ONC certification criteria; expand TEFCA to support direct patient contributions.
Innovaccer calls for full CRUD capabilities.
Apple supports a world where users can share data back to providers.
Microsoft recommends mandating electronic pathways for amendment requests.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The danger of inaccurate data propagating across systems; ensuring any patient-contributed data is clearly distinguished from provider-entered data; avoiding disruptive workflows; managing liability.
specificPoints: This group validates the problem of inaccurate data from the clinical side, citing examples like merged patient records or incorrect allergy lists (Texas Medical Association).
They support patient education and standardized correction methods but show less enthusiasm for direct, unmediated 'write' access.

Standardized methods to correct and retract erroneous data across all systems where it has propagated; funding for certification of PHRs with error correction tools; leveraging existing encounters like the Medicare Annual Wellness Visit to review and correct data like allergies (AAAAI).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The underutilization of valuable patient-contributed data (e.g., patient-reported outcomes, preferences, contextual data); the lack of standards and procedures for incorporating this data into EHRs.
specificPoints: They emphasize the evidence-based value of patient input for improving care quality, patient satisfaction, and research.
They highlight the gap between the potential of this data and the current inability to collect and use it systematically.
CMS should support the standardization of patient preferences and PROs; develop new policies and procedures for how providers review, manage, and act on patient-generated data (NORC).

noteworthyInsights.insight: "Wikipedia Model" for Records: Patient advocate Regina Holliday compellingly proposes a "Wikipedia model" for data updates, reframing patients and caregivers as knowledgeable contributors who can improve the accuracy of the collective record.

"Recording Encounters as a Right": James Younkin, a health IT professional and caregiver, makes a novel argument that under the Cures Act, a patient cannot "access, exchange, and use" health information if they are "blocked from capturing the real-time dialogue that gives data context," positioning the right to record one's own appointment as a protected activity.

"Errors Have Financial Consequences": A commenter provided a concrete example of how a misdiagnosis for cancer remaining in their record directly impacted their "ability to secure cancer and Life and Long Term Care Insurance," highlighting tangible harms beyond clinical safety.

"Patient-Reported Harm Events": Patients for Patient Safety US points out that patients are excluded from formal safety reporting systems (like PSOs) and that studies show they report safety events that providers do not, arguing for a direct-to-CMS patient harm reporting tool.

"Speculation on Intentional Errors": Several anonymous individuals articulated a deeply cynical but powerful view that errors may be intentional or that doctors knowingly copy incorrect information to avoid being "questioned for not providing the standard of care," suggesting a culture of defensive documentation that actively works against accuracy.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Shift from Read-Only to Read-Write: The comments signal a major evolution in the concept of patient engagement. The focus is shifting from passive "read access" (the goal of the last decade) to active "contribution access" as the next frontier for empowering patients and improving data quality.

From Fixing Errors to Enriching Data: While fixing dangerous errors is the primary catalyst, the vision articulated by many commenters is much broader. It includes enriching the formal medical record with patient-generated health data, patient-reported outcomes, social determinants of health, life context, and personal health goals—data that providers cannot capture on their own.

FHIR as the Undisputed Technical Pathway: There is a striking lack of debate among technical experts about what standard to use. Commenters from across the industry (vendors, consultants, associations) have coalesced around HL7 FHIR as the necessary foundation for any standardized correction and contribution mechanism.

The Caregiver as a Core User: Numerous comments from individuals, advocacy groups, and technology companies explicitly include caregivers as essential users who need the same tools and rights as patients to contribute to and correct records, recognizing their critical role in managing care for others.

keyQuotations.quote: Patients describe the challenge of correcting inaccurate information... as 'very unclear and complicated, if not impossible'.
The current process for requesting corrections is 'non-standard, work-intensive, low-tech, and outside the workflow'.
When I ask for corrections, they are usually denied even when I have proof. Or I hear no response about it at all.
Patients should have the ability to contribute their own data to their electronic medical records, fostering a more complete and accurate health picture.
We would be supportive of a world in which users can share patient generated data and self-reported data like medications back to their providers to close the loop on their health care.
Patients, families, and caregivers must be involved with developing patient-reported outcome measures as well as the policies that govern the use of the information.
Mandate the use of FHIR for patient requests for corrections to establish a standards-based process.

CMS should fund certification pathways for PHRs requiring error correction capabilities... Certified systems should implement mechanisms to retract or correct erroneous data using FHIR delete/patch or a standardized correction flag.
Mandate electronic pathways for patient record amendment requests through certified Health IT.

[My record] is so inaccurate that it seems to be about someone else. When I ask for corrections, they are usually denied even when I have proof. Or I hear no response about it at all. At best, the option is to add a statement to an obscure part of the record that is rarely read, and this process takes months.
Had I not caught her in time, this could have been a very serious and deadly adverse event.
Each doctor copies the last incorrect note, test result, or diagnosis. This occurs even if the patient is unsafe, even if the diagnosis contradicts reality. It is intentional. It is the culture.
A patient cannot 'access, exchange, and use' health information if blocked from capturing the real-time dialogue that gives data context.
As more data is exchanged and accessible to patients, false information in the chart is carried with the patient to every provider they visit once Provider Access APIs go live in 2027.

We want to be part of the solution, but you have to make it where we can get into the record and provide corrections because as the patient or family caregiver, we often know what did happen and the medical record often is incorrect.

The current situation reveals a stark absence of robust mechanisms that empower patients and their families to report harmful events, resulting in significant gaps in data quality and oversight in patient safety.
Functional status, living situation, or social needs, vital for care decisions but almost never documented in accessible systems.
CMS and ASTP/ONC should recognize patients not just as beneficiaries, but as data stewards, contributors, and innovators.

Overall, CMS should seek to ask patients and caregivers not only what data they would like from other stakeholders (i.e., CMS, plans, and providers), but what data they would like to send those stakeholders.

I think this capability doesn't exist because the records are set up with intentional errors. Because if the real information was there, the doctor might be questioned for not providing the standard of care.
How can patients exercise their HIPAA rights to request corrections if they cannot even view the records?

5.3.themeDescription: Patient's Right to Data Control and Deletion

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on patient data rights is unambiguous: there is an overwhelming, cross-stakeholder demand for patients to have fundamental ownership, control, and the right to delete their health information. This is crystallized in strong, coordinated calls for a "right to be forgotten." However, this drive for absolute patient autonomy creates a critical policy tension with the operational realities of value-based care, where organizations argue they cannot manage risk for patients who completely opt-out of data sharing. The dominant recommendations urge regulators to establish a legal right to data deletion and mandate granular, opt-in consent frameworks, supported by standardized technology that enforces patient-directed control and transparency.

consensusPoints.text: Patients must have fundamental ownership and control over their health data. An overwhelming majority of commenters from all stakeholder groups (patients, providers, advocates, and technology vendors) assert that the individual, not the provider, payer, or technology company, is the rightful owner of their health information. This principle is seen as the foundation for building a trustworthy digital health ecosystem.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all (95%+) of the submitted comments on this theme

exceptions.text: While agreement on the principle is universal, there is significant debate on the operational implications, particularly regarding a patient's absolute right to opt-out of data sharing within value-based care models.

consensusPoints.text: A "Right to be Forgotten" is a critical patient right. A large and vocal cohort of commenters, primarily individuals and advocacy groups, strongly advocate for a legally enforceable "right to be forgotten," allowing patients to demand the complete deletion of their health data from any system. This is seen as the ultimate expression of data control.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Over 35 commenters, including nearly every individual and many advocacy groups

exceptions.text: CareEvolution LLC noted that broad data-deletion commitments from some government agencies (like the VA) can inadvertently deter or delay app developers from enabling patient-directed access due to the technical complexity.

consensusPoints.text: Consent must be explicit, informed, granular, and easily revocable. A strong majority of commenters reject the status quo of "consent" being buried in lengthy, complex legal notices. They demand systems where patients actively opt-in and can provide granular permissions (e.g., share lab results but not therapy notes) for specific purposes, with a simple process to view and revoke access at any time.

areasOfDebate.topic: Patient's Right to Opt-Out of Data Sharing in Value-Based Care

positions.label: Absolute Right
positions.stance: Patients must have an unconditional right to opt-out of data sharing for any reason, without penalty.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of individuals, patient advocates, and privacy-focused groups.
keyArguments: It is a fundamental privacy right
patients must have agency over their data's existence and use
forcing data sharing erodes trust and autonomy.

positions.label: Conditional Participation
positions.stance: Opting out makes participation in value-based care (VBC) models like Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs) untenable.
positions.supportLevel: A vocal minority of VBC organizations and their associations.
keyArguments: It is impossible to manage patient risk, coordinate care, and be held financially accountable for outcomes without access to claims and clinical data
ACOs should not be penalized for patients they cannot effectively manage.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: (1) Loss of privacy and control over their digital footprint; (2) Unauthorized monetization of their data ("becoming the product"); (3) The inability to delete their data or sever ties with former providers.
specificPoints: They provide visceral, personal accounts of how data fragmentation and lack of control directly harm care, such as a provider accessing records without consent and prejudicing a second opinion, or the fear of data misuse by payers.
A federally mandated "right to be forgotten," strict opt-in consent for all data uses, and simple tools to see who has accessed their data and revoke permission.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: (1) Lack of clear, standardized consent and identity frameworks, which creates complexity and risk; (2) Building patient trust to drive adoption of their tools; (3) Incumbent vendor practices (like "scare screens") that block their access to data.
specificPoints: They frame patient control not just as a right, but as a technical and business prerequisite. They argue that giving patients control via APIs will unlock innovation and competition by circumventing data-hoarding by established players.
Standardized consent management APIs (e.g., FHIR Consent, OAuth 2.0), portable digital identity credentials (NIST IAL2), and building systems with privacy-by-design and Zero Trust principles.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: (1) Protecting patients from exploitation ("surveillance capitalism"); (2) Ensuring consent is genuine and not coerced; (3) Closing loopholes in privacy laws like HIPAA.
specificPoints: These groups often provide the strongest philosophical and legal arguments, framing data control as a human right. They are the most forceful critics of the current commercialization of health data and the perceived inadequacy of HIPAA.
A comprehensive federal privacy law that preempts weaker state laws, prohibiting conditioning of care on consent, and enforcing cryptographic erasure/deletion APIs.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Healthcare Providers, Payers, and their Associations
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: (1) Navigating complex and conflicting consent regulations (e.g., HIPAA vs. 42 CFR Part 2); (2) The operational burden of managing granular consent; (3) The inability to manage risk in VBC models when patients opt-out of data sharing.
specificPoints: This group lives at the intersection of patient demands and operational reality. While supporting patient control in principle, they are most attuned to the practical challenges and potential negative consequences for care delivery.
Clear federal guidance to align consent rules, standardized consent tools to reduce burden, and policy changes to address the VBC opt-out problem.

noteworthyInsights.insight: HIPAA as a "Permissive Data Sharing Rule": The Citizens' Council for Health Freedom provides a powerful and well-documented re-framing of HIPAA not as a privacy shield, but as a rule that legitimizes widespread data sharing without patient consent. They argue this misperception prevents the passage of true privacy laws and recommend renaming the "Notice of Privacy Practices" to "Notice of Disclosure Practices" to end the deception.

The Operational Reality of VBC Opt-Outs: Lumeris Healthcare offers a critical, pragmatic counterpoint to the absolute right to opt-out. They state it is "next to impossible" to manage a patient in a risk-based contract without claims data, framing the issue not as privacy vs. access, but as a fundamental incompatibility between absolute data denial and the goals of value-based care. This highlights a core policy tension that must be resolved.

EHR "Scare Screens" Undermine Patient Choice: Unite identifies a specific, manipulative vendor practice where dominant Certified Health IT developers insert "scare screens" into the standard data sharing authorization workflow to frighten patients and subvert their choice to use third-party apps. This is a concrete example of anti-competitive information blocking that directly thwarts patient agency.

The Patient as "The Product": Several commenters, most notably Patient Rights Advocate, crystallize the public’s fear with the phrase "the patient becoming the product." This captures the deep-seated concern that the primary purpose of data collection has shifted from care delivery to commercial exploitation.

Collective Governance and Data Stewardship: The Light Collective introduces the advanced concept of "collective consent," where entire patient communities engage in governance decisions about their data. This moves beyond individual control to a model of community-based data stewardship, a novel perspective in the comments.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Stakeholder-Specific Focus: A clear pattern emerges where Individuals/Advocates focus on absolute rights and principles; Technology Vendors focus on technical standards and solutions; and Providers/Payers focus on operational implementation and risk management.

The "Right to be Forgotten" as an Organized Movement: The identical phrasing "Everyone should have 'the right to be forgotten,' meaning if AI or other technological tools are being used to gather data on a patient, it should be up to the individual if that data is stored or deleted" appears verbatim in over 15 individual comments, indicating a coordinated advocacy campaign.

The Spectrum of Consent: Public demand has evolved beyond a simple "opt-in." There is a clear pattern of requests for a spectrum of consent controls, ranging from a basic data "freeze" to highly granular, attribute-based, purpose-specific, and time-bound permissions with full audit trails.

Unintended Consequences of Privacy Laws: Aegis Ventures points to a potential unintended consequence: overly restrictive data protection laws may "unintentionally restrict data sharing, discouraging the creation of comprehensive, patient-controlled health information tools that patients and families want." This highlights the need for careful legislative drafting to enable, not hinder, patient-empowering technology.

keyQuotations.quote: [HIPAA] is a permissive data sharing rule that allows for sharing of PHI without patient consent for a plethora of non-clinical purposes... You can’t force a covered entity to give your data to someone you choose, and you can’t stop them from giving it to someone they choose.”
keyQuotations.sourceType: Advocacy Group, Citizens' Council for Health Freedom (quoting fmr. ONC head David Brailer)

keyQuotations.quote: By far the most significant challenge thus far with our experiences in ACO REACH and MSSP has been when a beneficiary opts out of data sharing. It is next to impossible to effectively manage a beneficiary without access to claims data.

A patient should be in control of their data and it should not be monetized by everyone who touches it. The monetization of data adds significant costs to healthcare and discourages shared observations across clinical settings.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Trade Association, American College of Surgeons

keyQuotations.quote: We fully support interoperability, but only if patients own their data.
Face it, patient clams data is a commodity that private sector insurers control and monopolize for profit.
Everyone should have 'the right to be forgotten.'
Stop allowing providers to force patients to sign blank screens in order to receive medical care.
There is a n implicit right that providers can store patient records fore ver . This is unreasonable.
Patients should not have to trade privacy for access.
Our most private information is regularly monetized for purposes we are not aware of, with little direct benefit to us.

Dominant Certified Health IT developers use manipulative tactics ('scare screens') in the SMART on FHIR authorization workflow, which erode patient trust, subvert patient autonomy, and manipulate patient choice.
Patients and carepartners should be notified when their data is being used, and for what... We need true data agency.

For TEFCA to succeed, it must build public trust by incorporating intuitive patient controls: a simple data 'freeze,' an 'ask-me-first' option for sensitive queries, and a clear, accessible audit trail.
What is essential is the need for easily accessible, freely 'liquid' data that can flow, at the patient’s discretion and direction.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Consumer Group, The Sequoia Project's Consumer Engagement Strategy Workgroup

5.4.themeDescription: Improving Usability and Accessibility of Digital Tools

sections.executiveSummary:
There is an overwhelming, near-universal consensus among all stakeholder groups that the current ecosystem of digital health tools is failing a significant portion of the population. Commenters describe the user experience as a "complex digital maze" and a source of "Patient Flustration™," with the primary friction points being fragmented portals ("portalitis"), burdensome login processes, and incomprehensible medical jargon. This usability failure is not a minor inconvenience but a critical barrier to care that exacerbates health inequities, particularly for older adults, individuals with disabilities, caregivers, and those with limited digital or health literacy.

The core tension in the comments lies between those advocating for CMS to mandate and enforce rigorous accessibility and usability standards to protect vulnerable users, and those who believe that enabling open data access will fuel private-sector competition and innovation to solve these issues organically.

However, the most dominant recommendations converge on a multi-pronged, hybrid approach. This includes: 1) Establishing clear, foundational standards for human-centered design, including WCAG compliance, plain language, and multilingual support; 2) Investing heavily in digital literacy training and funding a robust human support layer (e.g., "digital navigators") to create a "tech-enabled, human-supported" system; and 3) Ensuring that real users—especially those from vulnerable populations—are integral to the design and testing process from the very beginning. Ultimately, commenters insist that even the most powerful technology is useless if the people who need it most cannot operate it.

consensusPoints.text: Current Digital Tools Are Unacceptably Complex, Fragmented, and Unusable for Many. There is unanimous agreement across all stakeholder groups—patients, providers, advocacy groups, and technology vendors—that the current digital health ecosystem is too difficult to navigate. The experience is consistently described with terms like "complex digital maze", "portalitis", and "Patient Flustration™", capturing the extreme frustration and cognitive burden placed on users. A key friction point is "portal fatigue"—the need to manage dozens of different usernames and passwords. A company focused on the patient experience coined the term "Patient Flustration™" to describe "the compound feeling of being both flustered and frustrated by a system that claims to be digital, yet still buries people in paperwork, portals, passwords, and phone trees". Froedtert ThedaCare Health coins the term "portalitis" to describe the "overwhelming burden we place on patients and caregivers" from managing multiple credentials and complex authentication workflows. A patient advocate shares a user's experience managing diabetes with "six different apps, each works differently, and I can never find what I need". A consumer group cites a CVS Health executive: "The No. 1 complaint from customers is the healthcare experience is not integrated".

exceptions.text: No commenters argued that the current system is sufficiently usable. Some tech vendors argued their specific platforms have solved these issues, but acknowledged the problem exists system-wide.

consensusPoints.text: Information Is Incomprehensible and Must Be Simplified. There is universal consensus that providing patients with raw data (e.g., lab results, clinical notes) is insufficient and can be harmful. Commenters across the board demand that tools translate medical jargon into plain, actionable, and culturally relevant language, often specified at a 6th to 8th-grade reading level. Multilingual support is also seen as a fundamental requirement for equity. Wellstar Health notes that the responsibility to interpret clinical notes often falls solely on the patient, as the "language used is rarely in lay terms". The American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP) recommends that companies "develop patient-facing FAQs and how-to guides written at an 8th-grade reading level". EMI Advisors, LLC states, "Even when technically available, this data is often displayed in clinical or coded formats that are difficult to interpret — particularly for individuals with limited health literacy or for whom English is not a first language".

Human-Centered Design and Human Support Are Essential. A strong majority of commenters explicitly call for human-centered design principles and the direct, compensated involvement of end-users (patients, caregivers, older adults, people with disabilities) in the co-design and testing of all digital tools. Furthermore, a broad consensus exists that technology cannot fully replace human interaction, and tools must be complemented by robust human support channels (phone, chat, in-person "digital navigators") to be adopted equitably. A patient safety group insists products must be designed "'with' (not just 'for') Medicare beneficiaries, especially those who have experienced harm or are navigating complex care". A consumer states simply and powerfully, "Whatever methods you use has to be patient tested by the appropriate age ranges". The Alliance of Wound Care Stakeholders stresses ensuring "availability of alternative support channels, such as telephonic assistance," while a patient advocate insists on a "clear, compassionate path to live help."

Digital Literacy and Access Are Foundational Barriers. A vast majority of stakeholders from all sectors identified gaps in digital literacy, lack of devices, and poor internet connectivity as critical obstacles that technology design alone cannot solve. This was a particular focus for advocacy groups, rural providers, and public health entities. The Policy Center for Maternal Mental Health states bluntly, "Accessibility hinges on broadband availability and digital literacy," noting these are major barriers to adoption. Johns Hopkins Medicine recommends CMS "invest in widespread digital literacy training programs targeted at seniors" through partnerships with community organizations, libraries, and health systems. The SCAN Foundation shares a quote from an older adult in rural Ohio: "I have a computer, but I don’t get on it because I’m afraid somebody will steal my information".

areasOfDebate.topic: Primary Driver for Improving Usability: Mandates vs. Market Forces
areasOfDebate.description: Primary Driver for Improving Usability: Mandates vs. Market Forces

positions.label: Mandates & Certification
positions.stance: CMS should establish and enforce mandatory standards for usability and accessibility to ensure a baseline level of quality and protect consumers.
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority of advocacy groups, patient organizations, professional associations, and some providers.
keyArguments: The market has failed to adequately serve vulnerable populations; clear requirements (e.g., WCAG, plain language) are needed to ensure equity; a "CMS Trusted App" framework would provide clarity.

positions.label: Market & Innovation
positions.stance: CMS should focus on unlocking data via APIs, providing incentives, and letting the private sector compete on user experience.
positions.supportLevel: A minority, primarily consisting of some technology vendors, data-focused organizations, and free-market-oriented individuals.
keyArguments: Rigid mandates stifle innovation, become quickly outdated, and lead to "checking the box"; if data is accessible, developers will naturally compete to build the most user-friendly interfaces to attract customers.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Nature of the Solution: Tech-First vs. Human-First
areasOfDebate.description: The Nature of the Solution: Tech-First vs. Human-First

positions.label: Tech-First Solutions
positions.stance: Prioritize advanced technology like AI, voice assistants, and unified platforms as the primary way to simplify user experience and scale support.
positions.supportLevel: Dominant view among technology vendors and some innovative providers.
keyArguments: Technology can provide personalized, 24/7 support at scale; AI can translate complex jargon automatically; voice-first interfaces can bypass literacy and screen-based challenges.

positions.label: Human-First Solutions
positions.stance: Emphasize that technology must be augmented with funded human support roles (digital navigators, coaches) and traditional communication channels to be effective and equitable.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from patient advocates, community-based organizations, providers, and caregiver groups.
keyArguments: Many populations will always need human assistance to build trust and overcome digital literacy barriers; technology alone cannot provide true empathy or address complex patient situations.

areasOfDebate.topic: Simplifying Logins: Federated Identity as a Solution or a New Barrier?
areasOfDebate.description: Simplifying Logins: Federated Identity as a Solution or a New Barrier?

positions.label: Federated Identity Solution
positions.stance: Mandate or incentivize a single, federated digital identity credential (e.g., Login.gov) to enable Single Sign-On (SSO) and eliminate "portalitis."
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority of technology vendors, providers, and interoperability-focused groups.
keyArguments: A unified credential reduces friction, eliminates the need for dozens of passwords, improves security, and streamlines the user experience.

positions.label: Federated Identity as Barrier
positions.stance: The process of obtaining a new high-assurance digital identity is itself a significant usability challenge that could exclude the most vulnerable.
positions.supportLevel: A significant and vocal minority, including a major EHR provider, tech vendors, and patient advocates.
keyArguments: A new credential is one more thing to manage and forget; high-assurance identity proofing (IAL2) can be complex and requires documents or technical skills that many users lack; users prefer simpler, device-native biometrics (FaceID, TouchID).

areasOfDebate.topic: The Future of Non-Digital Access: All-In Digital vs. Preserving Analog
areasOfDebate.description: The Future of Non-Digital Access: All-In Digital vs. Preserving Analog

positions.label: All-In Digital
positions.stance: Focus all efforts on creating a seamless, accessible digital-first ecosystem, assuming technology can eventually accommodate everyone.
positions.supportLevel: Implicitly supported by most technology vendors and innovators.
keyArguments: Maintaining parallel analog systems is costly and inefficient; designing for the lowest common denominator stifles innovation; technology like voice and cellular-enabled tablets can bridge most access gaps.

positions.label: Preserve Analog Options
positions.stance: Insist that phone, paper, and in-person options must be robustly maintained as an equity issue, because a significant portion of the population will never be able or willing to use digital tools.
positions.supportLevel: A vocal minority, primarily of practicing clinicians, patient advocates, and long-term care associations.
keyArguments: Forcing digital adoption will disenfranchise the most vulnerable; some older adults strongly prefer non-digital methods; assuming universal digital capability is unrealistic and dangerous.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The overwhelming cognitive load and emotional friction of managing health online ("Patient Flustration™"); tools are not designed for real-world conditions like cognitive decline or disability; complex logins are major barriers; receiving medical data in incomprehensible jargon causes anxiety.
specificPoints: This group uniquely emphasizes the emotional impact of poor design, describing it as "stressful" and "dangerous." Their comments are grounded in lived experience, providing powerful, often moving, testimony that defines usability not by features, but by burden reduction, empowerment, and safety.

Mandate strict, enforceable standards (WCAG, 6th-grade reading level); require co-design with patients and caregivers, not just for them, with compensation for their expertise; fund digital literacy and human support systems (navigators); ensure robust proxy access for caregivers.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Friction in user adoption (especially logins); lack of standardized APIs and data access; desire for CMS endorsement or certification to signal trust; concern that burdensome regulations will stifle innovation. Many comments serve to promote a specific proprietary product as the ideal solution.
specificPoints: These commenters provide the most specific, data-backed evidence of system failure points (e.g., 75% user drop-off rate at the portal credential step from b.well). They champion technology (especially AI and voice) as the most scalable solution and frame usability as a technical problem to be solved.

A unified digital identity (e.g., using Login.gov); leveraging AI to translate medical jargon and power voice navigation; creating public APIs to fuel innovation; CMS certification programs for "trusted apps"; creating open-source UI templates.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient inability to use prescribed digital tools undermines care plans; lack of patient health/digital literacy; tools are not integrated into clinical workflow; patient anxiety caused by uninterpreted data; no reimbursement for educating patients on technology; skepticism from years of using poorly designed EHRs.
specificPoints: This group bridges the gap between technology and clinical reality. They connect poor usability directly to increased provider burden and negative clinical outcomes. They are acutely aware of the "earned skepticism" of clinicians and the need to preserve non-digital options for their most vulnerable patients.

Fund and deploy "digital health navigators"; require plain-language standards; integrate tools seamlessly into EHRs (e.g., SMART on FHIR); create billing codes for patient training; hold digital health companies responsible for product accessibility.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Commenters coined powerful terms to crystallize the user experience. Patient.com introduced "Patient Flustration™" to capture the blend of being flustered and frustrated by digital friction. Froedtert ThedaCare Health and Selfiie diagnosed the problem of managing multiple logins as "portalitis," reframing a technical issue as a systemic ailment.

b.well Connected Health provides a stunning statistic: the single biggest drop-off point for users trying to access their health records is the portal credential step, with abandonment rates approaching 75%. This quantifies the catastrophic impact of login friction.

Patient advocates offered simple, powerful heuristics for policymakers. Kelee Petzelt proposed: “Would I want my mother navigating this?” If no, the system has failed." Similarly, Grace Cordovano stated, "If a user has to take a tutorial or call their grandchild to figure it out, it’s too complicated."

An 87-year-old with dementia is unable to use MyChart, a stark example of how mainstream tools fail the most vulnerable. The CEO of Nuna, Inc. provides a detailed account of the immense cognitive load of translating for her non-English-speaking mother during doctor visits, making the need for multilingual support viscerally understandable.

Ad Hoc compellingly argues that poor design is not just frustrating, "it's dangerous when patients can't access critical health information or miss important care reminders," elevating the conversation from user experience to patient safety.

Homeward Health astutely points out that clinician skepticism towards new technology is not irrational but a direct result of "years of exposure to poorly designed digital tools (often deployed for billing or compliance)" rather than for improving clinical care.

Omada Health powerfully argues that the narrative of seniors being unwilling to use technology is false. Citing 79% smartphone use and their own data showing higher engagement from Medicare-age users, they contend the true barrier is a lack of well-designed tools and Medicare coverage for them. Ad Hoc points to the highly-rated "VA Flagship mobile app" as definitive proof that "older Americans will eagerly adopt well-designed digital tools."

An academic proposes a novel regulatory idea: creating a "Cognitive Load Index" for certified apps. This moves beyond simple accessibility checklists to a more sophisticated measure of how much mental effort a tool demands from a user, a key factor for seniors or those with cognitive impairments.

The National Association of Community Health Centers (NACHC) frames broadband access not just as a utility but as a foundational "superdeterminant of health" in the digital age, elevating its importance in health equity discussions.

mPulse provides a sophisticated warning that "Certification criteria heavily weighted toward APIs may undervalue end-to-end user experience," suggesting a potential unintended consequence where developers focus resources on technical compliance at the expense of holistic, user-centric design.

AARP makes the crucial distinction that "age-friendly design" is different from "accessibility." While accessibility features are helpful, age-friendly design is a broader concept that accounts for the unique needs and preferences of older adults, which are often overlooked by developers.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Human-in-the-Loop is the Dominant Model: Across stakeholder groups, a clear pattern emerged recommending hybrid solutions that wrap technology with human support. This "human bridge" or "human-in-the-loop" model manifests in widespread calls for funding "digital navigators," ensuring prominent access to live phone/chat support, and providing digital literacy training. Purely technological solutions are seen as insufficient.

The Rise of AI as a Panacea: A strong pattern, driven by technology vendors, is the positioning of AI (particularly LLMs) as the solution to nearly every usability problem: AI for plain language translation, AI for empathetic voice-bots, AI for data summarization, and AI for personalized navigation. This suggests a major technological shift is underway but also raises questions about validation and equity.

A Shift Beyond Access to "Actionability": A clear pattern emerged moving beyond just giving patients data to making that data actionable. This includes translating jargon, providing context for lab results, offering next steps, and making it easy to schedule appointments or ask questions based on the information presented.

Unintended Consequences of Policy: Multiple commenters highlight critical tensions and potential unintended consequences. The push for stronger security (e.g., high-assurance digital identity) directly clashes with usability goals. The push for data liberation, without a corresponding push for interpretation, can lead to patient anxiety and confusion.

Focus on Specific User Personas: Commenters are thinking beyond the generic "user." A clear pattern of caregiver-centric design emerged, emphasizing needs like proxy access and burden reduction. Likewise, a pattern of addressing the rural/urban divide appeared, with rural-focused commenters stressing the need for offline capabilities and low-bandwidth solutions.

keyQuotations.quote: Patient Flustration™. It’s the compound feeling of being both flustered and frustrated by a system that claims to be digital, yet still buries people in paperwork, portals, passwords, and phone trees.

That friction has a name, and it’s called 'portalitis', it’s the overwhelming burden we place on patients and caregivers to: Manage multiple patient portal credentials, Navigate complex multi-factor authentication workflows...
If a user has to take a tutorial or call their grandchild to figure it out, it’s too complicated.
Would I want my mother navigating this?” If no, the system has failed.
Poor design isn't just frustrating; it's dangerous when patients can't access critical health information or miss important care reminders.
Accessibility is not merely a feature—it is a precondition for meaningful engagement and adoption.
While we give patients greater access to clinical information, we must help them interpret what they are given.

In our experience, the single biggest drop off point for users wanting access to their full medical record is the portal credential step in the process. In some cases, that drop off rate approaches 75% of users...
Please keep as much analog and phone and paper based information as you can for our elderly patients.
Years of exposure to poorly designed digital tools (often deployed for billing or compliance) have left many rural clinicians skeptical of new technology.
The barrier to digital tool adoption by seniors is an absence of coverage as a Medicare benefit, not the technology itself or a lack of more regulation.
I don’t know how to do any of that. I have a nice cell phone. I have a computer, but I don’t get on it because I’m afraid somebody will steal my information.

Multi-factor, high-assurance log-ins... introduce the single biggest source of user friction: every extra selfie/driver-license scan knocks a share of seniors, rural patients, non-English speakers and privacy-conscious consumers out of the digital channel.
We have more data but we don't know what to do with it.
[I have] six different apps just to manage my diabetes, each works differently, and I can never find what I need...
For too long, we’ve made it difficult for them and then blamed them for not interacting with their data.
The technology, when designed with humanity in mind, can transform the experience of care for Medicare beneficiaries and those who support them.

5.5.themeDescription: Supporting Family Caregivers with Technology and Access

sections.executiveSummary:
Public sentiment universally and urgently affirms that family caregivers are the "invisible infrastructure" of the U.S. healthcare system—a critical, yet profoundly underserved, pillar. There is a powerful consensus that the current digital health ecosystem fundamentally fails caregivers, primarily due to fragmented data access across a sea of disconnected provider portals, a condition aptly termed "portalitis." This fragmentation creates an unsustainable administrative and emotional burden, increasing patient risk and driving caregiver burnout.

The dominant and near-unanimous recommendation is for the establishment of standardized, secure, granular, and easy-to-use proxy access. This would empower caregivers to manage a loved one's health information through a unified "single pane of glass" or dashboard. While there is broad agreement on this goal, key tensions emerge regarding implementation. A debate exists between technology vendors promoting advanced solutions like federated digital identity and users who fear such systems could add complexity. Furthermore, stakeholders are divided on the ideal scope of access—balancing the need for a comprehensive view with the need to protect patient privacy for sensitive information. A final divergence appears in the preferred mechanism for change: regulatory mandates to ensure universal adoption versus market-based incentives to spur private-sector innovation.

Ultimately, the comments call for a paradigm shift: moving from a patient-centric model to one that recognizes the "caregiver-recipient dyad" as the foundational unit of care. This involves not only creating better tools, but mandating their co-design with caregivers, establishing clear technical and privacy standards, and creating financial models that reimburse for the essential work of care coordination.

consensusPoints.text: Caregivers are a Critical, Unsupported, and "Invisible" Component of Healthcare. Nearly all commenters stated or strongly implied that family caregivers are essential to patient care, forming the "backbone" and "invisible infrastructure" of the healthcare system, but are often "unrecognized" and unsupported by current technology and policy. The National Alliance for Caregiving (NAC) and Coalition to Transform Advanced Care (C-TAC) state, "Family caregivers form the backbone of our healthcare system and economy yet often go unrecognized". An individual caregiver described the role as being part of the "invisible infrastructure... systemically ignored, unprotected, and burned out". A technology vendor concurs, stating, "Caregivers perform essential functions but are often underserved by current technology".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (at least 120 across all stakeholder types)

exceptions.text: No commenters disputed this point.

consensusPoints.text: Fragmented Data Access and "Portalitis" is the Greatest Technological Burden. A strong majority of commenters, particularly individuals, advocacy groups, and health systems, identified the need to log into numerous, disconnected patient portals as a primary source of frustration, inefficiency, and risk. The desired solution is a unified "single pane of glass." Froedtert ThedaCare Health coins the term "'portalitis', the overwhelming burden we place on patients and caregivers". A health IT consultant stated, "A unified single system portal that pulls in data from providers, payers, labs, and pharmacies would be a game-changer". An individual patient describes his wife resorting to "'hacking' multiple MyChart accounts" to consolidate his records, an "exhausting and frustrating" process. An individual caregiver asks, "How many patient portals must a caregiver attempt to gain access to?"
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, particularly individuals, advocacy groups, and health systems

exceptions.text: No commenters defended the current fragmented system.

consensusPoints.text: Standardized, Secure, and Easy-to-Use Proxy/Delegated Access is Essential. An overwhelming majority of stakeholders called for the creation of robust, standardized, and legally recognized mechanisms for caregivers to access patient information with consent. Patient advocate Grace Cordovano states, "Digital tools must support 'delegated access and shared accounts—where caregivers can view, manage, and coordinate without needing a workaround'". The National Health Council stated, "CMS must establish uniform proxy credentialing for caregivers". UnitedHealth Group recommends the national digital identity standard "allow delegation of access to caregivers/advocates". A health IT firm called for "standardized proxy access support for caregivers, including clearly defined rules for adding, managing, and canceling proxy relationships".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: An overwhelming majority of stakeholders (at least 80 commenters)

exceptions.text: While there is consensus on the need, significant debate exists on the implementation details (see Areas of Debate).

consensusPoints.text: Digital Tools Must Be Specifically Designed For and With Caregivers. A strong consensus exists among advocacy groups, technology firms, and individual users that simply providing access is insufficient; tools must be co-designed with caregivers to ensure they are usable, relevant, and solve real-world problems. Sky Solutions LLC notes that most solutions are "patient-centric, not caregiver/family-centric". Aegis Ventures recommends CMS "back grants or programs that explicitly require patient and caregiver input during the design process". Another commenter notes that when tools "fail usability standards, the result is frustration—not empowerment".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong consensus exists among advocacy groups, technology firms, and individual users

areasOfDebate.topic: Digital Identity Credentials
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether a single, federated digital ID simplifies and secures caregiver access or merely adds another layer of complexity for users.

positions.label: The Solution
positions.stance: Proposes a single, federated digital ID as the key to simplifying and securing caregiver proxy access across all systems. This position is supported by representative voices like ID.me and ASG.
positions.supportLevel: Most technology and identity management vendors (ID.me, Private Identity LLC, Massive Bio, Databricks, UnitedHealth Group).
keyArguments: Eliminates "login fatigue" from managing multiple passwords
provides a secure, auditable, and standardized way to manage delegated permissions
enables a unified identity graph for all personas (patient, caregiver, provider)

positions.label: An Added Burden
positions.stance: Argues that mandating a new digital identity credential adds another layer of complexity and friction for users without a clear, tangible benefit, especially when current systems already fail. This position is supported by representative voices like Veradigm LLC, Froedtert ThedaCare Health, and ADVION Advocates.
positions.supportLevel: At least one major health IT developer and one large health system.
keyArguments: It is "yet another account" for users to manage
users are not requesting this functionality
the extra steps negatively impact user experience, and identity systems can break down for proxy use even with legal documents like a POA.

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of Caregiver Access: Granular Control vs. Comprehensive View
areasOfDebate.description: The debate focuses on the appropriate level of data access for caregivers, balancing the need for privacy and granular control against the need for a comprehensive view for effective care coordination.

positions.label: Granular Control
positions.stance: Advocates for systems that allow patients to grant different levels of access to different caregivers for different types of data, respecting patient autonomy. This position is supported by representative voices like Nava PBC, Texas Medical Association, Mental Health America, and Schizophrenia Policy Action Network.
positions.supportLevel: Several technology firms, professional associations, and advocacy groups focused on sensitive conditions.
keyArguments: Protects patient privacy, especially for sensitive information (e.g., behavioral health, substance use, adolescent care)
allows for nuanced delegation based on trust and role
encourages use by patients who want support without full disclosure

positions.label: Comprehensive View
positions.stance: Argues that to be effective coordinators, authorized caregivers (especially for those with cognitive impairment or complex needs) require a full, consolidated view of the patient's record. This position is supported by representative voices like Element Solutions, The Sequoia Project, and The FHIR Department.
positions.supportLevel: Many commenters focused on dementia, pediatrics, and complex chronic care.
keyArguments: Caregivers act as the bridge between providers and need a complete picture to spot errors, coordinate care, and manage complex conditions
limiting information can recreate fragmentation within a supposedly unified record
legal documents like MPOA should grant full digital access

areasOfDebate.topic: Mechanism for Implementation: Mandate vs. Market
areasOfDebate.description: The debate concerns the best approach to drive change: top-down regulatory mandates to ensure universal adoption versus bottom-up market incentives to foster private-sector innovation.

positions.label: Regulatory Mandates
positions.stance: CMS should create and enforce standards for proxy access, require caregiver support features in certified EHRs and apps, and signal that caregiver support is a core requirement, not an optional feature. This position is supported by representative voices like National Health Council, an individual advocate, and Family Caregiver Alliance.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from advocacy groups and some individual caregivers.
keyArguments: Ensures universal adoption and consistency
prevents market fragmentation
avoids leaving vulnerable populations behind

positions.label: Market Incentives
positions.stance: CMS should foster innovation through grants, innovation sprints, voluntary certification programs, and new reimbursement codes to encourage the private sector to build a diverse ecosystem of caregiver-centric tools. This position is supported by representative voices like Skyward IT Solutions, KuduCare, and Aegis Ventures.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from technology vendors and industry groups.
keyArguments: Promotes competition and user-centered design
allows for more rapid and diverse solutions
avoids a one-size-fits-all government solution that may stifle innovation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The overwhelming emotional, financial, time, and administrative burden of caregiving; feeling invisible and burned out; the risk of medical errors or losing benefits due to inaccessible data; the trauma of repeatedly recounting medical histories.
specificPoints: Their comments are grounded in powerful, lived experiences, such as having to "hack" portals, managing multiple family members, spending 30+ hours a week on coordination, and being "treated as a bystander" despite being the primary health data user.

Overwhelmingly focused on simplicity and consolidation. They ask for a single place to see all records, easy ways to share access, trauma-informed protocols, and innovative financial supports like a "Caregiver Loyalty Program".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The systemic failure to recognize and integrate caregivers into health policy; health equity; the lack of training for caregivers performing complex medical tasks; the financial unsustainability of unpaid care (cited at "$600 billion").
specificPoints: They elevate individual anecdotes into systemic policy arguments, often backed by research. They introduce foundational concepts like the "caregiver-recipient dyad" and highlight the unique needs of vulnerable populations, such as those with serious mental illness, and the "disability tax" on those seeking benefits.

Comprehensive policy shifts. They call for CMS to formally recognize the dyad, mandate caregiver access in certified health IT, fund caregiver-inclusive pilots, and establish uniform proxy credentialing.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of clear technical standards for proxy access and data exchange; insufficient reimbursement models for caregiver-focused tools; legal and privacy ambiguity that stifles innovation and creates risk.
specificPoints: They view the problem through the lens of a solvable technical and market problem. They offer potential solutions, including a dedicated "Caregiver proxy API", AI-powered assistants, and interoperability platforms.

Establish clear standards (FHIR profiles, OAuth scopes for caregivers); create financial incentives (innovation grants, new reimbursement codes); leverage TEFCA and national identity frameworks to enable their products to scale.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Workflow complexity and administrative burden; navigating state-level privacy laws (especially for adolescents and mental health); ensuring digital literacy; managing the legal aspects of verifying proxy relationships.
specificPoints: They bring a pragmatic, on-the-ground view of implementation. They emphasize challenges in specific settings, like limited digital literacy in post-acute care, and the need for tools to integrate seamlessly into existing workflows.

Prioritize ease of use and alignment with digital literacy; provide clear guidance on proxy access rules; preserve adolescent privacy per state laws; implement seamless calendar integrations; fund infrastructure in underserved settings (e.g., Wi-Fi in SNFs).

noteworthyInsights.insight: The "Caregiver-Recipient Dyad" as the Unit of Care - Multiple groups independently propose a paradigm shift: instead of viewing the patient and caregiver as separate, policy should recognize the "dyad" as the foundational unit. This reframes caregiver support not as an add-on but as integral to patient care.

The "Disability Tax" on Vulnerable Patients - The National Organization of Social Security Claimants' Representatives (NOSSCR) points out a "loophole" where caregivers helping individuals apply for disability benefits are charged "hefty fees" for medical records. They powerfully frame this as an "unconscionable" "disability tax".

The Trauma of Repetitive Storytelling - Imagine Pediatrics provides a caregiver's account of how data fragmentation is not just an administrative burden but a source of repeated psychological harm: "It’s very frustrating to continue to have to repeat the same information. Not only that, it’s triggering... my boys have to sit with me and revisit the same traumas over and over".

Making "Invisible Work" Visible with AI - A health informaticist articulates a vision for AI to capture, structure, and integrate the "invisible caregiving work"—the daily observations and coordination tasks that are currently lost—and make this data "visible and actionable within health systems."

The Adolescent Privacy "Black Hole" - A+J Patient Advocacy identifies a critical regulatory failure: for minors aged 12-17, parents are often denied electronic access due to privacy laws, yet minors have no mechanism to grant it, forcing patients to "start their medical record keeping at ground zero" at age 18.

Caregiver Needs in Serious Mental Illness (SMI) - The Schizophrenia Policy Action Network (SPAN) offers a unique perspective on the challenges for caregivers of those with SMI, particularly anosognosia (lack of insight into one's illness), which leads patients to refuse care while caregivers are locked out of information systems.

Innovative "Caregiver Loyalty Program" - An individual caregiver proposed a novel incentive structure where family members could track participation in care tasks and receive rewards like tax credits or HSA reimbursements, formally valuing caregiver engagement.

Compelling Personal Example of System Failure - The story from patient Tim Moon of his wife, a health information exchange professional, having to "hack" multiple EHR portals and manually transfer PDFs to prevent him from losing disability benefits, is a damning illustration of how the current system fails even experts.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Caregivers as "Invisible Infrastructure": A powerful narrative emerged, with multiple commenters independently describing caregivers as the "invisible infrastructure", the "invisible frontline", and the "de facto care managers", underscoring their critical but unsupported role.

From Patient-Centric to Dyad-Centric Design: A clear evolution in thinking is visible, moving beyond a "patient-centered" model to one that is "family-centered" or "dyad-centered." This reflects an understanding that care is a collaborative act that technology must support.

Aspiration vs. Real-World Usability: A significant pattern is the gap between the advanced solutions proposed by technology vendors (AI, complex platforms) and the fundamental needs expressed by users (a single, reliable login). Technical compliance with standards is not synonymous with usability.

The 'One-Size-Fits-All' Fallacy: A clear pattern emerged that caregiver tools must address the distinct needs of vulnerable sub-populations, including caregivers of children with special healthcare needs, adults with dementia, individuals with serious mental illness, and patients in post-acute and long-term care.

Unintended Consequences of Interoperability Frameworks: A sharp critique emerges that national frameworks like TEFCA are "flawed by design" because they do not treat proxy access as a foundational requirement, thereby designing a system for "theoretical patients—not real families".

"Care Circles" Beyond the Family: Commenters recognize that modern caregiving often extends beyond a single family member to a "care circle" of friends and neighbors, and technology must support multiple authorized users with different roles and permissions.

keyQuotations.quote: [If] caregivers remain unsupported, healthcare becomes unsustainable for everyone. We are the invisible infrastructure holding up your outcomes data, your readmission metrics, and your treatment adherence. Yet we are systemically ignored, unprotected, and burned out.
Without caregiver inclusion, we’re designing a system for theoretical patients—not real families.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Digital Health Policy Expert, Parent Advocate, Caregiver

keyQuotations.quote: That friction has a name, and it’s called 'portalitis', it’s the overwhelming burden we place on patients and caregivers...

[The] disconnect between health IT systems and human reality represents more than medication confusion—it’s one symptom of a larger crisis: the massive coordination gap between what medical providers document and what family caregivers actually manage daily.
keyQuotations.sourceType: MSHI, AI Strategist, Health Informaticist, Family Caregiver

keyQuotations.quote: Because of a loophole in the existing laws, these helpers... are charged hefty fees simply to obtain the needed health data—fees that are ultimately passed on directly to the patient. In effect, this is a 'disability tax.'”
The caregiver is still largely invisible in our system.
The existing system too often reduces them to 'authorized representatives,' when in truth they are the de facto care managers for millions of Medicare beneficiaries.
How many patient portals must a caregiver attempt to gain access to?
The single most important problem that a digital health product could personally solve for me would be to make it easier to manage the healthcare received by my elderly loved ones...

Caregivers of people living with schizophrenia serve as the de facto care coordinators, case managers, and crisis responders for their loved ones. Yet, these caregivers are often excluded from patient portals, provider communications, and digital navigation tools.

From our perspective, there is little perceived benefit to patients or caregivers in the broader adoption of digital identity credentials... it is often viewed as an additional requirement that adds complexity without delivering clear or tangible value.
Even when patients willingly share log-in credentials—something they should not have to do—dual authentication and security barriers make consistent access difficult or impossible.
Recognize the caregiver-recipient dyad as a foundational unit of care.
Proxy access must be a baseline, not a feature.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Digital Health Policy Expert, Parent Advocate, Caregiver

keyQuotations.quote: The most transformative digital health applications would support the complex medical tasks that fifty-eight percent of caregivers now perform... Despite providing these critical nursing-level services... only seven percent report receiving any formal training.

Older adults and family caregivers seeking to use technology for health and home care must typically stitch together suites of devices and apps, and much of the data generated doesn’t reach health-care providers.
AI's greatest opportunity lies in making this invisible caregiving work visible and actionable within health systems.
keyQuotations.sourceType: MSHI, AI Strategist, Health Informaticist, Family Caregiver

keyQuotations.quote: “Care coordination should not depend solely on caregiver capacity. We need tools that treat families as partners — not data entry clerks — and shift part of the system’s responsibility back to those being paid to deliver care."

6.1.themeDescription: Opposition to Mandatory Digital IDs and Technologies

sections.executiveSummary: Public feedback on mandatory digital IDs reveals a stark divide between two forms of opposition. A significant and highly vocal contingent of individuals and advocacy groups expresses absolute, philosophical opposition, viewing any mandate as an intolerable step towards government control, surveillance, and loss of constitutional freedoms. In contrast, healthcare organizations, technology vendors, and professional associations, while also opposing mandates, frame their concerns pragmatically, focusing on the high risks of creating access barriers for vulnerable populations, the immense security vulnerabilities of centralized data, and the prohibitive costs and administrative burdens on the healthcare system.

consensusPoints.text: Any Implementation Must Be Voluntary and Include Robust Opt-Outs. Nearly all commenters who entertained the possibility of digital IDs, from professional associations to technology vendors and advocacy groups, agreed that any system must be optional. They stress that a clear, accessible opt-out pathway that guarantees equal access to care is a non-negotiable prerequisite.

exceptions.text: The only exceptions are the many individual commenters who reject the premise entirely and oppose even an optional system.

consensusPoints.text: Mandatory Digital IDs Will Create or Worsen Health Inequities. A strong majority of stakeholders, including provider groups, technology companies, and patient advocates, concur that mandating digital IDs would erect significant barriers to care for vulnerable populations. They consistently identify the elderly, low-income individuals, people with disabilities, those with low digital literacy, and those lacking internet access or modern identification as groups who would be disproportionately harmed.

Centralized Biometric Databases Pose Unacceptable Security Risks. A wide range of commenters, from the AMA to private industry and health systems, express grave concerns about the security of centralized biometric hubs. The consensus is that creating a single, massive repository of immutable data (like fingerprints or facial scans) creates an irresistible target for malicious actors and a single point of failure with catastrophic potential if breached.

areasOfDebate.topic: Fundamental Approach to Digital ID
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether digital identity systems should be outright banned due to fundamental risks to liberty, or if they can be cautiously explored as long as they are voluntary and built with strong safeguards.

positions.label: Total Prohibition
