For example, the chronic pain patient who fears being labeled a "noncompliant outlier" by database algorithms; the individual who compares the risk to the weaponization of IRS data.

Proposed solutions are often absolute, such as outright rejection of any government data collection, mandates, or digital IDs. Some propose specific policy changes like requiring opt-in for data sharing or revising HIPAA.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Building trust to ensure system viability; security risks of centralized platforms; establishing clear, privacy-preserving standards.
specificPoints: Views the problem as a technical and architectural challenge. They are acutely aware of the public's distrust and propose technological solutions (federated models, homomorphic tokens) to mitigate it. They also raise concerns about the practices of other vendors, such as data monetization. For example, LexisNexis Risk Solutions warned about the privacy concerns of centralized federated identity; CareCard, Inc. demanded that digital ID scope be strictly limited to prevent surveillance or commercial reuse.
Overwhelmingly advocate for non-centralized, federated identity systems and privacy-preserving infrastructures.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Repurposing of data for surveillance or marketing (AMA); risk of linking sensitive data (AARP, Family Caregiver Alliance); control of data by vendors with defense/surveillance ties (The Light Collective); creation of a "cradle-to-grave" record via a UPI (Citizens' Council for Health Freedom).
specificPoints: Act as highly focused critics, often representing large constituencies (patients, doctors, older Americans) and bringing specific, well-articulated risks to the forefront. For example, the AMA's stark warning that centralized hubs create a "single trove of immutable data" for surveillance; AARP's position that combining claims with personal health product data is "unadvisable" because it "sows" distrust.
Proposed solutions range from technical (WEDI's decentralized directory) to policy-based (restricting vendors, AMA's warnings against centralized hubs).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Government overreach; constitutional limits; potential for scope creep in identity systems like Real ID.
specificPoints: It is striking that commenters identifying as government entities or employees express the same deep distrust of federal data aggregation as individuals. They provide an "inside" view on the potential for political misuse. For example, the anonymous federal employee who fears a "seamless data trove" will be used by administrations to "target their 'opponents'".
The California Department of Technology explicitly recommends a federated architecture specifically to avoid federal government overreach.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Sophisticated critique of "Surveillance Capitalism": Michael Millenson provided a nuanced warning not just about government surveillance, but about providers using commercially available, non-HIPAA protected data (shopping habits, online searches) and proprietary AI to make health inferences, terming it a "questionable form of surveillance capitalism."

Compelling personal example of data misuse: A chronic pain patient described how data is already being shared between agencies to identify "noncompliant outliers," with prosecutors using these "lists" to obtain convictions. This provided a concrete, chilling example of the fears many others expressed abstractly.

Government entity advocating against government overreach: The California Department of Technology argued for a federated identity system specifically because it "avoid[s] government overreach by preventing CMS or HHS from becoming the sole arbiter of digital identity." This is a powerful endorsement for decentralization from an unexpected source.

The "Post-COVID" Context: Several commenters explicitly framed their distrust in the context of the "post-Covid era," indicating that recent experiences with mandates and vaccine registries have hardened opposition and given concrete form to previously abstract fears about government data collection.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Technical vs. Ideological Divide: A clear pattern emerged between stakeholders who view this as a technical design problem to be solved (with federated architecture) and those who view it as a fundamental, ideological threat where any form of universal digital linkage is a dangerous slippery slope.

Mistrust Extends to the Private Sector: While government was the primary target of concern, there is a strong secondary pattern of distrust aimed at the private sector, specifically for-profit technology vendors who may monetize data, and companies with ties to defense and surveillance contracting.

The "Slippery Slope" Argument is Prevalent: Many commenters view any move toward a digital ID or streamlined data access not as an isolated policy but as a foundational step toward a future of greater control, linking it to Real ID, vaccine passports, and other systems of monitoring.

Unintended Consequences of Data Linkage: A recurring pattern was the fear of "unintended linkage" where data, once connected, could be used for purposes never intended, such as denying insurance coverage or restricting services.

keyQuotations.quote: [P]utting data in the names of the Federal Government only allows the data to be weaponized – like IRS Data.
We know certain administrations will target their 'opponents' with this information.
Centralized biometric hubs create a single trove of immutable data that can be repurposed for surveillance or marketing.
Mistaking surveillance for support.
The logical conclusion of the government gathering detailed health information is that it can and will be used by people both domestic and foreign who will use it for reasons unrelated to health.
This will only end up with our government being able to control what we do and shut us off when we don't toe the line.
Data has been used to identify doctors and patients as noncompliant outliers yet the algorithms used to create the database search engine unavailable and its classified information.
Support non-centralized, privacy-preserving infrastructures like federated learning.
The paradigm of the NHCD changes from a 'single source of truth' to a connected and distributed single source of truth and mitigates the risk of the NHCD becoming just one more federal database.
What Palantir wants to do for the Deep State and the Ultra Wealthy is use our information against us.

A UPI would create a national patient identification number for every person in America. This unconstitutional federal number would be used to track patients, create a lifelong, fully linked, cradle-to-grave medical record...
It is dangerous, unethical, and highly concerning that the federal government who is supposed to uphold citizen's Constitutional rights, wants to put this in place.

Any patient digital ID system should be explicitly limited in scope, to be used only to verify identity for accessing health records or services, 'not for broader surveillance, behavior profiling, or commercial reuse.'
These are tactics used against enemies of the state.
I certainly don’t want the government to be a sole gateway for all our health data.

sections.executiveSummary:
Public input on Theme 7.1 reveals a unanimous and urgent consensus: the administrative burden from clinical documentation and quality reporting has reached a crisis point, serving as a primary driver of clinician burnout, eroding patient care, and creating profound systemic waste. The sentiment is not one of mere complaint but of existential threat to the healthcare workforce, with clinicians spending more time on computers than with patients.

There is overwhelming agreement that technology—specifically AI-driven automation and standardized data exchange via Bulk FHIR—is the only viable path forward. Commenters across all stakeholder groups envision a future where AI scribes auto-populate notes, data captured once for clinical care is seamlessly reused for all administrative purposes, and manual processes like prior authorization are fully automated.

However, this technological optimism is tempered by deep-seated skepticism and significant debate over implementation. While technology vendors advocate for rapid, mandated adoption of new standards, healthcare providers, burned by past initiatives like Meaningful Use, issue a stark warning: premature mandates of immature, poorly integrated technologies will only exacerbate the burden. This central tension shapes the entire discussion.

The dominant recommendations call for a two-pronged approach led by CMS: first, to radically simplify and harmonize the underlying regulatory landscape by aligning quality measures and data requirements across all public and private payers; and second, to orchestrate a careful, incentive-driven transition to new technology. A major point of debate centers on the operational model for quality reporting, with a strong push for CMS to centralize measure calculation by pulling raw data from EHRs, thereby relieving providers of that complex task. Opponents, particularly specialty societies, caution that this could sacrifice the clinical context and value-added feedback provided by intermediaries like registries. Ultimately, the collective message is a plea for smarter, not just more, technology, embedded invisibly within workflows and underpinned by simplified, harmonized rules.

consensusPoints.text: The Administrative Burden is at a Crisis Level - Virtually every commenter identifies excessive documentation and reporting as a critical, unsustainable problem. This burden is universally linked to severe clinician burnout, reduced time with patients, workforce attrition, and systemic inefficiency. This view is held by a broad coalition of healthcare workers, administrators, tech vendors, payers, and patient advocates.

exceptions.text: A nurse advocate cites a study that "For some physicians, 90% of their eight-hour shifts are spent on EHRs" and "Nurses spend up to 41% of their workdays in EHRs". Ateev Mehrotra, MD, notes that "EHR tasks consuming nearly half of clinic time and often spilling outside clinic hours," leading to "pajama time". A patient describes the process of obtaining medical records for disability benefits as so "exhausting and frustrating" that he "almost lost my disability benefit". A home health professional states, "Our care providers’ paperwork burden has exponentially increased with each new implementation".

consensusPoints.text: Automation and Technology are the Primary Solutions - A strong majority of commenters see technological solutions as the key to reducing burden. There is broad enthusiasm for using AI for ambient scribing and note generation, automating quality reporting via APIs like Bulk FHIR, and digitizing manual processes like prior authorization and FMLA forms.

exceptions.text: The Blue Cross Blue Shield Association states that "NLP and machine learning (ML)" can "reduce documentation burden and automate chart review". Ochsner Health believes "Ambient Listening can reduce administrative burdens by freeing physicians from being 'tethered' to the EHR". The American College of Physicians states that "Advancements in EHR technology allowing bulk extraction of clinical quality data are promising and should be pursued".

consensusPoints.text: Standardization and Harmonization are Essential - A vast majority of stakeholders, especially provider organizations, health systems, and professional societies, agree that burden reduction is impossible without greater standardization. This includes widespread calls for aligning quality measures across all payers and programs (CMS, MA, commercial), adopting universal standards like FHIR for data exchange, and creating a "collect once, use many times" framework.

exceptions.text: Deloitte shares a stark example where a health system must calculate "‘four different definitions of death’" for various reporting requirements. NACHC notes that Community Health Centers report "nearly identical quality measures... multiple times to multiple entities with minor variations." The National Committee for Quality Assurance (NCQA) advocates to "Sunset QDM and QRDA in favor of US Core and QI-Core profiles" to reduce the burden of outdated standards.

consensusPoints.text: Prior Authorization is an Acute Pain Point - A significant and diverse group of commenters specifically single out prior authorization (PA) as an excessively burdensome, often manual process that delays care and harms patients. There is broad agreement on the need to automate and streamline PA workflows using standardized, FHIR-based APIs.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A significant and diverse group of commenters

exceptions.text: The Association for Clinical Oncology (ASCO) states that "Prior authorization is consistently identified as the largest barrier to care for insured patients," especially for life-saving drugs. Payer association AHIP agrees that the process can be "burdensome" and supports advancing "electronic prior authorization (ePA) through technology". An AOA survey shows 94% of physicians report PA causes care delays.

areasOfDebate.topic: Quality Reporting Methodology: Centralized vs. Intermediary vs. Provider-Controlled Models

positions.label: Centralized Calculation at CMS
positions.stance: CMS should receive raw clinical data via Bulk FHIR and perform all quality measure calculations.
positions.supportLevel: Strongly supported by a majority of commenters, including most technology vendors, many health systems, and some professional associations.
keyArguments: Dramatically reduces provider reporting burden by offloading the complex and costly task of building and maintaining calculation infrastructure; ensures consistency and standardization in how measures are calculated across all entities; allows providers to focus on care, not reporting mechanics.

positions.label: Registry/Intermediary Model
positions.stance: Leverage existing specialty registries, Qualified Clinical Data Registries (QCDRs), and Health Information Exchanges (HIEs) to collect, curate, and submit quality data.
positions.supportLevel: A significant minority, led by specialty medical societies and HIEs.
keyArguments: Registries provide essential benchmarking, research, and quality improvement feedback that raw data submission to CMS would lack; they normalize data from various EHRs, provide specialty-specific context, and shield individual practices from technical complexity; HIEs can aggregate data across multiple providers for a more complete picture.

positions.label: Maintain Provider Control
positions.stance: Centralized calculation raises significant risks and the burden of calculation should remain with providers and their vendors until these are resolved.
positions.supportLevel: While few advocate for this as a better long-term system, an influential minority, including the largest physician associations (AMA) and some EHR vendors, raise critical concerns about the readiness of a centralized model.
keyArguments: Providers lose visibility and control over data submitted on their behalf; feedback loops from a central calculator are often slow or nonexistent, preventing real-time quality improvement; data completeness and quality from EHRs are not yet reliable enough for this model, risking inaccurate assessments.

areasOfDebate.topic: Pace and Method of Technology Adoption

positions.label: Mandate & Accelerate
positions.stance: CMS should aggressively mandate new standards (e.g., Bulk FHIR, dQMs, FHIR-based PA APIs) to force industry-wide adoption and overcome inertia.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily supported by technology vendors and interoperability-focused groups.
keyArguments: Without mandates, adoption will be slow and inconsistent, perpetuating the status quo; strong federal requirements create a level playing field and a clear business case for investment.

positions.label: Incentivize, Phase-In & Ensure Readiness First
positions.stance: The transition to new technologies is complex and expensive; CMS should use a more flexible, incentive-based approach with realistic timelines, delaying mandates until standards are mature and workflows are proven.
positions.supportLevel: Strongly supported by the majority of hospitals, health systems, and provider professional associations.
keyArguments: Premature adoption of unproven technology increases burden and costs; one-size-fits-all mandates ignore the financial and technical limitations of smaller or rural facilities; past experiences (e.g., Meaningful Use) show that rushed mandates create poorly designed tools that can "add to clinician burden rather than reducing it" (Roji Health Intelligence).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Extreme burnout from "pajama time" spent on documentation outside work hours; time stolen from direct patient care; being forced to act as "human APIs"; frustration with poorly designed EHRs, "useless quality metrics", and specific administrative pain points like FMLA forms.
specificPoints: They provide the most visceral, ground-level accounts of the human cost of administrative burden, framing it as an existential threat to their professions and a direct impediment to the patient relationship. They detail how specific tasks, like the 110-question OASIS assessment, are "redundant and excessive".

Want solutions seamlessly embedded in workflow, not more clicks or logins. Strong support for AI-driven note generation, simplified and aligned quality measures, digitized manual forms, and compensating for unbilled work like "in-basket" management.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The high operational costs of compliance (e.g., $40-60M annually for a 300-bed hospital); unrealistic federal timelines; the burden of navigating a patchwork of unaligned reporting requirements; and the immense difficulty and cost of implementing new mandates and aggregating data from disparate EHR systems.
specificPoints: They focus on the immense operational and financial challenges of system-wide change. While supportive of burden reduction, they provide a pragmatic and risk-averse reality check on the difficult reality of implementation, highlighting the gap between the promise of new technology and the chaos it can create if not carefully planned.

Longer timelines for new rules; more regulatory flexibility, pilot programs, and "safe harbors"; strong financial incentives; federal leadership to harmonize reporting requirements across all payers; and centralizing quality metric calculation at CMS.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of clear, standardized technical requirements from CMS; fragmented systems and payer rules that hinder scalability; burdensome certification processes; provider resistance to adopting new tools without a clear business case or incentive.
specificPoints: They see every problem as a market opportunity for their specific technology (AI, data platforms, interoperability hubs). Their comments are rich with technical detail and specific product-based solutions, presenting a vision of a highly automated, efficient future.
Mandate adoption of their preferred standards (APIs, Bulk FHIR); reimburse for use of AI tools; have CMS create clear implementation guides; create a "compelling use case" to drive provider adoption.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Synthesizing member frustrations about fragmented reporting, prior authorization burdens, and poorly designed EHRs. They are deeply concerned that new policies, while well-intentioned, will create more unfunded mandates and administrative work, disproportionately harming small and independent practices.
specificPoints: They act as a crucial bridge, translating on-the-ground frustrations into specific, data-backed policy recommendations. They cite member surveys and formal studies to back their claims and often raise the most detailed and nuanced objections to proposed solutions.

Harmonize quality measures across all payers before mandating new reporting tech; rescind burdensome MIPS PI and CEHRT requirements for APM participants; fully enforce and strengthen PA API rules; provide incentives and technical assistance for small practices.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of direct access to provider EHRs, which they see as the root cause of burdensome back-and-forth for prior authorization, quality reporting, and risk adjustment; the proliferation of unaligned quality measures.
specificPoints: They frame provider burden as a problem that they could help solve if only they had better, more direct access to clinical data.

Mandate that EHR vendors provide payers with remote EMR access at no extra cost; make APIs bidirectional; align quality measures across all payers through initiatives like the Core Quality Measures Collaborative (CQMC).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The immense, often invisible, burden placed on patients and caregivers to manually manage paperwork, obtain medical records ("kill the clipboard"), and navigate fragmented systems, which can directly jeopardize access to care and benefits. This burden is described as a "full-time job".
specificPoints: They highlight the "human friction" of the system, describing the exhaustion and desperation of navigating processes designed for institutions, not people. Their stories reveal how administrative burdens directly impact patients' economic stability and ability to manage their health.

A "write-once, share-everywhere" data model for patient information; digitizing and standardizing all patient-facing forms (FMLA, disability, consent); giving patients tools to aggregate and share their own health data; streamlining data requests from government agencies like the SSA.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The "Human API" Problem: Jean Ross of Primary Record powerfully frames the issue by stating that highly skilled professionals like nurses are being used as "'human APIs' to manually retrieve, translate, and relay clinical data that should be automated."

The "Other Pocket Problem": Open City Labs provides a powerful mental model, arguing that federal agencies often optimize their individual compliance requirements without considering the crushing cumulative burden on providers, sometimes causing compliance costs to "exceed program benefits."

The "F Grade" for Usability: The Health Care Transformation Task Force cites a national study giving EHRs a standardized usability score of 45.9 out of 100—an "F" grade. This quantifiable failure metric provides a stark contrast to the technology's promise and explains much of the user frustration.

The "AI-Induced Workload" Counterpoint: FHIR IQ offers a critical caution that if AI models are trained on currently flawed data, they will produce flawed outputs, creating a new "AI-induced workload" for clinicians who must spend time verifying and correcting AI-generated content.

The Invisible Burden on Patients and Caregivers: Comments from patient advocates like Grace Cordovano and a TBI survivor shine a critical light on the immense, often unacknowledged, labor that patients perform to manually assemble records and fight for benefits—a burden that is "cruel and inhumane."

EHRs Built for Billing, Not Care: Selfiie astutely notes that "the most valuable data is the narrative," which was lost when EHRs moved to templates and checkboxes for billing. Aegis Ventures concurs, stating today's infrastructure is "geared toward documentation and billing for existing care, not proactive prevention."

Data-Backed Cost of Reporting: Mathematica cites research that "Physician practices in the United States spend more than $15.4 billion annually on quality measure reporting," putting a staggering price tag on the problem.

Data-Backed "Physician Abrasion": Consumers' Checkbook quantifies "physician abrasion"—the work clinicians do to satisfy convoluted insurer payment rules—as consuming over 3 hours of physician time per week and costing the system $23-$31 billion annually.

The Infeasibility of Data Completeness Thresholds: The American Medical Association delivers a meticulous deconstruction of CMS's MIPS data completeness threshold, revealing a profound disconnect between policy assumptions and practice reality where physicians are penalized for systemic interoperability failures beyond their control.

The Paradox of Data Sharing and Liability: JSL Health Capital points out a critical tension: "Aggressive data sharing requirements without adequate safeguards may paradoxically lead to underdocumentation, as providers become reluctant to create records that might be inappropriately shared or misinterpreted."

Concrete Human Impact of FMLA Paperwork: A nurse with over a decade of experience with stroke patients provides a powerful, ground-level perspective, pointing out that delays in completing FMLA forms directly lead to patients risking job loss during their most vulnerable moments.

Misaligned Incentives in Quality Reporting: The Trebuchet subgroup astutely points out that "Virtually all Promoting Interoperability requirements are quality focused, not improper payment focused," highlighting a disconnect where documentation standards aimed at one goal do not address other significant sources of administrative burden.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Technology as a Double-Edged Sword: A clear pattern of tension exists between the promise of technology and the experience of it. While vendors and futurists see automation as the solution, providers point to past initiatives (like Meaningful Use) as proof that poorly implemented or prematurely mandated tech becomes part of the problem, increasing clicks and disrupting workflows.

Unintended Consequences of Past Policy: Multiple commenters note that previous well-intentioned efforts have backfired. The 1995 Paperwork Reduction Act was cited as having increased charting time, the HITECH Act was blamed for excluding mental health providers, and the recent requirement for MSSP ACOs to report MIPS PI measures was identified by numerous, independent commenters as a new policy that increases, rather than reduces, burden.

Disproportionate Burden on Certain Groups: A strong theme is that administrative burdens are not felt equally. Small practices, rural facilities, safety-net providers (e.g., Community Health Centers), and post-acute care facilities consistently express deep anxiety about their ability to afford and implement new technology mandates, fearing they will be left behind.

Specialty-Specific Burdens: The one-size-fits-all nature of EHRs and reporting programs creates unique and severe burdens for different specialties. Anesthesiologists have workflows that don't fit MIPS, behavioral health providers navigate irrelevant medical-centric data elements, and dentists lack integration with medical systems.

Prior Authorization as the Flashpoint: While the overall theme is documentation, the administrative burden of prior authorization is the single most frequently and passionately cited sub-issue, acting as a universal symbol of administrative dysfunction across nearly every provider type.

“Kill the Clipboard” and End the Fax Machine: The continued reliance on patients manually filling out repetitive forms (“the clipboard”) and the use of fax machines are recurring symbols of the system's inefficiency, mentioned by stakeholders across the spectrum as tangible, solvable problems.

keyQuotations.quote: [Clinicians are being used as] 'human APIs' to manually retrieve, translate, and relay clinical data that should be automated and accessible via certified patient access channels.

One representative from a large health system speaking at a national meeting for a CMMI APM reported needing to calculate ‘four different definitions of death’ for various VBC and other reporting requirements.
[For some physicians, 90% of their eight-hour shifts are spent on EHRs. Nurses spend up to 41% of their workdays in EHRs.]
A national study of 5,197 physicians found EHRs scored 45.9 out of 100 on standardized usability measures – an 'F' grade compared to Google Search (93) or Microsoft Excel (57).
Physician practices in the United States spend more than $15.4 billion annually on quality measure reporting.
The HHS 'Other Pocket Problem': When Compliance Costs Undermine Program Goals

It’s cruel and inhumane to expect our most vulnerable of patients and our communities that experience the greatest gaps in care to perform these tasks manually when the future of operationalizing our health information is here today.
Physicians spend up to twice as much time interacting with EHRs as with patients.
TIMELY SUBMISSION OF FMLA PAPERWORK IS CRITICAL IN PREVENTING UNNECESSARY UNEMPLOYMENT.
The current quality model based on HEDIS, HCC coding, and payor charts rewards documentation over care delivery, wasting billions of taxpayer (patient) dollars.
Reporting PI measures and meeting required objectives for this program are extremely burdensome and will serve only as a check-the-box exercise and not add any value to patients.
Are you kidding me? And, this specification is used to extract quality data and send it to another system, when this isn’t something that needs to be done in the ambulatory world.
Prior authorization is consistently identified as the largest barrier to care for insured patients.
Most of the abrasion providers experience in the prior authorization process... could immediately be avoided if payors were simply given remote access to the EMR.
ACOs frequently have to work directly with EHR vendors to build data extracts, which can cost anywhere from $5,000 to $45,000... a significant hurdle for small and independent practices.
In a poll of APM participants, 94% of MGMA members reported that moving to value-based care initiatives has not lessened the regulatory burden on their practices.
DTR, in its current form, imposes a substantial implementation burden on integrated systems like Kaiser Permanente without a corresponding benefit for providers or patients.
I was shocked to find that I couldn’t just export my medical information and upload it when applying for SSDI.
The current process to gain disability status is so complex that many must hire a lawyer—an impossible step for those already living on the financial edge.

Until problems like these are comprehensively addressed within the government’s reporting systems... widespread adoption of applications which allow for direct reporting of quality data will not occur.
CMS policies should ensure interoperability is intuitive, invisible, and beneficial.

7.2.themeDescription: Addressing EHR and Health IT Costs and Complexity

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on health IT costs and complexity is one of profound and near-universal frustration. Stakeholders from all sectors describe the current financial and operational burdens as unsustainable, creating a deepening digital divide that threatens the viability of small, rural, safety-net, and specialty providers. Commenters identify two primary drivers of this crisis: the exorbitant cost of acquiring, maintaining, and integrating technology, and the anti-competitive practices of incumbent EHR vendors who leverage their market power to impose excessive fees for basic data access. The historical exclusion of many provider types (e.g., post-acute, behavioral health) from HITECH Act incentives is consistently cited as a root cause of today's systemic inequities. The key tension in the input is not in diagnosing the problem, but in assigning responsibility for the solution. However, a dominant, cross-stakeholder consensus emerges, calling for urgent and robust federal intervention through a dual approach: 1) provide targeted financial and technical assistance, akin to a HITECH 2.0, to support under-resourced providers; and 2) implement stronger regulatory controls and enforcement to curb vendor price-gouging, mandate transparent pricing, and foster a more competitive, innovative, and truly interoperable market.

consensusPoints.text: Exorbitant EHR/IT Costs are a Prohibitive Barrier, Creating a Digital Divide.

exceptions.text: No commenters disputed the existence of high costs. However, some large technology vendors argued their platforms could reduce the total cost of ownership by replacing disparate systems, while EHR vendors often pointed to the high cost of meeting complex, unfunded regulatory mandates as a key driver of their prices.

consensusPoints.text: Vendor-Imposed Fees and Practices Significantly Inflate Costs and Stifle Innovation.

exceptions.text: No commenters directly defended these practices. EHR vendors and their associations countered that development costs are driven by complex and unfunded regulatory mandates and that providers are often unwilling to pay for capabilities that don't offer them a direct, tangible ROI.

consensusPoints.text: The HITECH Act's Exclusionary Incentives Created a Lasting Digital Divide.

exceptions.text: None noted. This point is a widely held and consistently articulated historical grievance used to explain current disparities.

areasOfDebate.topic: Best Approach to Correct Market Failures

positions.label: Regulate and Mandate
positions.stance: The government should use its regulatory authority (CEHRT, information blocking) to force vendors to include interoperability functions in base products, prohibit unreasonable fees, and mandate transparent, cost-based API pricing.
positions.supportLevel: A vast majority of providers, professional associations, and non-EHR technology innovators.
keyArguments: The market has failed to correct anti-competitive behavior from dominant vendors who act as gatekeepers. Direct regulatory intervention is the only way to ensure fair access and prevent price-gouging at the source.

positions.label: Incentivize and Compete
positions.stance: The government should promote competition through open standards, funding for shared infrastructure, and market-based incentives, which will naturally drive down costs and break vendor lock-in.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily large technology companies (e.g., Google, Amazon) and open-source advocates.
keyArguments: Overly prescriptive regulation can stifle innovation and create new burdens. The best way to combat "walled gardens" is to foster a competitive, open ecosystem where providers have real choices, forcing vendors to compete on price and functionality.

areasOfDebate.topic: Best Approach to Support Under-Resourced Providers

positions.label: Direct Financial Aid
positions.stance: Provide direct grants, subsidies, low-interest loans, and other financial incentives to offset the high costs of acquisition, upgrades, and integration.
positions.supportLevel: A large group of commenters, including many health systems, provider groups, and technology companies.
keyArguments: The primary barrier is financial, so direct funding is the most straightforward and urgently needed solution to get technology into the hands of providers.

positions.label: Hands-on Technical Assistance
positions.stance: Provide structured, expert technical assistance, modeled on past successful programs like the Regional Extension Centers (RECs).
positions.supportLevel: A significant group of commenters, particularly professional associations and health IT experts.
keyArguments: Giving money is not enough if organizations lack the expertise to use it effectively or sustain the improvements. A trusted, hands-on advisory model is needed to help small practices with vendor selection, implementation, and workflow redesign.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Existential financial pressures from EHR costs; exorbitant and often unexpected vendor fees for basic data access and integration; a lack of on-site technical expertise; being historically ignored by policymakers and left out of federal incentives.
specificPoints: For this group, the issue is not about optimizing IT, but about survival. They feel abandoned by policy and see the high costs and complexity as a direct threat to their independence and ability to serve vulnerable populations, creating a fundamental care disparity.

A resounding and urgent call for direct financial assistance (grants, subsidies), free or subsidized technical assistance programs (e.g., a revived Health IT Extension Program), and strong regulation to curb predatory vendor fees.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: High cost of upgrading legacy systems and integrating new digital health tools; the immense operational burden of aggregating data for ACOs and quality reporting; the lack of a clear return on investment (ROI) for interoperability projects; expanding certification requirements increasing costs.
specificPoints: They have EHRs but are often trapped by high maintenance costs, vendor restrictions, and the complexity of managing disparate platforms. They often bear the cost of data exchange for smaller community partners and must absorb the cost of digital tools for their patients due to lack of reimbursement.

Subsidies for technology acquisition and integration; upfront investments for ACOs to build IT capabilities; standardization to reduce custom builds; addressing resource disparities between large and small hospitals.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: (Challenger Vendors) Anti-competitive "walled garden" practices by dominant EHRs, including vendor lock-in, exorbitant API fees, and proprietary systems that function as "tollgates," stifling competition. (Incumbent Vendors) The high cost and burden of meeting ever-changing, unfunded, and sometimes misaligned certification mandates.
specificPoints: This is a fractured group. Incumbent EHR vendors feel squeezed between regulatory demands and provider unwillingness to pay for features without a clear ROI. "Challenger" tech companies see incumbent vendors as the primary obstacle to innovation and position themselves as allies of providers, advocating for open standards and stronger regulation to level the playing field.

(Challengers) Mandate cost-based pricing for APIs; strengthen information blocking enforcement; promote open standards and modular, API-first certification. (Incumbents) Better alignment between CMS payment policy and ONC technology rules; more flexibility in certification.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Aggregating their members' concerns, focusing on the disproportionate burden on small/rural practices; the lack of a clear ROI for IT investments; vendor-imposed fees that undermine care and quality initiatives.
specificPoints: They bridge the gap between frontline provider experiences and high-level policy, often providing stark financial examples and proposing specific, structured federal programs.

Almost universally advocate for federal financial and technical assistance (often a revived Health IT Extension Program), stronger regulation of the EHR marketplace, and incentives that align with the real-world costs borne by their members.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Cost as an Artificial Barrier: The American Board of Family Medicine powerfully argues that "Cost is not trivial, but it too is a created barrier more than a real one." They assert that vendors increasingly use cost as a strategic business practice to inhibit interoperability, reframing the issue from an unavoidable technical expense to a deliberate tactic.

"Hands-on Assistance" Trumps "Blank Checks": Health IT expert Susan Houck Clark provides a compelling argument that simply giving incentive money to under-resourced organizations is ineffective. They often lack the expertise to use it wisely and fear they cannot sustain the improvements, advocating instead for a structured technical assistance pathway.

The QHIN Cost Barrier: A stark, data-backed example from an individual commenter reveals a quote from a QHIN with a minimum monthly spend "significantly higher than the $10k per month" and per-transaction costs of $1-$3. This provides concrete evidence that participation in the national interoperability framework may be financially impossible for smaller entities.

Unintended Consequences of HITECH: Several commenters point out that the HITECH Act, while successful in driving initial adoption, inadvertently "accelerated Epic's market dominance" and contributed to the provider consolidation, vendor lock-in, and high costs that now plague the system.

A Free Solution Hiding in Plain Sight: Ad Hoc LLC points out a dramatic inefficiency: "Hospitals and clinics across America are paying thousands of dollars monthly for medication history data that CMS already provides for free via Blue Button 2.0," with a typical hospital spending $50,000 annually for commercial services.

The "Chilling Effect" of Fees: Phreesia makes the sharp observation that even if they don't technically meet the definition of information blocking, "undue costs and delays to access data have the same chilling effect," highlighting that the practical outcome for innovation is the same.

"Cultural Artifacts, Not Obstacles": ppxTEC offers a unique framing of legacy systems, arguing against the disruptive "rip-and-replace" mentality and advocating for technology that integrates with and preserves these long-term investments.

Cloud Computing and Grant Overhead: The Connected Health Initiative points out a specific policy barrier: federal grants treat cloud service costs as "overhead," while hardware purchases are not, creating a disincentive for researchers to use modern, scalable cloud infrastructure.

The Crushing Cost for Micro-Providers: Seen Health, a small PACE program, provides a powerful, concrete example of how fee structures create care disparities. An annual HIE/QHIN access fee of up to $75,000 is "unjustifiable" for their program of ~100 participants.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Digital Chasm and the "Forgotten Providers": The most pronounced pattern is the chasm between large, well-resourced health systems and virtually everyone else. Small practices, rural clinics, FQHCs, and specialty providers (Long-Term/Post-Acute Care, Home Health, Behavioral Health, Dental, Pharmacy, Wound Care) share a common story of being priced out of modern health IT, lacking the capital and staff to participate, and feeling ignored by policymakers. They consistently trace their struggles to being excluded from HITECH Act incentives.

The Provider-Vendor Blame Cycle and Vendor-on-Vendor Critique: Providers almost uniformly blame vendors for exorbitant costs and data-hoarding. Vendors push back, citing the high cost of developing for unfunded government mandates. A sub-pattern is the clear dynamic of smaller, innovative technology companies being extremely critical of the anti-competitive business practices of large, incumbent EHR vendors, whom they see as a barrier to the entire health-tech ecosystem.

“Public Good” vs. Private Cost Misalignment: A recurring theme is that many interoperability and data sharing functions are a “public good” (benefiting the entire system), but the implementation cost is borne almost entirely by private provider organizations, creating a fundamental economic misalignment that hinders progress.

Unintended Consequences as a Root Cause: Multiple commenters trace current problems of cost and vendor lock-in directly back to the structure of the HITECH Act. Similarly, many worry that new, well-intentioned mandates will inadvertently add more cost and complexity, further burdening providers if not implemented with financial support and a clear ROI.

keyQuotations.quote: Today’s digital healthcare infrastructure is not accidentally fragmented, costly, and ineffective — it was built that way.
We do not have electronic health records because of exorbitant cost to buy and maintain the system.
keyQuotations.sourceType: DENISE WENTZ, Physician in a rural private practice

keyQuotations.quote: Cost is not trivial, but it too is a created barrier more than a real one. Cost issues are increasingly used to inhibit interoperability at a time that technology drives those issues down.
keyQuotations.sourceType: The American Board of Family Medicine

keyQuotations.quote: Even when an API is available, the fees charged by the EHR may be exorbitant and far above the marginal costs to service the request. We strongly urge CMS and ONC to mandate cost-based pricing for APIs...

The system is collapsing under its own weight: Healthcare administration consumes $1.5 trillion annually, 34% of total spending. A typical 300-bed hospital spends $40-60 million yearly just on regulatory compliance.
[The] lack of technology is not the problem—lack of resource availability is.
keyQuotations.sourceType: National Alliance for Care at Home

keyQuotations.quote: Small providers are the backbone of innovative care delivery to underserved communities... Yet current health IT infrastructure systematically disadvantages us...
Interoperability remains under-funded precisely because private health systems and electronic health record (EHR) vendors are not compensated for delivering what is a public good.

[A]n independent physician-owned clinic participating in a value-based care arrangements reported spending over $450,000 to make changes to their EHR and practice management systems to meet requirements and improve reporting.
The unfunded EHR mandate... Stop this costly and dangerous system...
keyQuotations.sourceType: Twila Brase, RN, PHN, Citizens' Council for Health Freedom

keyQuotations.quote: The amount of data CMS expects providers to collect and share puts them in a never-ending upgrade cycle with the EMR vendor.
The HITECH Act of 2009 provided significant funding for EHR adoption in physical health settings but neglected mental health and substance use disorder settings.
Reduce the anti-competitive behaviors of large EHR vendors who use bundling and intimidation tactics to squash innovative technology companies.
Opaque and unpredictable API pricing structures... effectively function as tollgates...
API activation costs are costly; often $5K–$20K per endpoint.
The goal of enhanced interoperability must be to lighten the administrative load and improve patient care at the front lines, rather than piling on new layers of complexity and cost.
For our small home care agency in Iowa the cost of the EMR is very expensive approximately 20% of my revenue is spent on software license and support.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Diana Price RN, Freedom Home Health Care Inc.

keyQuotations.quote: Hospitals and clinics across America are paying thousands of dollars monthly for medication history data that CMS already provides for free.
The lack of recognition as providers in the Social Security Act and, subsequently, the lack of access to incentive funds... has contributed to slower adoption of electronic systems...
keyQuotations.sourceType: The Pharmacy Health Information Technology Collaborative (PHIT)

keyQuotations.quote: A 'Magic Wand' for EHR integration does not yet exist... due in part to the proprietary interests of electronic health record vendors...

7.3.themeDescription: Aligning "Incident to" Billing with Data Reality

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on "incident to" billing is sharply divided, exposing a fundamental tension between data integrity and reimbursement flexibility. A strong majority of professional associations and policy groups advocate for eliminating or drastically reforming "incident to" billing, arguing it critically distorts data, prevents accurate quality measurement, and unfairly evaluates non-physician providers. Conversely, a vocal group of health technology innovators views the existing "incident to" structure as a vital and pragmatic pathway for reimbursing new services and non-traditional care team members, such as caregivers and AI-driven support tools.

consensusPoints.text: "Incident to" Billing Distorts Data and Undermines Transparency. A strong majority of commenters agree that the current practice of billing non-physician services under a physician's NPI creates inaccurate and unreliable data. The American Academy of Physician Associates (AAPA) states that this practice "skew[s] assessments of provider care quality, creating inaccurate data on both PAs and physicians, and resulting in data that inhibits a beneficiary’s ability to make objective assessments." The Bipartisan Policy Center (BPC) notes that MedPAC has raised concerns that "incident to" billing "mak[es] quality monitoring harder" and complicates fraud detection. Leavitt Partners argues for standardized documentation for pharmacy services to create a "level competitive field," a goal impossible without accurate attribution of the rendering provider.

exceptions.text: While no commenter explicitly defends data distortion, innovators like Pair Team and KuduCare implicitly accept it as a trade-off for gaining a reimbursement pathway for new services.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Future of "incident to" Billing

positions.label: Eliminate & Reform
positions.stance: Phase out "incident to" billing to ensure services are billed under the NPI of the actual rendering provider.
positions.supportLevel: Held by professional associations and policy organizations (AAPA, BPC)
keyArguments: Data Integrity: It is essential for accurate quality measurement, especially in value-based care models.
Workforce Evaluation: The current system makes it impossible to accurately assess the volume, quality, and cost-effectiveness of care delivered by PAs and other professionals.
Fraud & Abuse: The lack of transparency complicates monitoring and fraud detection, a concern previously raised by MedPAC and the OIG.

positions.label: Leverage & Expand
positions.stance: Use the existing "incident to" framework as a flexible mechanism to reimburse new and innovative services.
positions.supportLevel: Held by health technology companies and new care model providers
keyArguments: Reimbursement Pathway: It provides a ready-made, albeit imperfect, vehicle for compensating new roles (e.g., caregivers) and new technologies (e.g., AI copilots) that lack dedicated billing codes.
Innovation Accelerator: Requiring a full legislative or regulatory process for every new service type would stifle innovation; "incident to" offers a pragmatic shortcut.
Care Continuity: It can be used to formally recognize and reimburse the support that team members, like caregivers, provide to ensure patient adherence.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Inaccurate data, unfair evaluation of their constituents (PAs, behavioral health providers), systemic barriers to value-based care, and flawed payment methodologies that undervalue cognitive work.
specificPoints: These groups focus on the long-term, structural integrity of the healthcare system. They argue that flawed data inputs lead to flawed policy, payment, and patient outcomes.
The AAPA highlights that the problem extends beyond Medicare to commercial and managed care plans that won't enroll PAs, forcing distorted billing.
Proposed solutions include legislative and regulatory action to eliminate "incident to" billing (AAPA, BPC) and fundamentally reassess how services are valued and reimbursed (MHA).
The BPC cites concerns from MedPAC and the OIG. Mental Health America connects flawed reimbursement to provider shortages in mental health.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Establishing viable reimbursement pathways for new technologies and services, reducing friction for adoption, and improving system infrastructure.
specificPoints: This group views the regulatory framework pragmatically, seeing "incident to" not as a flawed principle to be fixed, but as a useful tool to be leveraged for immediate progress. They are focused on speed to market and operational viability.
Proposed solutions include expanding "incident to" to cover new functions like AI copilots and caregiver support.
One commenter proposes a more radical, foundational fix: replacing the NPI system with cryptographically secure digital identities to solve attribution problems at their root.
Pair Team’s proposal to create a temporary G-code for an AI copilot billable under "incident to" rules is a clear example of this pragmatic approach.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Pragmatic Tool vs. Flawed Principle: The submissions reveal a deep philosophical divide. Established organizations see "incident to" as a fundamentally flawed principle that corrupts the system, while startups and innovators see it as a pragmatic tool that, while imperfect, enables progress in the absence of a better alternative.
A Symptom of a Deeper Problem: Mental Health America links 'incident to' to a payment system that structurally undervalues cognitive care.
A Symptom of a Deeper Problem: HTD Health suggests the root problem is an insecure and outdated provider identity infrastructure (NPPES), proposing a far more ambitious technological solution.

System-Wide Payer Problem: The AAPA astutely points out this is not just a Medicare Fee-for-Service issue. The refusal of Medicare Advantage, Medicaid Managed Care, and commercial QHP plans to enroll PAs forces the use of "incident to"-style workarounds, demonstrating a systemic challenge beyond CMS's direct FFS rules.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Experience Correlation: A clear pattern emerges based on the commenter's position in the healthcare ecosystem. Established professional and policy groups with a focus on system-wide, long-term integrity advocate for reform and data purity. In contrast, newer, technology-focused companies prioritize immediate, practical pathways to reimbursement and market adoption, even if it requires using flawed mechanisms.

Unintended Consequences: The comments highlight a critical policy trade-off. Aggressively eliminating "incident to" billing (as AAPA and BPC recommend) could unintentionally halt or delay valuable innovation (as feared by Pair Team and KuduCare). Conversely, expanding its use to encourage innovation would exacerbate the data integrity and transparency crises that are already undermining the move to value-based care.

keyQuotations.quote: [P]A services submitted under Medicare’s “incident to” billing provision and attributed to physicians skew assessments of provider care quality, creating inaccurate data on both PAs and physicians, and resulting in data that inhibits a beneficiary’s ability to make objective assessments.

To improve price transparency, CMS should phase out 'incident to' billing for providers who can bill Medicare directly. Although BPC recommends eliminating 'incident to' billing, a good first step would be for CMS to identify and monitor this type of billing.
Enable caregivers to participate under 'incident to' structures, provided documentation shows they are supporting patient adherence and care continuity.
The CMMI demonstration for the AI copilot should Designate a temporary G-code (e.g., modeled on G0511) so copilot work is billable under incident-to rules.

[CMS should] Replace the current NPI system with cryptographically secure, un-spoofable provider identities...enabling digital attestation of affiliations or organizational verification of employment, creating bidirectional verification and making ghost networks impossible.
MHA urges CMS to consider the true value of cognitive work and weigh the demand and difficulty of obtaining in-network care to reassess primary care, behavioral healthcare, and integrated care codes.

7.4.themeDescription: Managing In-Basket and Patient Portal Message Burden

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment confirms that the burden of managing electronic in-basket and patient portal messages has reached a crisis point, contributing significantly to workforce burnout. There is universal consensus that the status quo is unsustainable, with a primary tension between stakeholders who prioritize new payment models to compensate for this labor and those who champion technological solutions like AI and open APIs to fix the underlying workflow inefficiencies. The dominant thrust of recommendations is a dual approach: CMS must both establish sustainable payment models for this work and advance regulations that foster a more innovative, interoperable technology ecosystem to intelligently manage, not just transmit, communication.

consensusPoints.text: The In-Basket/Portal Message Burden is a Severe and Unsustainable Problem. Dr. Ateev Mehrotra describes the situation as a crisis of 'death by patient portal,' as physicians manage a rapid increase in largely uncompensated work. The Texas Medical Association (TMA) states physicians are "overwhelmed by the influx of messages" which constitutes a "significant in-basket message management burden". Integrated health system WellSpan Health confirms that "Providers are already burdened with EHR inboxes".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (at least 15 of 24) across all stakeholder groups—including physician associations, health systems, technology companies, and individual clinicians—explicitly identify the high volume of electronic messages as a significant driver of workload, stress, and burnout.

exceptions.text: While all acknowledge the problem, technology companies tend to frame it as an inefficiency to be solved, whereas clinicians and their associations frame it as an uncompensated labor crisis.

consensusPoints.text: The Current Model of Uncompensated Message Management Must Change. TMA directly recommends that "Physicians should be compensated for their time spent on in-basket management". Public Health Innovators, LLC states that time spent on messages "should be factored into provider scheduling and reimbursement to encourage, rather than discourage, the use of messaging". Researcher Dr. Mehrotra warns that "Without sustainable payment models for messaging, there is a risk of exacerbating clinician burnout".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, particularly clinicians, researchers, and their associations, argue that the status quo of managing messages without dedicated compensation is not viable.

exceptions.text: There is significant debate about the method of compensation, but near-universal agreement that some form of financial recognition for this work is necessary.

consensusPoints.text: Technology is a Key Part of the Solution. CareCo proposes that "Digital tools should automate and streamline communication" to prevent it from becoming an "unmanageable burden". Health IT professional David Rocha suggests launching hackathons to develop "SMART on FHIR applications, SNOMED CT triage rules... and NLP combined with FHIR for free-text messages" to intelligently route and prioritize work. The SMART Health IT Team advocates for "Open messaging APIs" to allow third-party apps to become "active communication hubs," implying better tools can be built to manage the flow.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most stakeholders, from technology vendors to clinicians and health systems, agree that technology itself—specifically AI, automation, and better interoperability—is critical to managing the message burden.

exceptions.text: No commenter explicitly opposed using technology, but several (e.g., Anonymous, Doctronic) highlighted the negative patient impacts of current "solutions" like chatbots and charging for messages, suggesting that technology must be implemented thoughtfully.

areasOfDebate.topic: Primary Solution: Payment vs. Technology
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether to prioritize new payment models to address uncompensated labor or to focus first on technological solutions to improve workflow efficiency.

positions.label: Payment First
positions.stance: The fundamental problem is uncompensated labor, which must be addressed through new payment models before technology can be truly effective.
positions.supportLevel: Most healthcare professional associations and academic researchers.
keyArguments: Technology can't solve a labor issue
physicians are performing work "outside of work" that requires compensation
existing billing codes are inadequate and administratively burdensome

positions.label: Technology First
positions.stance: The fundamental problem is workflow inefficiency, which can be solved with smarter, more interoperable tools like AI triage and automation, thereby reducing the need for complex payment schemes.
positions.supportLevel: Most technology companies and some health IT professionals.
keyArguments: Open APIs and AI can triage, automate, and route messages to the right team member, drastically reducing physician burden
the problem is data being locked in proprietary systems, preventing innovation

areasOfDebate.topic: Payment Model Design: Fee-for-Service vs. Alternative Models
areasOfDebate.description: This debate focuses on the best payment model, contrasting the practicality of traditional fee-for-service (FFS) billing against newer hybrid or capitated models.

positions.label: FFS is Flawed
positions.stance: Simple fee-for-service (FFS) billing for individual messages is impractical and insufficient.
positions.supportLevel: A key academic researcher and implied by provider experiences.
keyArguments: The administrative burden of billing for each message outweighs the low reimbursement
adoption by payers is extremely low
it doesn't account for the skewed distribution of message volume

positions.label: Hybrid/Capitated Models
positions.stance: A blended or capitated payment model is necessary to balance compensation and administrative simplicity.
positions.supportLevel: Proposed by academic researchers and research organizations.
keyArguments: A "partial capitation" model (fixed fee for median work + FFS for excess) could reduce administrative burden while fairly compensating high-volume physicians
new models are needed to account for work outside clinic hours

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Physician burnout from uncompensated work; cognitive overload; the sheer volume and pace of incoming messages.
specificPoints: They are experiencing the "death by patient portal" firsthand. Their focus is squarely on the unsustainable workload and the need for direct, tangible relief through compensation and better tools.
Mandate that EHRs track time spent on messages; create new payment codes or models for message management; limit the type and amount of data sent to providers to avoid overwhelm.
TMA's clear and repeated call for CMS to study the time burden and compensate physicians for it.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: System-wide operational inefficiency; staff burden (beyond just physicians); reliance on manual, phone-based communication.
specificPoints: They see the problem at an institutional scale, linking in-basket burden to broader goals like value-based care and reducing administrative waste across the entire organization.
Mandate APIs for specific high-volume tasks like prescription refills; simplify processes to reduce reliance on phone calls; unlock message data from proprietary formats to enable innovation.
Providence highlights that message data is "locked behind proprietary data formats and APIs," preventing the development of innovative management solutions.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of open APIs; inefficient workflows; data silos that prevent their tools from being deployed effectively.
specificPoints: They view the in-basket burden as a solvable technical challenge and a market opportunity. They are evangelists for technology (AI, automation, interoperability) as the primary solution.
Mandate open messaging APIs; use AI for triage and proactive outreach; build a unified digital platform for patients; run hackathons to spur innovation.
David Rocha provides a detailed, actionable plan for a cross-functional team to analyze, prototype, and pilot technological solutions to reduce in-basket load.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Being charged for messages; lack of follow-up on requests; difficulty getting answers from a human; chatbot barriers.
specificPoints: They reveal the unintended negative consequences for patients when providers try to manage the burden. They experience the friction and new barriers created by "solutions" like charging for portal messages.

Their comments focus on problems rather than solutions, but implicitly call for communication channels that are responsive, accessible, and don't create new financial burdens for routine clarification.
An anonymous TBI survivor notes that "Some health systems have turned to charging individuals for messaging which creates a barrier to a healthy exchange of information".

noteworthyInsights.insight: Data-backed observation on Skewed Volume: Dr. Ateev Mehrotra's research provides critical data showing the problem isn't evenly distributed: the top 1% of high-volume patients send over 20% of messages, and top-quartile PCPs get five times more messages than the median. This insight is crucial, as it suggests that solutions must be targeted and risk-adjusted, not one-size-fits-all.

The Burden is System-Wide, Not Just on Physicians: Health IT professional David Rocha compellingly points out that "The in basket overload is not only happening to the clinicians, but also happening on the administrative/clerical side," including licensed nurses, medical assistants, and front desk staff. This broadens the scope of the problem and the need for solutions that benefit the entire care team.

Patient Education as a Solution: Public Health Innovators, LLC offered a unique, proactive recommendation: teach patients how to be better digital communicators. Guiding them on how to write "concise, appropriate, and targeted messages" could improve efficiency on the provider side while empowering patients to get better responses.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Unintended Consequences of Digital Access: The patient portal, a technology intended to empower patients and improve communication, has inadvertently created an unsustainable new channel of uncompensated work for clinicians. Now, the provider-led "solution" of charging for messages is creating a new unintended consequence: a financial barrier to patient-provider communication.

Technology as a Double-Edged Sword: The comments reveal a fundamental tension where technology (EHRs, portals) is the root cause of the problem, yet more advanced technology (AI, interoperable APIs) is universally seen as the most promising solution. The policy challenge is not to add more tech, but to foster an ecosystem of smarter tech.

Broadening Definition of "Burden": The theme's focus on "physician burnout" is expanded by commenters to include the burden on the entire clinical and administrative team. The problem is increasingly understood as a systemic, operational challenge, not just an individual physician's time management issue.

keyQuotations.quote: [The increase in portal messages] has created a crisis described as 'death by patient portal,' as physicians manage a rapid increase in largely uncompensated work.
The in basket overload is not only happening to the clinicians, but also happening on the administrative/clerical side.
Physicians feel that the administrative burden of fee-for-service billing for individual messages outweighs the low reimbursement per message.
Some health systems have turned to charging individuals for messaging which creates a barrier to a healthy exchange of information where patients might tell providers what is and isn’t working.
[New tools should ensure that] each response originates from an individual working at the top of their license.

Showing which finalists actually reduce in basket messages and showing the use case to the clinicians and front line staff personally could be a game changer and could almost make the staff cry from alleviating such a heavy burden.

Patients are 'exponentially increasing their digital message interactions with providers (aka in basket message burden)', yet this data is still locked behind proprietary data formats and APIs in EMRs.

8.1.themeDescription: Meeting the Critical Data Needs of VBC

sections.executiveSummary:
There is overwhelming, urgent, and universal consensus among all stakeholders that value-based care (VBC) entities are "flying blind," fundamentally handicapped by data that is delayed, fragmented, and incomplete. This is not a minor challenge but a foundational failure that jeopardizes the entire VBC enterprise. The lack of timely, comprehensive, and standardized data—especially real-time Admission, Discharge, Transfer (ADT) feeds, reliable patient attribution, and actionable payer data (claims, quality, performance)—is consistently cited as the single greatest barrier to managing risk and improving patient care.

A powerful tension defines the landscape: providers in risk-based contracts are desperate for timely performance and utilization data from payers, while payers are equally focused on gaining access to comprehensive clinical data from providers' EHRs. VBC is caught in the middle of this data standoff.

While there is broad agreement on the problem, the debate centers on the solution. A strong majority favors regulatory mandates for bi-directional data exchange over more flexible, incentive-based programs, arguing the latter have proven insufficient. The most insistent and frequent recommendations call for CMS to use its authority to: 1) mandate that all payers provide standardized, near real-time claims and performance data to risk-bearing providers; 2) strengthen and enforce requirements for real-time ADT notifications across all care settings; and 3) create a standardized, transparent, and timely system to fix the broken patient attribution process that undermines all other efforts.

consensusPoints.text: VBC Entities Are Operating "Flying Blind" Due to Critical Data Delays. This was the most universal point of agreement, cited by virtually every commenter across all stakeholder types. The phrase "flying blind" was used repeatedly to describe the reality of managing patient risk without timely information. There is overwhelming consensus that current data delays (often weeks or months for claims, performance, and utilization data) make proactive intervention and effective risk management impossible, rendering much of the data useful only for retrospective review, not active care management. Lumeris Healthcare states it is "next to impossible to effectively manage a beneficiary without access to claims data," which can be delayed by almost 90 days. "Providers in VBC programs... require access to CMS claims data that is 'as real-time as possible' to meet health IT requirements and effectively manage patients... This is an egregious disadvantage in being able to perform well in these programs." "Current delays in claims data access reduce its clinical or operational actionability," according to Stellar Health.

A Comprehensive, Longitudinal Patient Record Is Essential for VBC. A vast majority of commenters agree that VBC requires the seamless integration of multiple data types into a single, unified view. Stakeholders consistently identified data silos—across EHRs, payers, labs, pharmacies, and post-acute care—as a primary obstacle. There is broad agreement that a 360-degree patient view must include clinical/EHR data (structured and unstructured), claims data (FFS and MA), real-time ADT feeds, pharmacy and lab data, Social Determinants of Health (SDOH), and Patient-Reported Outcomes (PROs). "The complete, longitudinal patient record is the essential data type for VBC." The Health Care Transformation Task Force (HCTTF) emphasizes that success requires a "unified data platform that aggregates, normalizes, and integrates data from disparate sources... into longitudinal patient records." Findhelp noted that "Comprehensive social care data... is essential for successful participation in value-based care arrangements."

Data Standardization is a Foundational Requirement. Most stakeholders, particularly technology vendors and provider associations, stressed that scalable and effective data exchange must be built on modern, open standards. There is broad and deep support for leveraging FHIR APIs (especially Bulk FHIR) and expanding the USCDI to include VBC-critical data elements. The current lack of standardization is blamed for massive administrative waste, duplicative reporting, inhibited analytics, and the creation of barriers to VBC participation. AMGA argues that "timely, standardized access to claims and clinical data is foundational for success in Advanced Payment Models (APMs)." MEDITECH stated, "A critical challenge lies in ensuring consistency in data types and definitions across CMS and various payer programs... without this uniformity, aggregating and comparing data... becomes an arduous, error-prone, and often impossible task."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most stakeholders, particularly technology vendors and provider associations

consensusPoints.text: Broken Patient Attribution Systems Cripple VBC Operations. Provider organizations and the tech vendors serving them are in near-universal agreement that patient attribution is a critical failure point. Current processes are described as slow, inaccurate, opaque, and inconsistent across payers. This fundamental flaw makes it impossible for VBC entities to know who they are responsible for, target interventions, or be measured fairly. NACHC states that "CHCs struggle to target patients for Annual Wellness Visits (AWVs) due to incorrect patient attribution, currently relying on outdated payer reporting." Deloitte reports they "consistently see a 10-40% gap" between practice-submitted rosters and official payer attribution lists. The Wright Centers noted that "Misaligned panels and inaccurate patient attribution create confusion and undercut care coordination for providers in value-based care."

areasOfDebate.topic: The Primary Driver for Data Sharing
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to compel data sharing through regulatory mandates or to encourage it via financial incentives and market flexibility.

positions.label: Regulatory Mandates
positions.stance: CMS must use its regulatory authority to require bi-directional data sharing (e.g., mandating ADT feeds, payer-to-provider data exchange, participation in TEFCA) as a condition of participation in VBC models.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of commenters, including providers, ACOs, tech vendors, and even payer associations.
keyArguments: The current voluntary and incentive-based system is not working; market forces alone are insufficient to overcome data-hoarding and fragmentation; clear rules are needed to create a level playing field and a predictable environment for investment.

positions.label: Financial Incentives & Flexibility
positions.stance: CMS should use financial incentives (e.g., shared-savings bonuses), technical assistance, and increased financial risk/reward in VBC models to create a stronger business case for data sharing.
positions.supportLevel: A smaller but vocal group, particularly consultants and some tech vendors.
keyArguments: Mandates could impose significant costs and stifle innovation; if data sharing is directly linked to payment, providers and payers will invest; one-size-fits-all mandates are unworkable for diverse VBC models.

areasOfDebate.topic: Responsibility and Architecture for Data Aggregation
areasOfDebate.description: Conflict over whether data aggregation should be handled by a centralized, CMS-led system or a decentralized, federated network like TEFCA.

positions.label: Centralized, CMS-Led Solutions
positions.stance: CMS should create centralized data hubs or significantly expand its APIs (like Data at the Point of Care) to be the primary source for comprehensive VBC data, especially for MA encounter data and claims across all payers.
positions.supportLevel: A significant number of providers and tech vendors.
keyArguments: CMS is uniquely positioned to aggregate data across all payers (FFS, MA, Medicaid); this would solve the problem of dealing with hundreds of individual payer formats; it creates a single, authoritative source.

positions.label: Decentralized/Federated Network
positions.stance: The focus should be on strengthening and mandating participation in a decentralized network of endpoints, leveraging TEFCA, QHINs, and HIEs as the data-sharing backbone.
positions.supportLevel: The majority of tech vendors, HIEs/HDUs, and many policy groups.
keyArguments: Leverages existing infrastructure; is more flexible and scalable than a single, monolithic federal system; aligns with modern, API-driven technology. However, some HIEs argue TEFCA in its current form is insufficient for key VBC functions like panel-based encounter notifications.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Healthcare Providers & VBC Entities (ACOs, Health Systems, Clinics)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Inability to manage patient populations effectively due to data delays (especially claims, performance, and ADT feeds); opaque and inaccurate patient attribution from payers; the high cost and administrative burden of integrating data from disparate sources; feeling held accountable for outcomes without the necessary data to succeed.
specificPoints: They provide the most visceral, day-to-day operational consequences of data gaps, such as missed opportunities for post-discharge intervention, chasing closed care gaps, and being unable to validate performance until long after a performance period ends. They are the most likely to assert that data withholding is often a strategic, competitive decision by other actors, not a technical failure.
Overwhelmingly favor mandates for CMS and all payers to provide standardized, near real-time claims, encounter, ADT, and attribution data to risk-bearing entities.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of standardized, reliable, and performant APIs (especially Bulk FHIR); inconsistent data formats requiring costly, bespoke integrations; data silos limiting the effectiveness of their analytics and AI models.
specificPoints: They universally frame their specific platforms as the solution to VBC data challenges while simultaneously advocating for the public infrastructure (standardized APIs) that would make their products more valuable. They bring specific technical solutions to the table and often distinguish between transactional data exchange (FHIR's strength) and the separate need for a population-level analytics layer.
Mandate universal adoption of FHIR APIs and TEFCA; expand USCDI to include VBC-relevant data; create a national provider directory of FHIR endpoints; support and accelerate projects like Da Vinci.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Gaining reliable, standardized access to clinical data from providers' EHRs to support their own VBC operations, quality measurement (HEDIS), and risk adjustment.
specificPoints: They are the primary group focused on the need for inbound data from provider systems, framing the issue as a need for mandatory bi-directional exchange. They feel providers are withholding the clinical data they need to operate VBC programs effectively.
Require provider data sharing as a condition of participation; make "Healthcare Operations" a mandated use case in TEFCA; make payer-provider data exchange a VBC quality measure.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: System-wide lack of interoperability as a barrier to VBC adoption; the need for data standards that accommodate diverse member needs (specialists, hospitals, ACOs); ensuring patient identification is accurate; duplicative reporting burdens.
