stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Unreliable infrastructure hampering their own operations, inability to use digital tools due to patient access/skill gaps, patient cost-sharing as a deterrent, and the lack of reimbursement for time spent on patient tech support.
specificPoints: They highlight that the digital divide is a two-sided problem, affecting providers as much as patients, and connect it directly to clinical outcomes. A rural physician notes "we frequently have internet and power outages".
Ochsner Health provides damning evidence that 43% of beneficiaries opted out of digital programs when faced with potential cost-sharing.
NACHC cites data that over 22% of rural Americans lack adequate internet.

Proposed solutions include federal investment in rural broadband, ensuring permanent reimbursement for audio-only telehealth, creating payment for "digital navigator" services, and expanding fraud and abuse safe harbors to allow them to provide devices/internet access to patients.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring a market for their products, overcoming patient adoption barriers, standardizing identity verification, and securing reimbursement pathways.
specificPoints: Many frame their own products as direct solutions to the digital divide, highlighting features like voice AI to bypass literacy (XSELL), cellular-enabled devices to bypass broadband gaps (ChronicCare), or simplified UIs to lower barriers for seniors (Wellth).

Proposed solutions include incentivizing adoption of accessible tools, mandating user-centric and offline/low-bandwidth design, providing funding for RPM devices and broadband access, and creating new reimbursement models for digital literacy and support.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring policy promotes equity, coordinating federal programs (e.g., CMS, FCC, and NTIA's BEAD program), and funding the necessary public health infrastructure.
specificPoints: They highlight the failure of past federal commitments and policy incoherence. Public Health Innovators provides a devastating account of the Administration allegedly canceling "the entire $2.7 billion Digital Equity Act (DEA) program," which had federally approved state plans ready for implementation.

Proposed solutions include aligning digital health initiatives with broader strategies to promote broadband access, funding community-based workforces, and implementing policies that target resources to sectors with low digital capacity.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The 'CALL or DIE' Example: The testimony of Michael Ney (Individual) is exceptionally powerful. His claim of being denied Medicare for lack of a phone and told to "CALL or DIE" provides a stark, humanizing, and unforgettable illustration of how digital-first policies can actively endanger the most vulnerable.

Policy Working at Cross-Purposes: Public Health Innovators, LLC (Consultant) delivers a stunning insight that while CMS solicits input on closing the digital divide, the Administration has "canceled the entire $2.7 billion Digital Equity Act (DEA) program," which was poised to fund state-level initiatives, highlighting a critical disconnect between stated goals and federal action.

Cost-Sharing as a Digital Adoption Killer: Ochsner Health (Provider) provides a precise, data-backed observation: 43% of their Medicare beneficiaries who had been participating in digital health programs opted out when notified of a potential 20% cost-sharing responsibility. This demonstrates that even small out-of-pocket costs are a significant barrier.

Data-Backed Insight on Portal Barriers: The San Francisco Tech Council's pilot (Consumer Group) offers rare empirical data on patient portal non-use. Their finding that the top two reasons for non-use were "(i) not sure how to use it and (ii) never heard of it" is a crucial insight, suggesting that awareness and training are as important as access.

Counter-Narrative on Beneficiary Savviness: SKYGEN (Business) cites a 2022 survey finding that 91% of Medicaid beneficiaries are online and a majority use mobile banking daily. This data challenges the broad assumption that all low-income populations lack digital skills and suggests user-friendly applications can see high adoption.

The 'Offline' Imperative: Multiple commenters (D4T4 DR1V3N, The Carter Center) converge on the critical need for "offline-first" or "broadband-resilient" design. This goes beyond low-bandwidth functionality and envisions tools that remain useful during internet outages or in areas with zero connectivity.

Infrastructure Gaps Create Data Bias: Consensus Cloud Solutions (Tech Vendor) points out a systemic risk: providers in rural areas and small clinics lacking advanced EHRs will be unable to participate in data exchange networks, meaning they "won't be able to participate in population health efforts, which could introduce potential bias into public health data."

Innovative Community-Based Solutions: Grace Cordovano (Patient Advocate) moves beyond typical suggestions of libraries, recommending partnerships with "Barbershops, beauty salons, and laundry mats as well as places of worship" to deliver tech support in trusted, everyday environments.

The "Orphaned Patient": Patients For Patient Safety US (Advocacy Group) shares a compelling real-world example of "orphaned" patients without a primary care provider who are completely disconnected from digital services, creating dangerous information gaps.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural-Urban Disparity as the Central Fault Line: The most consistent pattern is the profound difference in need between rural and urban areas. Rural commenters almost universally prioritize the fundamental lack of broadband and cellular infrastructure. Urban-focused comments, while acknowledging the divide, tend to give more weight to digital literacy, device affordability, and usability.

Convergence on a Hybrid "Tech + Touch" Model: A clear pattern across nearly all stakeholder groups is the call for solutions that combine technology with human support. Whether framed as "digital navigators," "CHWs," "on-the-ground support," or "telephonic assistance," there is a strong consensus that technology alone is insufficient and must be paired with accessible, human-led education and troubleshooting.

The "Two-Sided" Divide: Many comments reveal the digital divide impacts providers just as much as patients. Small, rural, and post-acute care facilities often lack the funding, IT staff, and infrastructure to adopt modern digital tools, creating a provider-side bottleneck to digital transformation.

Solution-Oriented Vendor Positioning: There is a distinct pattern of technology vendors framing their specific product category as the ideal solution to the digital divide—e.g., voice AI bypasses literacy barriers, cellular devices bypass broadband gaps, and simplified UIs solve usability for seniors.

Unintended Consequence of Digital Mandates: A recurring theme is the fear that well-intentioned digital mandates, especially around identity credentials or digital-only services, will backfire and widen health disparities rather than close them if implemented without robust support and non-digital alternatives.

keyQuotations.quote: [The] digital divide, now recognized as a super social determinant of health, risks exacerbating health disparities for certain populations.

[The] gap between a multi-million-dollar urban EHR and a modest rural clinic's system represents more than just differing technology; it embodies a systemic failure to prioritize seamless, patient-centered data flow.
...every extra selfie/driver-license scan knocks a share of seniors, rural patients, non-English speakers and privacy-conscious consumers out of the digital channel.
A 2022 BCG survey found 91 percent of Medicaid beneficiaries are online...a clear signal that Medicaid beneficiaries are digitally savvy and would use a one-stop Medicaid app if it were available.
Access to digital tools is becoming essential for accessing care; many beneficiaries are locked out.
An unintended consequence could be pushing critical CBOs with less technical capacity out of the community at the expense of individuals who require those services.
Barriers to patient portal use among two-thirds of participants who identified as non-users...were: (i) not sure how to use it and (ii) never heard of it.
CMS can work with cell companies to create an exemption from data caps for digital healthcare services...so that patients do not need to compromise their access to care.
CMS must treat internet access, devices, and tech literacy as foundational to care participation. Patients cannot use what they cannot reach.
Feedback from both patients and providers underscores that successful technology adoption requires more than just user-friendly design; it calls for proactive, everyday guidance.
I don’t do anything online... I can’t afford a cell phone. Yours cost about a thousand dollars. I finally found one for like $80.
In a very rural state, 'orphaned' patients (those with no designated primary care provider), a critical and worsening problem, are by default without digital services.
Lack of modern broadband infrastructure is a primary barrier for rural Americans.
On May 9, 2025, the Administration canceled the entire $2.7 billion DEA program.
Please keep as much analog and phone and paper based information as you can for our elderly patients.
So many people in rural areas do not have access to PCs, many do not even have cell coverage and do not use cell phones.
The temptation is to think about solving healthcare challenges by putting software in front of patients, but this can add complexity, increase friction, and reduce engagement.
Upon turning 65 in 2020, they were denied Medicare, being told 'CALL or DIE.'
We frequently have internet and power outages.
Without robust connectivity, clinicians cannot access critical EHR systems, engage in telehealth, or exchange information during transitions of care, putting rural patients at significant risk.

9.2.themeDescription: Supporting Small, Rural, and Safety-Net Providers

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment is unanimous and urgent: small, rural, safety-net, and post-acute care providers face an existential threat from the high cost, technical complexity, and staffing requirements of modern health IT. Commenters from all sectors agree that this resource gap, exacerbated by historical exclusion from federal incentives, is widening the digital divide, threatening practice viability, and creating care deserts. There is overwhelming consensus that a "one-size-fits-all" regulatory approach is inequitable and will disproportionately harm these essential providers, many of whom are the sole source of care in their communities. While the problem is universally acknowledged, the debate on solutions centers on the optimal mix of direct financial aid, structured technical assistance, and regulatory flexibility. The dominant thrust of recommendations calls for a multi-pronged federal intervention combining all three approaches—direct funding, a revived Health IT Extension Program, and tiered or modular requirements—to ensure these vital practices can survive and participate in a digitally connected healthcare system.

consensusPoints.text: Prohibitive Financial and Staffing Burdens are the Primary Barrier. Virtually all commenters across every stakeholder group agree that crippling financial and staffing limitations are the root cause of the technology gap. They identify the high cost of health IT—including upfront acquisition, interface fees, ongoing maintenance, and costly data extracts—as the most significant obstacle. This is compounded by a severe lack of dedicated IT staff and technical expertise, making implementation and maintenance impossible even if the technology were affordable. A small rural practice states plainly, "We do not have electronic health records because of exorbitant cost". Cognitive Medical Systems provides specific data, citing upfront EHR costs of "$15k-$50k per provider." The National Association of Community Health Centers (NACHC) highlights that integration costs alone can run from "$6,000 to over $49,000," an impossible sum for centers on thin margins. The American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP) elaborates that "Small clinics... lack resources for full-time IT staff," a point echoed by a rural physician: "We cannot afford the digital health products nor the IT personnel needed for its maintenance".

exceptions.text: A few technology vendors propose business models with low or zero upfront costs, but this is presented as a private sector solution to the universally acknowledged problem, not a refutation of it.

consensusPoints.text: A "One-Size-Fits-All" Regulatory Approach Is Harmful and Inequitable. A strong majority of stakeholders, particularly provider associations and the providers themselves, argue that uniform mandates disproportionately harm resource-constrained practices. They assert that policies designed for large, urban health systems are punitive and impossible for smaller entities to meet, risking practice closures and exacerbating health disparities. The American Health Care Association (AHCA/NCAL) argues a "‘one-size-fits-all’ interoperability advancement strategy would be doomed to failure." The American Hospital Association (AHA) warns that inflexible disincentives may "threaten the financial viability of economically fragile hospitals." AMGA insists CMS should adopt "tiered requirements reflecting differences in provider size, location, and capabilities."

exceptions.text: While no one advocates for a harmful one-size-fits-all approach, a few commenters, e.g., Altera Digital Health, caution that creating different technology requirements can also cause "market confusion and exacerbate the digital divide."

consensusPoints.text: Post-Acute, Long-Term Care, and Specialty Providers Have Been Systematically Excluded. A broad coalition of commenters agrees that providers in post-acute care (PAC), long-term care (LTPAC), behavioral health, dental, and other specialty sectors were historically excluded from federal incentive programs like the HITECH Act. This exclusion is directly blamed for their current technology gap, leaving them reliant on paper or non-interoperable systems and now being asked to "play catch up" without adequate resources. CHIME notes these providers "were excluded from prior federal incentive programs" and "are still 'playing catch up'." The Association for Behavioral Health and Wellness (ABHW) states this exclusion has resulted in "lagging adoption of health IT." Premier Inc. adds that providers ineligible for HITECH "lag due to upfront costs, leading to 'disconnected' patient data."

Targeted Support is Essential for Viability and Equity. There is overwhelming, near-unanimous agreement that these provider groups require a combination of targeted financial aid, robust technical assistance, and regulatory flexibility to survive and participate in the modern digital health ecosystem. This is framed not just as a provider issue, but as a fundamental health equity issue, since these practices are often the "sole source of care" in their communities. OCHIN, a nonprofit network, asserts, "We cannot achieve the goals of a health technology ecosystem unless all providers have access to up-to-date systems and technical support." The Bipartisan Policy Center states that "Consolidating funding for independent rural or small primary care practices is critical." Homeward Health argues, "Rural healthcare will not benefit from digital transformation through passive diffusion. It will require proactive, sustained intervention."

areasOfDebate.topic: Optimal Approach to Technology Adoption and Federal Support
areasOfDebate.description: The central debate is not whether to support these providers, but how. The discussion splits between two main philosophies, though most commenters ultimately advocate for a hybrid approach.

positions.label: Flexible Pathways and Waivers
positions.stance: This position prioritizes pragmatism, arguing for direct financial subsidies, significant regulatory flexibility, and alternative technology pathways.
positions.supportLevel: The most common position, supported by nearly all provider groups, professional associations, individual practices, and many tech vendors.
keyArguments: Full CEHRT adoption is simply not feasible due to prohibitive costs, infrastructure limits (e.g., unreliable internet), and a lack of staff. A rigid mandate would force practices to close, destroying access to care. Direct funding is the only way to overcome cost barriers, while waivers and flexible, modular, or "health IT light" certification pathways allow for incremental progress without being punitive.

positions.label: Standardize and Subsidize
positions.stance: This position prioritizes universal, high-quality interoperability, arguing for a single standard for all providers, made possible by robust funding and shared infrastructure.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily advocated by HIEs, some technology vendors, and policy groups focused on national data exchange.
keyArguments: Creating separate, "lighter" tracks for technology risks perpetuating the digital divide, creating market confusion, and leaving some providers permanently behind. A single, high standard (like CEHRT) ensures true interoperability. The policy focus should be on providing the necessary resources—funding, technical assistance, and shared platforms (like HIEs or regional data hubs)—to help everyone meet that standard, not lowering the bar.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Financial survival in the face of "exorbitant" and unsustainable IT costs; lack of on-site technical staff; unreliable infrastructure (internet, power); inability to participate in value-based care; excessive administrative burden.
specificPoints: They provide visceral, on-the-ground testimony of the direct, existential impact on their practices and communities, highlighting how even seemingly small costs can be prohibitive and that they are often the sole source of care.

A combination of direct financial assistance (grants, subsidies), significant regulatory flexibility (waivers, tiered requirements), and government-funded, hands-on technical assistance (a revived Health IT Extension Program).

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Post-Acute, Long-Term Care, Dental, & Behavioral Health Providers
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Being historically excluded from HITECH incentives; the unsuitability of hospital-centric EHR certification criteria for their unique workflows; the immense cost of "work-arounds" or adopting irrelevant technology.
specificPoints: They emphasize that they are not just smaller versions of hospitals but entirely different care settings with different needs, making them "data black holes" in the patient journey. They are particularly vocal in their opposition to "one-size-fits-all" mandates.

Targeted funding programs specifically for their sectors; development of relevant, setting-specific certification modules; incremental, non-punitive approaches to technology adoption; and expanding safe harbors to allow hospitals to subsidize their IT.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: High integration costs and complexity hindering their ability to serve smaller clients; fragmented data exchange slowing innovation; burdensome certification processes for smaller vendors who could offer competitive solutions.
specificPoints: They often frame the problem as solvable through their specific technology platform (e.g., AI co-pilots, interoperability hubs) or a shared infrastructure model they can help build. They argue they can help providers do more with less if the purchasing barriers are removed.
Widespread calls for CMS to fund provider adoption of digital tools; funding for shared infrastructure; simplified, API-first vendor certification; and pilot programs to test their solutions.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of sustainable funding; being overlooked in favor of purely national or commercial network models; the challenge of connecting the "last mile" of providers.
specificPoints: They position themselves as trusted, neutral, "public utility" infrastructure that is essential for connecting smaller, rural, and safety-net providers who cannot afford direct participation in national networks or expensive vendor solutions.

Sustained federal and state funding for HIEs; official recognition and funding of their role as on-ramps to TEFCA; leveraging their existing community governance and trust to serve as shared service enablers.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The financial unsustainability of member practices, the administrative burden of complex regulations, and the potential for one-size-fits-all rules to harm their most vulnerable members.
specificPoints: They provide a powerful, unified voice that aggregates the concerns of thousands of providers. They are adept at proposing specific, detailed policy levers like tiered requirements, modular certification, funding mechanisms, and changes to VBC models.

A comprehensive suite of financial aid (grants, incentives), robust technical assistance (reviving RECs), and significant regulatory flexibility (extended timelines, modular certification, exceptions for small practices).

noteworthyInsights.insight: Compelling Personal Example: A pregnant patient in rural Minnesota details how the closure of her local maternity ward led to her being turned away from her planned delivery hospital, forcing four diversions during active labor. This story makes the abstract threat of practice closure a visceral, life-threatening reality.

The "Orphaned Patient" Phenomenon: The patient advocacy group Patients For Patient Safety US describes "'orphaned' patients" in very rural states with no primary care provider. When they visit an ER, there is no one to send notes to, creating a dangerous cycle of fragmented, memory-based care.

Data-Backed Cost Disparity: Cognitive Medical Systems provides unusually specific financial data, reporting that rural providers face IT costs "5-7 times higher relative to revenue compared to urban peers" and must pay "30-50% salary premiums" for scarce IT specialists.

Data-Backed Value Proposition: The National Association of Community Health Centers (NACHC) provides data that health center care is highly cost-effective, with Medicare patient costs "30% lower than outpatient clinics," framing investment in their infrastructure not as a handout, but as a high-value proposition.

Unintended Consequence of Policy on Vendor Market: An EHR company founder argues that onerous federal certification costs are "helping industry giants to monopolize, while crushing small businesses that might compete with them," raising costs for small practices.

Fear of Vendor Retaliation: A Square Group offers a rare glimpse into provider-vendor power dynamics by recommending a "secure feedback channel for small practices, so they can share patterns of concern without fear of vendor retaliation."

Vicious Cycle of Underfunding: The National Organization of Social Security Claimants' Representatives (NOSSCR) points out that poor data interoperability delays disability determinations. This forces patients to rely on uncompensated care, increasing the financial strain on the very safety-net hospitals that serve them.

HIEs as Potential Monopolies: Beyond Lucid Technologies, representing EMS providers, warns that if state-sanctioned HIEs have a history of excluding certain sectors like Mobile Medical, they could "crystallize" into monopolies that permanently lock out critical pre-hospital data.

Innovative Solution for Staffing: Nexus Bedside proposes using hybrid-virtual nursing to address acute nursing shortages in Critical Access Hospitals, arguing it can provide 24/7 specialist coverage, improve patient retention, and reduce reliance on expensive contract labor.

Powerful Framing: Seen Health, a small PACE program, states that current IT infrastructure "systematically disadvantages us" and pleads for CMS to "Help us focus on care, not compliance", capturing the essence of the provider burden felt throughout the comments.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The "Left-Behind" Coalition: A clear chasm exists between traditional hospitals/physician practices and a coalition of historically marginalized provider types: Post-Acute/Long-Term Care, Behavioral Health, Dental, Community Pharmacy, and Home Health. These groups share a unified narrative of being excluded from HITECH incentives and burdened by hospital-centric regulations that ignore their unique workflows.

Unintended Consequences of Policy: Multiple commenters identify a pattern where the well-intentioned push for certified health IT has led to negative outcomes. These include market consolidation as small practices are forced to sell, vendor monopolization that raises costs, exclusion from value-based care for those who cannot afford the prerequisite technology, and the risk of bankrupting fragile hospitals with punitive measures.

The "Haves vs. Have-Nots" Schism: Comments repeatedly paint a picture of a fractured ecosystem where large, urban, well-resourced systems can meet new requirements, while small, rural, independent, and safety-net providers are being pushed to the brink. This creates a two-tiered system for both providers and the patients they serve.

The "Public Utility" and Intermediary Framework: A recurring theme is the framing of essential data exchange infrastructure as a "public utility." This model posits that foundational interoperability requires sustained public investment. Regional HIEs, Health Center Controlled Networks (HCCNs), and other nonprofit intermediaries are consistently positioned as the crucial "on-ramps" and "last mile" connectors for underserved providers.

keyQuotations.quote: A ‘one-size-fits-all’ interoperability advancement strategy would be doomed to failure.
ACOs frequently have to work directly with EHR vendors to build data extracts, which can cost anywhere from $5,000 to $45,000... a significant hurdle for small and independent practices.
Behavioral health providers... were previously left out of incentive programs...
CMS might also consider a secure feedback channel for small practices, so they can share patterns of concern without fear of vendor retaliation.
Every resident has their own team, hospital, primary care, family, specialists, and Medicare plan, and none of them are on the same system.”
Forcing immediate migration to certified EHR systems would impose unsustainable costs, potentially leading to practice closures in underserved areas.

For rural providers with limited staff and high patient-to-provider ratios, AI co-pilot platforms... can synthesize data... to surface missed diagnoses, recommend care plans, and flag safety concerns.
Forgive medical student debt so that doctors can serve in these hospitals.
Help us focus on care, not compliance.
HHS addressed the need for technical assistance by deploying Regional Extension Centers (RECs) previously and should consider doing so again.
If you want to know if you are hindering innovation... yes, you are. Additionally, you are helping industry giants to monopolize, while crushing small businesses that might compete with them.
In essence, you’re asking a community behavioral health center to subsidize ventilator settings for ICU patients. That's not interoperability, that’s inequity.
Many small practices are forced by circumstances to continue reporting quality data via manual methods due to a lack of IT support and funding...
Overly stringent technology requirements could discourage less-resourced providers... from VBC participation due to financial inability to purchase HIT.
Pharmacy deserts are growing, and access is shrinking; pharmacists, the most frequently accessed health professionals, are cut out of the care team. This is illogical.
Rural healthcare will not benefit from digital transformation through passive diffusion. It will require proactive, sustained intervention.
Rural hospitals are particularly vulnerable due to limited IT staffing, outdated infrastructure, and fragmented security.
Small providers are the backbone of innovative care delivery to underserved communities... Yet current health IT infrastructure systematically disadvantages us.
[The gap between an urban EHR and a rural clinic's system] embodies a systemic failure to prioritize seamless, patient-centered data flow.
The disincentive structure in this rule is excessive and puts hospitals’ stability at risk, especially for small and rural hospitals.
The ‘last mile’ cost of participating in a health information exchange can often be a barrier for... skilled nursing facilities and smaller primary care offices.
The future of the health technology ecosystem must be one where no provider is excluded because of their size, location, or inability to pay for a commercial solution.

The lack of infrastructure in Minnesota puts me and my baby at risk, and the lack of beds and rural maternal child care for pregnant moms puts 10% of all births in Minnesota in jeopardy of a bad outcome.

We a rural practice with one provider. We cannot afford the digital health products nor the IT personnel needed for its maintenance... Implementation of the ONC Health IT Certification Program is not reasonable...There should be waivers available for practice situations like ours.
We cannot achieve the goals of a health technology ecosystem unless all providers have access to up-to-date systems and technical support.
Without targeted support, these organizations risk being left behind in the digital transformation, which could amplify disparities and reduce consumer choice in the long term.

9.3.themeDescription: Integrating Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) Data

sections.executiveSummary: There is universal consensus among stakeholders that integrating Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) data is essential for achieving health equity and succeeding in value-based care. The central tension lies in how to implement this vision: a strong majority calls for mandating standardized data through expanded APIs and certification criteria, while providers, especially smaller practices and community organizations, warn of significant unfunded burdens. The dominant recommendations urge CMS to expand USCDI and other APIs to include structured SDOH elements, adopt the Gravity Project standards, and create financial and technical pathways to fully integrate Community-Based Organizations (CBOs) as equal partners in the healthcare ecosystem.

consensusPoints.text: SDOH Integration is Crucial for Health Equity and Value-Based Care. Nearly all commenters assert that collecting and using SDOH data is no longer optional but a fundamental prerequisite for understanding the whole person, improving health outcomes, achieving health equity, and enabling the success of value-based care (VBC) models. This view is shared across all stakeholder types, from technology vendors and large health systems to patient advocates and community health centers.

Data Standards and APIs Must Be Expanded to Include SDOH. A strong majority of commenters, particularly technology vendors, health IT professionals, and provider associations, explicitly call for enhancing technical infrastructure to support SDOH. The most frequent recommendations are to expand the U.S. Core Data for Interoperability (USCDI) and CMS APIs like Blue Button 2.0 to include structured SDOH data elements.

Community-Based Organizations (CBOs) are Essential Partners. A broad consensus exists that CBOs, social service agencies, and other non-clinical entities are critical for addressing identified social needs. Commenters from all sectors advocate for creating 'closed-loop referral' systems that allow for bidirectional data sharing between clinical providers and CBOs, ensuring that referrals are received and acted upon.

areasOfDebate.topic: How to Collect SDOH Data
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether to prioritize systematic, standardized screening within clinical workflows or to focus on integrating diverse external data sources, including unstructured information.

positions.label: Systematic Screening
positions.stance: Mandate and standardize screening tools within clinical workflows.
positions.supportLevel: High among health systems, FQHCs, and VBC-focused organizations.
keyArguments: Ensures consistent data collection at the point of care
tools like PRAPARE are already in use and can be scaled
enables direct integration into care planning.

positions.label: Data Integration
positions.stance: Prioritize integrating external and unstructured data sources.
positions.supportLevel: Strong among technology vendors, analytics firms, and HIEs.
keyArguments: Leverages existing data without adding to provider burden
sources like public datasets (census, HUD), consumer data, and unstructured notes contain rich context
AI/NLP can make this data computable.

areasOfDebate.topic: How to Drive Adoption
areasOfDebate.description: The debate focuses on the primary driver for adoption: whether to use mandates and compliance requirements or to lead with financial incentives and support, particularly for under-resourced organizations.

positions.label: Mandates & Compliance
positions.stance: Require SDOH capabilities for certification and VBC participation.
positions.supportLevel: A majority of commenters, especially tech vendors and large provider systems.
keyArguments: Mandates create a level playing field and clear targets for developers
linking to VBC models provides a strong business case for adoption.

positions.label: Incentives & Support
positions.stance: Provide funding and technical assistance to encourage adoption.
positions.supportLevel: Nearly all community-based providers, FQHCs, and small practice advocates.
keyArguments: Mandates without funding will penalize under-resourced organizations serving the most vulnerable populations
CBOs and small practices cannot afford IT upgrades or workflow changes without support.

areasOfDebate.topic: What to Standardize in USCDI
areasOfDebate.description: The debate revolves around the scope of standardization for SDOH data in USCDI, contrasting a comprehensive, granular approach with a more prioritized, focused approach that may leverage patient-sourced data.

positions.label: Comprehensive Data
positions.stance: Include a wide range of granular SDOH elements.
positions.supportLevel: Most technology vendors and data analytics firms.
keyArguments: More data enables more sophisticated risk models and personalized interventions
a broad standard is needed to cover diverse needs.

positions.label: Prioritized & Patient-Sourced Data
positions.stance: Focus on a smaller set of high-impact elements and/or what patients can provide themselves.
positions.supportLevel: A smaller but notable group, including some public health entities and a tech vendor.
keyArguments: A more focused set of standards is easier to implement and reduces provider burden
patients can and should provide some of this data directly, lessening system requirements.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of clear, enforced data standards (especially APIs); fragmented data markets; need for a clear business case (reimbursement, VBC integration) to drive investment.
specificPoints: These commenters frame the issue as a solvable technical challenge. They position their existing platforms (analytics engines, AI tools, interoperability hubs) as ready-made solutions and advocate for policies that would accelerate their adoption. They are the strongest proponents of expanding and mandating FHIR APIs and USCDI standards.
Mandate FHIR-based API access to SDOH data; expand TEFCA to include SDOH use cases; align certification criteria with SDOH capabilities; fund pilots to demonstrate ROI.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Administrative burden of data collection; cost of technology upgrades and workflow changes; lack of integration with existing EHRs; need for risk adjustment in VBC that accounts for social complexity.
specificPoints: This group is on the front lines of implementation. They emphasize the practical realities of care delivery. While universally supporting the goal of SDOH integration, they are wary of unfunded mandates and tools that add complexity to their already strained workflows.

Provide financial support for technology adoption; ensure VBC models risk-adjust for SDOH to avoid penalizing those who care for complex populations; expand the Gravity Project to standardize referrals and outcomes; prioritize tools that are seamlessly integrated into EHRs.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Health equity; ensuring technology serves vulnerable populations; funding and technical capacity for CBOs; addressing root causes of poor health (e.g., food insecurity, financial strain).
specificPoints: This group champions the voices of patients and the community sector. They provide powerful, evidence-based arguments for why addressing SDOH is non-negotiable for achieving health justice. They are the strongest advocates for CBOs and provide detailed models for integration.

Fund and provide technical assistance to CBOs; expand USCDI to include social needs; build on successful models like the Gravity Project and state-level Community Information Exchanges (CIEs); normalize needs assessment as a standard part of care.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The real-world stress of navigating fragmented systems; unmet needs for housing, food, and transportation; the need for practical, easy-to-use tools.
specificPoints: These comments ground the abstract policy discussion in human experience. They describe the direct health impacts of mold in a home, the under-recognition of malnutrition in the elderly, and the trauma of retelling stories.

Health tech should flag housing instability and connect families to legal aid; empower seniors to self-screen for malnutrition; allow patients to input their own 'life context' like caregiving obligations into their records.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Scaling successful state-level pilots; establishing cross-agency data sharing agreements; creating sustainable infrastructure (like Health Data Utilities).
specificPoints: These entities showcase what is already working on the ground and advocate for federal policy that supports and standardizes these efforts. They highlight the legal and regulatory fragmentation (e.g., between SNAP, HUD, and healthcare data) as a key barrier.
Fund state-based demonstration pilots; create a unified federal framework for social data sharing; leverage HIEs/HDUs as core infrastructure; expand USCDI+ to include SDOH.

noteworthyInsights.insight: EMI Advisors powerfully articulated that a key harm of siloed systems is not just inefficiency but the emotional toll on vulnerable individuals. Their observation that "patients often must repeatedly share the same story to access multiple benefits, increasing stigma and trauma" reframes interoperability as a matter of human dignity.

Beyond Lucid Technologies offers a unique perspective, quoting an emergency physician who states that EMS crews have critical insight into a patient's home environment (e.g., hoarding, safety risks, social support) that is "the big missing piece that we need in the emergency department" to make safe discharge decisions.

The National Patient Advocate Foundation makes a compelling case for systematically assessing financial hardship as a primary SDOH, citing data that 73% of Americans report financial stress and that it directly contributes to poorer health outcomes. This moves the conversation beyond traditional SDOH categories like food and housing.

NielsenIQ and Gainwell Technologies propose an innovative approach: using consumer purchase and credit bureau data to gain insight into lifestyle behaviors and social risk factors, enabling a "modern form of population health analytics" to understand "upstream health effects."

NielsenIQ and Gainwell Technologies propose an innovative approach: using consumer purchase and credit bureau data to gain insight into lifestyle behaviors and social risk factors, enabling a "modern form of population health analytics" to understand "upstream health effects."

Kinetik, a transportation provider, points out that transportation patterns can reveal much more than just missed appointments; they can be a proxy for "health trends, medication adherence issues, or social determinants of health impacting outcomes." This highlights how data from non-clinical partners can provide crucial context.

emergingPatterns.pattern: States as Laboratories of Innovation: A clear pattern emerges where states are pioneering the integration of health and social care. Commenters from Michigan, California, Colorado, Massachusetts, New York, and Maryland describe advanced, state-level initiatives like Community Information Exchanges (CIEs), integrated 211 systems, and data-sharing frameworks that serve as proven models for national policy.

A Two-Tiered System of Capability: There is a significant gap between large health systems/tech vendors and smaller community-based organizations. Large entities are focused on advanced analytics, AI, and integrating massive datasets. In contrast, FQHCs, CBOs, and small practices are concerned with the fundamental cost and burden of basic data collection and IT upgrades, raising concerns about an emerging "digital divide" in SDOH capabilities.

The Gravity Project as a De Facto Standard: The Gravity Project is mentioned by at least 15 different organizations as the consensus-based foundation for SDOH data standards. There is a strong pattern of support for CMS to formally adopt and align with its work to avoid creating duplicative or conflicting standards.

Shift from Referrals to True Integration: An emerging theme is the evolution from a simple "referral" mindset to a more sophisticated "integration" model. This involves bidirectional data exchange, shared care plans, outcome tracking, and valuing CBOs as equal, data-enabled partners in the care ecosystem, as articulated by Jonathan Zimmerman.

keyQuotations.quote: [A] fundamental driver for these aims is addressing non-medical needs such as food, housing, and transportation as key components of managing chronic conditions and delivering whole-person care.

The most valuable data is a longitudinal record that includes both clinical and social care histories. A record showing a patient’s high A1c is useful; a record showing a high A1c and recent referrals to a food pantry provides context for effective interventions.

This is not merely a technical frustration but a real barrier to our ability to implement innovative tools for care coordination, behavioral health integration, social needs screening, and population health management.
Malnutrition isn’t just starving kids in Africa or India. Malnutrition is rampant in older Americans, especially those living alone.
In CMS models such as MSSP, ACO REACH, D-SNPs, and Medicaid managed care, social risk must be a core element of design, payment, and measurement — not a siloed adjunct.
Fragmented social health information regulations challenge Colorado and the nation.
For most patients and families, financial distress is a common consequence of illness. This is true even for individuals with health insurance.
[If data is siloed,] communities will 'sacrifice details about polychronic patients needing managed care (e.g., community paramedicine)' and information on areas 'acutely impacted by substance use.'

Without reliable transportation access, members frequently miss preventive care visits, leading to delayed diagnoses, worsening chronic conditions, and overall driving up healthcare costs through potentially avoidable emergency interventions and hospitalization.
CMS should explore platforms for bidirectional data exchange to enable provider visibility into patient supports and direct referrals.
The most significant opportunity from a health access perspective is the fragmented, unstructured, uncoded, and undercapitalized segment of IT systems serving the social services industry.

Access and update social needs and life context. Whether it’s transportation insecurity, caregiving obligations, or language barriers — patients should be able to input what matters to us and have that information reflected in how care is delivered.

If we want to manage chronic disease we need to connect patients to programs and services outside the walls of healthcare... To many of us, referrals to these programs and healthy food should be as easy and important as a referral to a healthcare specialist like a dermatologist.
Our experience supporting Social Determinants Accelerator Act projects has shown that misaligned reporting requirements and siloed data systems create unnecessary barriers.
[out-of-hospital data obtained from Fire & EMS] is the big missing piece that we need in the emergency department.”
SNAP enrollment reduces healthcare costs by $1,400-$4,100 per person annually, but only 42% of eligible older adults access it... These are infrastructure failures costing lives and billions.

9.4.themeDescription: Ensuring Equitable and Inclusive Technology Design

sections.executiveSummary: There is an overwhelming and cross-sector consensus that technology must be designed with equity as a core, non-negotiable principle, not an afterthought. Public input coalesces around the need for tools that are accessible (multilingual, plain language, WCAG compliant), meaningfully co-created with diverse end-users, and evaluated with equity-specific metrics. The primary and profound concern is that a rushed or poorly considered shift to digital health will exacerbate existing disparities, creating a two-tiered system of care. Key tensions exist between those who advocate for mandatory technical standards and certification to ensure a baseline of equity, and those who emphasize that technology alone is insufficient without robust, human-centered support systems—like digital navigators and caregiver integration—to bridge critical gaps in trust and literacy. Commenters also highlight the dual potential of Artificial Intelligence, viewing it as both a powerful enabler for scaling equitable solutions and a significant risk for amplifying algorithmic bias. The dominant recommendations urge CMS to move beyond mere technical availability and actively foster an ecosystem where technology is designed to be inclusive, usable, and trustworthy for the most vulnerable populations.

consensusPoints.text: Human-Centered and Co-Created Design is Non-Negotiable - There is overwhelming consensus that technology must be developed with—not just for—end-users. This includes prioritizing input from patients, caregivers, older adults, individuals with disabilities, and those from diverse and underserved communities in the design, testing, and evaluation of all digital health tools. Many stressed this is the only way to ensure products are usable, trusted, and effective. Representative Examples: AHRQ recommends that "patients, families, and caregivers should be meaningfully integrated into the design, implementation, use, and evaluation" of tools. The Light Collective urges CMS to "mandate patient-led usability reviews," while Aegis Ventures states, "The best way to build tools people will use is to involve them from the start."

exceptions.text: While the principle is universally supported, tech vendors often frame this as "user-centered design," while advocacy groups and patients call for more authentic, power-sharing "co-creation." Several commenters, particularly advocacy groups (The Light Collective; National Health Council), stressed that patient participation must be compensated.

consensusPoints.text: Multilingual and Plain Language Support are Foundational Requirements - A strong majority of commenters identified language access as a critical and non-negotiable feature for equitable technology. This includes both providing interfaces and content in multiple languages and translating complex medical information into plain language, often specified at a 5th, 6th, or 8th-grade reading level. Representative Examples: The Center for Telehealth and eHealth Law (CTeL) provides extensive data on negative health outcomes for over 25 million people with Limited English Proficiency (LEP). The Carter Center provides a detailed recommendation to "Use plain language at a 6th-grade reading level or lower." Hippocratic AI provided a powerful example where its multilingual AI "achieved FIT-test opt-in rates 2.6× higher for Spanish speakers than for English speakers." Researcher Ateev Mehrotra cites research showing that AI-powered simplification provides the "largest" benefits for comprehension "among those with lower education and older adults."

Accessibility for People with Disabilities Must Be Prioritized and Enforced - A vast number of commenters from all sectors demand that digital tools adhere to established accessibility standards, such as the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG 2.1/2.2 AA) and Section 508, to serve users with physical, cognitive, sensory, and motor impairments. Representative Examples: NACHC calls adherence to WCAG 2.2 "crucial." The National Alliance for Caregiving demands digital tools "incorporate universal design principles including large fonts, high contrast displays, and voice navigation." A Square Group LLC, a firm specializing in 508 compliance, provides a detailed list of necessary features, including touch-friendly design, keyboard-only navigation, and screen reader compatibility.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A vast number of commenters from all sectors

areasOfDebate.topic: How Best to Achieve Equitable Adoption: Technical Mandates vs. Human-Centered Support

positions.label: Mandatory Certification & Standards
positions.stance: Enforce equity through rigorous, federally mandated standards and certification for accessibility, usability, and equity for any digital tool used in federal programs.
positions.supportLevel: A strong contingent of advocacy groups, provider associations, and many technology vendors.
keyArguments: Ensures a baseline level of quality and equity; protects vulnerable users from poorly designed apps; creates a level playing field and a clear "floor" for all products; provides scalable and verifiable metrics for compliance.

positions.label: Human-Centered Support & Incentives
positions.stance: Prioritize funding, reimbursement, and integration of human support systems (digital navigators, CHWs, caregivers) and use financial incentives to encourage, rather than mandate, the development of equitable tools.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from advocacy groups, caregiver organizations, community-based providers, and some tech vendors focused on innovation.
keyArguments: Technology is insufficient without trust and guidance; avoids stifling innovation with rigid regulations; human support is essential for engagement in underserved communities; many beneficiaries rely on family caregivers who need simple, delegated access.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Role of Artificial Intelligence (AI): Equity Enabler vs. Bias Amplifier

positions.label: AI as Equity Enabler
positions.stance: Position AI as a powerful tool to advance health equity by providing real-time translation, simplifying complex medical language, and personalizing outreach to diverse populations.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily tech vendors specializing in AI, as well as some academic researchers.
keyArguments: AI can scale solutions like multilingual support and plain language summaries in ways that are not humanly possible; can tailor engagement to cultural contexts to improve outcomes.

positions.label: AI as Bias Amplifier
positions.stance: View AI with extreme caution, highlighting the significant risk that biased training data and opaque algorithms will perpetuate and worsen existing health disparities.
positions.supportLevel: Predominantly advocacy groups, provider associations, and some government-focused vendors.
keyArguments: Biased AI can lead to discriminatory care recommendations and resource allocation; lack of transparency makes it impossible to audit for fairness; "garbage in, garbage out" means existing systemic biases will be encoded and scaled.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of clear standards to build against; demonstrating their products already meet equity goals; seeking clear certification pathways and a level playing field; market barriers to scaling their solutions.
specificPoints: These commenters frequently use their submissions to showcase their specific products as solutions, providing concrete examples of equitable design in action (e.g., MEDA's customizable avatars, Hippocratic AI's multilingual outreach, or platforms with low-bandwidth modes).
Establish clear, voluntary certification programs; adopt their specific platforms; create market incentives and federal "sandboxes" for testing high-quality, equitable tools.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Technology exacerbating existing disparities; exclusion of vulnerable populations; ensuring the voices of patients and caregivers are central to development; preventing a new "digital divide" and reinforcing stigma.
specificPoints: This group provides the most detailed, prescriptive recommendations, grounded in the lived experiences of the communities they represent. They are the strongest proponents of co-creation, mandatory evaluation of equity impacts, and champion the principle of "nothing about us without us."
Mandate and fund patient-led usability reviews; require plain language and WCAG compliance; co-design quality metrics with patient networks; ensure caregiver needs are central to design.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The usability of tools within clinical workflows for their specific patient populations; ensuring new tech doesn't exacerbate health disparities; the cost and burden of integrating new systems; equitable access for rural providers.
specificPoints: They connect high-level policy to on-the-ground reality. NACHC provides hard data on their patients' language and bandwidth needs. Ensora Health highlights how tools for general medicine can be inappropriate for behavioral health.

Hold digital health companies accountable for product accessibility; create certification pathways for tools optimized for safety-net providers; provide financial incentives for meeting digital equity benchmarks.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Overwhelming complexity of current tools; frustration, exclusion, and fear of discrimination or harm. One individual describes the digital push as potentially "genocidal" against the elderly.
specificPoints: Their comments provide raw, powerful, and often emotional testimony about the real-world impact of poorly designed systems.

Make existing applications "user friendly" with "simple step-by-step instructions"; design for less complexity, not more features; allow caregivers to easily and securely help patients navigate tools.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring solutions are evidence-based; promoting systematic and rigorous evaluation; leveraging existing research and frameworks.
specificPoints: These groups provide a more theoretical and systems-level view, often pointing to existing resources and methodologies.

AHRQ encourages CMS to use its existing lifecycle frameworks for patient-centered design. The Data Equity Coalition urges the adoption of revised OMB SPD 15 data standards as foundational to measuring equity.

noteworthyInsights.insight: "Discrimination at Scale": Patient advocate Kelee Petzelt offers a powerful framing: "Equity is a design requirement; a digital tool that doesn’t work for everyone is 'discrimination at scale'." This reframes poor design from a usability issue to an ethical and civil rights failure.

Beyond the "Mythical Average User": Patient advocate Grace Cordovano makes the sophisticated point that equity doesn't just mean simplifying for the least experienced user. It means designing flexible tools that can "meet people where they are and grow with them," serving both the digitally novice and the expert patient.

"Emotional Accessibility": A Square Group introduces the concept of "emotional accessibility," noting that patients are often overwhelmed and tools "should be intuitive to use, and reduce anxiety, not add to it." This expands the definition of accessibility beyond technical compliance.

Designing for the "Edge" to Benefit the "Core": A parent advocate for children with rare diseases makes the insightful point that if a tool can be made to work for the most complex cases (like rare disease communities), "it will likely work for broader populations, too."

The Power of Language, Quantified: Hippocratic AI provides a rare, powerful data point: their AI agent, by speaking a patient's preferred language (Spanish), increased colorectal cancer screening test opt-ins by 2.6 times compared to English-speaking outreach. This is a concrete demonstration of how inclusive design directly improves health outcomes.

Age-Friendly Design vs. Accessibility: AARP makes a crucial distinction, noting that while accessibility features are helpful, "the accessibility lens does not fully address the needs of 50-plus users and can sometimes reinforce stereotypes about aging." They advocate for "age-friendly design" that focuses on an optimal experience for all ages.

Reframing the Fraud vs. Access Dilemma: HTD Health challenges the "old thinking" that fraud prevention must come at the cost of access for vulnerable populations. They argue that modern technology can simultaneously increase security and reduce friction.

A Compelling Personal Example of Stigma: Mental Health America shares a story of a patient with schizophrenia who went to the ER for stomach pain and was immediately treated as a threat once his diagnosis was known, highlighting the severe real-world consequences of data sharing without safeguards.

Specialty-Specific Equity Needs: Ensora Health offers a crucial insight that technology designed for primary care workflows can be inappropriate and even unsafe for behavioral health, where different standards for consent and data sharing apply.

A Concrete Example of Policy Misalignment: Jonathon Feit of Beyond Lucid Technologies provides a compelling case study from Los Angeles County, arguing that creating a new, separate emergency registry for vulnerable populations is "ultimately misguided" and riskier than leveraging the existing statewide interoperability framework.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Human-in-the-Loop Imperative: A clear pattern emerges that technology alone is insufficient. Many commenters advocate for models where digital tools are paired with human support—such as CHWs, navigators, or call center staff—to guide users and bridge literacy and trust gaps.

Beyond Apps: The Need for Low-Friction Modalities: There is a growing recognition that "equitable design" must move beyond complex smartphone apps. A strong theme is the need to leverage simpler, more universal technologies like SMS/texting and voice calls ("meet users where they are") to maximize reach, especially for older and lower-income populations.

Focus on Caregivers as Critical Users: A significant number of commenters highlight that many beneficiaries rely on family caregivers. They argue that designing for the patient alone is insufficient; tools must be built around "trusted family relationships, not just patient portals" and feature simple, secure proxy access.

Vendor Optimism vs. Advocate Caution: Technology companies frequently position their AI and automated platforms as tools that can eliminate human bias and "democratize" healthcare. In contrast, advocacy groups and patient representatives warn that without extreme care, these same tools will amplify bias and widen disparities.

Focus on Cultural Competence, Not Just Translation: Sophisticated comments distinguish between literal translation and true cultural competence. They call for interfaces, content, and workflows that respect diverse health beliefs and community dynamics.

Provider Directories as an Equity Tool: Several commenters see the modernization of provider directories as a key lever for equity. They recommend including data on provider race/ethnicity, languages spoken, and cultural competencies to help patients find providers they trust.

keyQuotations.quote: Equity is a design requirement; a digital tool that doesn’t work for everyone is 'discrimination at scale'.
English-only, Western-centric design is exclusionary.
[W]e risk widening existing disparities in care and outcomes.
A widespread lack of culturally and linguistically accessible formats... reduces the utility of health data for decision-making and care navigation.
Potential biases in AI training data and AI algorithms can perpetuate harmful stereotypes and impact patient and community health.
If HIEs become Health Data Utilities (HDUs) and exclude Mobile Medicine, it could be disastrous, leading to a 'separate and unequal' situation.
you can exterminate us, seniors, just the same as Nazi did...
Let’s stop designing for the mythical “average user” and instead create tools that are flexible enough to meet people where they are and grow with them.
If patients are involved upstream in health technology product design, the products are more likely to produce results patients find valuable.
The best way to build tools people will use is to involve them from the start.
When we design for those who need help most, we create tools that work better for everyone.
If a digital tool works for us, it will likely work for broader populations, too.
Older adults and underserved patients need less complexity, not more features.
Accessibility is not just about screen readers and font sizes, but emotional clarity, cognitive load, and meeting people where they are.
By speaking the patient’s preferred language and using empathetic, culturally-tailored dialogue, the AI achieved FIT-test opt-in rates 2.6× higher for Spanish speakers than for English speakers.
Research showed AI simplification benefits for comprehension, confidence, and reduced anxiety, with these improvements being 'largest among those with lower education and older adults'.
Agentic Voice is designed to meet Medicare members where they are: on the phone, in their preferred language, and with no apps or portals to navigate.

CMS should limit payment for digital health technology unless assurance is provided that people who face systemic barriers to digital health adoption have been involved at every stage of product development and product deployment.
Crucially, these products must support human-mediated technology support (e.g., direct messaging/calls to a CHW or care coordinator), as many beneficiaries require assistance.
Standardizing these [race/ethnicity] elements across federal programs, health IT systems, and value-based care models is foundational to identifying disparities... and advancing health equity.
modern tools must be multilingual, accessible after hours, and built around trusted family relationships, not just patient portals.
